s2.english_meaning: What all is it that you keep saying? You are the knower of hearts. Whether one calls Him Ram or Rahim, the true Name of all is the same; Bulleh Shah stands bewildered before this mystery, returning again to the refrain of wonder.

s2.title: The Lord Dwells in the Heart
s2.why_dense: This couplet distills interior devotion into a line simple enough to memorize yet deep enough to anchor a whole spiritual worldview. A learner gains a compact sentence pattern and a culturally common formulation of inward religiosity.
s2.cultural_signal: widely quoted in speech; Sufi mehfil; Punjabi devotional discourse
s2.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਰੱਬ ਦਿਲਾਂ ਵਿਚ ਰਹਿੰਦਾ, ਦਿਲਾਂ ਵਿਚੋਂ ਲੱਭਿਆ ਜਾਇ
ਜੰਗਲ ਬੇਲੇ ਮਸਜਿਦ ਮੰਦਰ, ਵੱਸਦਾ ਨਾਹੀਂ ਥਾਇਂ
s2.romanization:
Rabb dilaan vich rahinda, dilaan vichon labhia jaae
Jangal bele masjid mandar, vassda naaheen thaain
s2.english_meaning: The Lord abides within hearts; He is found by searching within the heart itself. In wilderness, mosque, or temple He is not fixed to any outer place, for His true dwelling is the inward chamber.

s2.title: The World Is a Garden of Color
s2.why_dense: This siharfi segment uses color and multiplicity to reflect on illusion, attraction, and the fleetingness of worldly forms. A learner gains descriptive vocabulary and another example of how acrostic form can carry philosophical reflection in Punjabi.
s2.cultural_signal: literary recitation; Sufi mehfil; advanced Punjabi learning contexts
s2.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਰੰਗ ਰੰਗੀਲੀ ਦੁਨੀਆ ਵੇਖੀ,
ਮੈਂ ਵੇਖੀ ਜਗ ਸਾਰੀ।
ਜਿਥੇ ਵੇਖਾਂ ਓਥੇ ਰੰਗ ਹੀ ਰੰਗ ਹੈ,
ਰੰਗ ਬਿਨਾ ਨਹੀਂ ਕਾਰੀ।
s2.romanization:
Rang rangeelee duniyaa vekhee,
main vekhee jag saari.
Jithe vekhaan othe rang hee rang hai,
rang binaa nahin kaari.
s2.english_meaning: I beheld the world dyed in many hues; wherever I turned my gaze, all creation shimmered with color. Nothing comes to fruition without this dyeing, for the Divine tint is the hidden force that gives every form its life and meaning.

s2.title: I Am Yours
s2.why_dense: This radically brief declaration shows how Shah Hussain can compress surrender, intimacy, and identity into the smallest possible lyric unit. A learner gains a foundational possessive structure and a pure example of devotional minimalism in Punjabi.
s2.cultural_signal: Sufi mehfil; Punjabi kafi singing; widely quoted in devotional settings
s2.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਮੈਂ ਤੇਰੀ ਤੇ ਤੂੰ ਮੇਰਾ, ਫੁੱਲ ਤੇ ਖੁਸ਼ਬੂ ਵਾਂਗੂੰ,
ਦਿਲ ਵਿਚ ਵੱਸੇਂ ਸੱਜਣਾ, ਜਿਉਂ ਰੂਹ ਤਨ ਦੇ ਸੰਗੂੰ।
ਤੈਥੋਂ ਵਿੱਛੁੜ ਕੇ ਜੀਣਾ, ਅੱਗ ਵਿਚ ਸੜਨਾ ਜਾਪੇ,
ਸ਼ਾਹ ਹੁਸੈਨ ਫਕੀਰ ਨਿਮਾਣਾ, ਤੇਰੇ ਨਾਮ ਦੀ ਰਟ ਲਾਪੇ।
s2.romanization:
main teri te toon meraa, phull te khushboo vaangoon,
dil vich vassen sajjnaa, jioon rooh tan de sangoon.
taithon vichhur ke jeenaa, agg vich sarnaa jaape,
Shaah Husain fakeer nimaanaa, tere naam di rat laape.
s2.english_meaning: I am yours and you are mine, as the flower is joined to its fragrance; beloved, you dwell within the heart as the soul abides with the body. To live parted from you is like burning in fire, and Shah Hussain, the humble faqir, can only keep turning the rosary of your Name.

s2.title: If You Would Be a Lover, Practice Love
s2.why_dense: This poem frames love as disciplined labor rather than sentiment, making it a practical ethics of the Sufi path. A learner gains imperative and conditional structures while meeting Shah Hussain's austere understanding of what love demands.
s2.cultural_signal: Sufi mehfil; Punjabi classical-folk performance; teaching contexts
s2.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਆਸ਼ਕ ਹੋਵੈਂ ਤਾਂ ਇਸ਼ਕ ਕਮਾਵੈਂ,
ਜ਼ਾਤ ਸਿਫਾਤਾਂ ਸਭ ਭੁਲਾਵੈਂ।
ਆਪਣਾ ਆਪ ਗਵਾਵੈਂ ਸਾਰਾ,
ਤਾਂ ਹੀ ਪਿਆ ਦਾ ਭੇਦ ਪਾਵੈਂ।
s2.romanization:
Aashak hovain taan ishq kamaavain,
zaat sifaatan sabh bhulaavain.
Aapnaa aap gavaavain saaraa,
taan hee piyaa da bhed paavain.
s2.english_meaning: If you would be a lover, then earn love in truth; forget all claims of lineage, qualities, and self-regard. Only by losing the whole of oneself does one come upon the Beloved’s secret.

s2.title: What Do I Know of Who I Am?
s2.why_dense: This offers a valuable pre-Bulleh version of the identity-question motif and helps learners hear continuity across Punjabi Sufi generations. A learner gains comparative insight into diction, tone, and the shared language of self-unmaking.
s2.cultural_signal: Sufi mehfil; literary comparison settings; Punjabi heritage study
s2.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਕੀ ਜਾਣਾਂ ਮੈਂ ਕੌਣ
ਨਾ ਮੈਂ ਮੋਮਨ ਵਿਚ ਮਸੀਤਾਂ
ਨਾ ਮੈਂ ਵਿਚ ਕੁਫਰ ਦੀਆਂ ਰੀਤਾਂ
ਨਾ ਮੈਂ ਪਾਕਾਂ ਵਿਚ ਪਲੀਤਾਂ
ਕੀ ਜਾਣਾਂ ਮੈਂ ਕੌਣ
s2.romanization:
Ki jaanaa main kaun
Naa main moman vich maseetaan
Naa main vich kufar diyaan reetaan
Naa main paakaan vich paleetaan
Ki jaanaa main kaun
s2.english_meaning: What do I know of who I am? I am not contained among the faithful in the mosque, nor within the customs of unbelief; neither pure among the pure nor impure among the defiled. The voice stands outside all fixed religious naming, seeking the hidden self beyond division.

s2.title: Neither Believer nor Infidel
s2.first_line_gurmukhi: ਨਾ ਮੈਂ ਮੋਮਨ ਨਾ ਮੈਂ ਕਾਫਿਰ
s2.why_dense: This kafi rejects binary religious identity in language that is stark, memorable, and historically resonant. A learner gains key Perso-Arabic religious vocabulary and a strong comparison point with later Bulleh Shah formulations.
s2.cultural_signal: Sufi mehfil; interfaith citation; literary recitation
s2.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਨਾ ਮੈਂ ਮੋਮਨ ਨਾ ਮੈਂ ਕਾਫਿਰ,
ਨਾ ਮੈਂ ਪਾਕਾਂ ਵਿੱਚ ਪਲੀਤ।
ਨਾ ਮੈਂ ਮੂਸਾ ਨਾ ਫਿਰਔਨ,
ਨਾ ਮੈਂ ਆਦਮ ਨਾ ਮੈਂ ਸ਼ੀਤ।
s2.romanization:
naa main moman naa main kaafir,
naa main paakaan vich paleet.
naa main Moosaa naa Firaun,
naa main Aadam naa main Sheet.
s2.english_meaning: I am neither believer nor infidel; neither among the pure nor the defiled. I am not Moses, nor Pharaoh; not Adam, nor Seth—the voice stands outside all inherited names and oppositions, seeking the station where the soul is known beyond every label.

s2.title: The River Is Deep
s2.why_dense: River imagery in Shah Hussain often stands for danger, passage, and the soul's testing, and this makes that symbolic world immediately accessible. A learner gains nature vocabulary and a key Punjabi metaphor cluster linking water, crossing, and spiritual risk.
s2.cultural_signal: Punjabi kafi singing; Sufi mehfil; folk-classical recitation
s2.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਦਰਿਆ ਡੂੰਘਾ ਵਗਦਾ,
ਵੇ ਮੈਂ ਕੀ ਜਾਣਾਂ ਜਲ ਦੀਆਂ ਗਹਿਰਾਈਆਂ।
ਅੰਦਰ ਤੂੰ, ਬਾਹਰ ਤੂੰ,
ਰੋਮ ਰੋਮ ਵਿਚ ਤੂੰ ਸਮਾਇਆ।
s2.romanization:
dariyaa doonGhaa vagdaa,
ve main kee jaanaa jal diyaan gahiraaiyaan.
andar toon, baahar toon,
rom rom vich toon samaaiaa.
s2.english_meaning: The river runs deep; how could I know the hidden depths of its waters? Within and without, it is You alone, pervading every pore, as the seeker stands before an unfathomable current of the Beloved.

s2.title: Come, Let Us Cross the Water
s2.why_dense: This lyric works simultaneously as a love-call and as an allegory of crossing from separation to union. A learner gains motion verbs, feminine address, and the layered symbolism of journeying so central to Punjabi song.
s2.cultural_signal: Punjabi folk music; Sufi mehfil; women's and folk singing traditions
s2.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਚੱਲੀਏ ਨੀ ਪਾਣੀ ਪਾਰ,
ਓਥੇ ਬੈਠਾ ਯਾਰ।
ਜਿੱਥੇ ਵੱਜਣ ਪ੍ਰੇਮ ਦੀਆਂ ਬਾਂਸਰੀਆਂ,
ਓਥੇ ਨਾਹੀੰ ਦੁੱਖ ਸੰਸਾਰ।
s2.romanization:
Challiye ni paani paar,
Othe baitha yaar.
Jitthe vajjan prem diyaan baansariyaan,
Othe naaheen dukh sansaar.
s2.english_meaning: Come, let us cross the water; the Beloved sits on the farther shore. Where the flutes of love are sounding, the sorrows of the world do not enter, and the crossing becomes the soul’s passage from separation into union.

s2.title: The Boat Is Small, the River Vast
s2.why_dense: This image of a fragile boat on a vast river teaches humility, dependence, and the precariousness of human effort. A learner gains concrete nouns and a memorable metaphor for the soul's vulnerability that recurs across Punjabi mystical traditions.
s2.cultural_signal: Sufi mehfil; Punjabi folk-classical performance; literary teaching
s2.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਨਿੱਕੀ ਜਿਹੀ ਕਿਸ਼ਤੀ ਵੱਡਾ ਦਰਿਆ,
ਕਿਵੇਂ ਲੰਘਾਂ ਪਾਰ.
ਚਾਰ ਚੁਫੇਰੇ ਲਹਿਰਾਂ ਉਠਦੀਆਂ,
ਵਿਚ ਵਿਚ ਪੈਂਦਾ ਭਾਰ.
ਸ਼ਾਹ ਹੁਸੈਨ ਫਕੀਰ ਸਾਈਂ ਦਾ,
ਨਾਮ ਲਏਂ ਤਾਂ ਉਤਰੇ ਪਾਰ.
s2.romanization:
Nikki jehi kishti vadda dariyaa,
kivein langhaan paar.
Chaar chufere lahiraan uthdiyan,
vich vich painda bhaar.
Shah Hussain faqeer Saaeen da,
naam layein taaN utre paar.
s2.english_meaning: The boat is tiny and the river immense: how shall one cross to the farther shore? Waves rise on every side and the burden weighs in the middle of the passage, yet Shah Hussain says that by taking the Beloved Master's Name, one is ferried across.

s2.title: Opening Invocation of Saif-ul-Malook
s2.why_dense: This opening invocation introduces the elevated narrative register of Saif-ul-Malook while grounding the tale in praise, humility, and sacred beginning. A learner gains formal literary Punjabi and sees how epic romance in Punjab begins with devotional framing.
s2.cultural_signal: Saif-ul-Malook recitation; Punjabi literary gatherings; heritage study curricula
s2.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਅੱਲ੍ਹਾ ਨਾਮ ਧਿਆਈਏ ਪਹਿਲਾਂ, ਦੂਜਾ ਨਬੀ ਰਸੂਲ
ਮੁਹੰਮਦ ਨੂਰ ਜਹਾਨ ਦਾ, ਬਖ਼ਸ਼ਿਸ਼ ਦਾ ਸਰਮੂਲ
ਸਾਹਿਬ ਆਪਣੇ ਨਾਮ ਦਾ, ਸਦਾ ਰਹੇ ਮਸ਼ਗੂਲ
ਦਿਲ ਦਰਿਆ ਵਿਚ ਡੁੱਬ ਕੇ, ਲੱਭੀਏ ਰੱਬ ਕਬੂਲ
s2.romanization:
Allha naam dhiaaeeae pehlaan, dooja nabi rasool
Muhammad noor jahaan da, bakhshish da sarmool
Sahib aapne naam da, sada rahe mashghool
Dil dariya vich dubb ke, labhie Rabb qabool
s2.english_meaning: First let the mind dwell upon the Name of Allah, and then upon the Prophet, the Messenger. Muhammad is the light of the world, the fountainhead of grace; remaining absorbed in the Lord’s Name, one must plunge into the river of the heart to seek the God who receives and accepts.

s2.title: The Lake and the Fairy Encounter
s2.why_dense: This famous narrative moment captures wonder, beauty, and visionary encounter in the lush descriptive style that made the poem beloved across Punjab. A learner gains landscape and beauty vocabulary while entering the romance-mysticism bridge that defines the text.
s2.cultural_signal: Saif-ul-Malook recitation; Punjabi folk storytelling; literary gatherings
s2.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਝੀਲ ਕਿਨਾਰੇ ਰੂਪ ਨਿਹਾਰਿਆ, ਦਿਲਬਰ ਦਿਲ ਵਿਚ ਵੱਸਿਆ
ਹੁਸਨ ਜਮਾਲ ਦੇਖ ਕੇ ਉਸ ਦਾ, ਇਸ਼ਕ ਅੰਗੀਠੀ ਧੱਸਿਆ
ਨੈਣਾਂ ਦੇ ਵਿਚ ਨੂਰ ਸੀ ਚਮਕਦਾ, ਚੰਨ ਵੀ ਸ਼ਰਮਾਂਦਾ ਸੀ
ਸੈਫੁਲ ਮਲੂਕ ਬੇਖੁਦ ਹੋਇਆ, ਭੇਦ ਇਸ਼ਕ ਦਾ ਦੱਸਿਆ
s2.romanization:
Jheel kinaare roop nihaariya, dilbar dil vich vassia
Husn jamaal dekh ke us da, ishq angeethi dhassia
Nainaan de vich noor si chamakda, chann vee sharmaanda si
Saiful Malook bekhud hoia, bhed ishq da dassia
s2.english_meaning: At the edge of the lake he beheld a beauty whose image came to dwell within his heart. Seeing that radiance and grace, the fire of love sank deep into him like a brazier’s heat; light flashed in her eyes so that even the moon seemed abashed, and Saif-ul-Malook was undone, initiated into the secret of عشق.

s2.title: Love Leaves the Heart Restless
s2.why_dense: This ghazal-style selection shows Bakhsh's ability to compress longing into polished, aphoristic couplet form distinct from his narrative verse. A learner gains exposure to the ghazal register and to a more Persianized Punjabi lyric texture.
s2.cultural_signal: literary recitation; Punjabi adabi mehfil; heritage poetry circles
s2.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਇਸ਼ਕ ਅੰਦਰ ਚੈਨ ਨ ਰਹਿੰਦਾ, ਕੀ ਜਾਣਣ ਸੁਖ ਲੋਕੀ
ਜਿਨ੍ਹਾਂ ਦਿਲਾਂ ਵਿਚ ਦਰਦ ਨ ਉਠਿਆ, ਉਹ ਕੀ ਜਾਣਣ ਰੋਗੀ
ਮੀਆਂ ਮੁਹੰਮਦ ਬਖ਼ਸ਼ ਇਸ਼ਕ ਦੀ ਨੀਵੀ ਅੱਗ ਬਲੰਦੀ
ਜਿਸ ਦਿਲ ਅੰਦਰ ਲੱਗੇ ਇਹ ਚਿੰਗਾਰੀ, ਸੋ ਜਲ ਜਲ ਹੋਵੇ ਜੋਗੀ
s2.romanization:
Ishq andar chain na rahinda, kee jaanan sukh loki
Jinhaan dilaan vich dard na uthia, oh kee jaanan rogi
Mian Muhammad Bakhsh ishq di neevi agg balandi
Jis dil andar lagge eh chingaari, so jal jal hove jogi
s2.english_meaning: Within love, no peace remains; how could the ease-loving world understand it? Hearts that have never been pierced by pain cannot know the condition of the afflicted. Mian Muhammad Bakhsh says that love is a low-burning yet rising fire; when its spark catches in the heart, the lover is consumed again and again, becoming like a wandering ascetic.

s2.title: The World Is a Temporary Inn
s2.why_dense: This wisdom couplet teaches impermanence through a travel image that is instantly understandable and culturally widespread. A learner gains a proverb-like line and a key ethical theme of Punjabi Sufi reflection: live lightly because nothing here is permanent.
s2.cultural_signal: widely quoted in speech; Punjabi literary circles; Sufi discourse
s2.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਦੁਨੀਆ ਇਕ ਸਰਾਇ ਹੈ, ਇਸ ਵਿਚ ਕਰ ਲੋ ਵਾਸ
ਮੀਆਂ ਮੁਹੰਮਦ ਬਖ਼ਸ਼ਾ, ਆਖ਼ਿਰ ਹੋਣਾ ਪਾਸ
s2.romanization:
duniyaa ik saraai hai, is vich kar lo vaas
Mian Muhammad Bakhsha, aakhir honaa paas
s2.english_meaning: The world is but an inn; dwell in it only as a passing guest. Mian Muhammad Bakhsh reminds the soul that in the end one must move on, for no stay here is lasting.

s2.title: Do Good and Walk On
s2.first_line_gurmukhi: ਨੇਕੀ ਕਰ ਤੇ ਦਰਿਆ ਵਿਚ ਪਾ
s2.why_dense: This compact moral teaching exemplifies the quotable wisdom tradition associated with Bakhsh and broader Punjabi adab. A learner gains an imperative construction and a culturally durable ethical formula about selfless action without expectation.
s2.cultural_signal: widely quoted in speech; school and family moral instruction; Punjabi literary circles
s2.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਨੇਕੀ ਕਰ ਤੇ ਦਰਿਆ ਵਿਚ ਪਾ, ਕੁਝ ਨਾ ਮੰਗੇ ਲੋੜ,
ਜਿਸ ਰੱਬ ਦਿੱਤਾ ਏ ਦਾਤ ਤੈਨੂੰ, ਓਹ ਦੇਵੇਗਾ ਹੋਰ।
s2.romanization:
Neki kar te dariya vich paa, kujh na mange lor,
Jis Rabb dittaa e daat tainoon, oh devegaa hor.
s2.english_meaning: Do good and cast it into the river, asking no return and claiming no need. The Lord who has already placed this gift in your hands will bestow yet more; generosity is sustained by the unseen bounty of God.

s3.title: Opening invocation to the Pir
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਅੱਵਲ ਹਮਦ ਖੁਦਾ ਦਾ ਵਿਰਦ ਕੀਜੈ
s3.author: Waris Shah
s3.why_dense: This opening stands alone because it introduces the kissa tradition through praise of God, the Prophet, and the spiritual guide in a compact literary frame. A learner gains core Perso-Arabic devotional vocabulary, the etiquette of classical openings, and a feel for how Punjabi narrative poetry begins with sacred authority.
s3.cultural_signal: school curricula; widely quoted in literary gatherings; recited in mehfil singing as the canonical opening of Heer
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਅੱਵਲ ਹਮਦ ਖੁਦਾ ਦਾ ਵਿਰਦ ਕੀਜੈ,
ਇਸ਼ਕ ਕੀਤਾ ਸੁ ਜਗ ਦਾ ਮੂਲ ਮੀਆਂ।
ਪਹਿਲੇ ਆਪ ਹੀ ਰੱਬ ਨੇ ਇਸ਼ਕ ਕੀਤਾ,
ਤੇ ਮਾਸ਼ੂਕ ਹੈ ਨਬੀ ਰਸੂਲ ਮੀਆਂ।
ਇਸ਼ਕ ਪੀਰ ਫਕੀਰ ਦਾ ਮਰਤਬਾ ਏ,
ਮੱਥੇ ਇਸ਼ਕ ਨੇ ਪਾਇਆ ਧੂਲ ਮੀਆਂ।
s3.romanization:
Avval hamd Khuda da vird keejai,
ishq keetaa su jag da mool miyaan.
Pahile aap hee Rabb ne ishq keetaa,
te maashooq hai Nabi Rasool miyaan.
Ishq peer fakeer da martabaa e,
mathe ishq ne paayaa dhool miyaan.
s3.english_meaning: First, recite the praise of God: love is the root of the world. In the beginning, the Lord Himself loved, and the Beloved of that love is the Prophet, the Messenger. Love is the rank of pirs and faqirs; it has cast even dust upon the forehead, marking all creation with its humility and grace.

s3.title: Poet enters the tale
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਵਾਰਸ ਸ਼ਾਹ ਮੀਆਂ ਇਸ਼ਕ ਫਕੀਰੀ ਦਾ
s3.author: Waris Shah
s3.why_dense: This passage is standalone-dense because Waris folds his own poetic identity into the doctrine of ishq as faqiri, making the poem self-aware from the start. A learner gains the key Punjabi-Sufi linkage between love, poverty, renunciation, and authorship that echoes throughout later Punjabi verse.
s3.cultural_signal: widely quoted in diaspora literary circles; referenced in modern Punjabi poetry discussions; common in Waris Shah recitation
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਵਾਰਸ ਸ਼ਾਹ ਮੀਆਂ ਇਸ਼ਕ ਫਕੀਰੀ ਦਾ,
ਕੀਤਾ ਸੁਫ਼ੀਆਂ ਅਸਲ ਬਿਆਨ ਮੀਆਂ।
ਇਸ਼ਕ ਪੀਰ ਫਕੀਰ ਦਾ ਮਰਤਬਾ ਏ,
ਮਖ਼ਸੂਸ ਹੈ ਇਸ ਦਾ ਜਹਾਨ ਮੀਆਂ।
ਜਿੱਥੇ ਇਸ਼ਕ ਦਾ ਭੇਤ ਨ ਖੁਲ੍ਹ ਸਕੇ,
ਓਥੇ ਅਕਲ ਰਹੀ ਹੈ ਹੈਰਾਨ ਮੀਆਂ।
s3.romanization:
Waris Shah mian ishq fakeeri da,
keeta sufiaan asal bayaan mian.
Ishq peer fakeer da martaba ae,
makhsoos hai is da jahaan mian.
Jitthe ishq da bhet na khulh sake,
othe akal rahee hai hairaan mian.
s3.english_meaning: Waris Shah says he has set forth the true account of love as the path of faqirs and Sufis. Love belongs to the rank of saints and mendicants; it is a world apart, marked off from ordinary knowing. Where the secret of love does not open, reason itself is left standing bewildered.

s3.title: Heer’s beauty described
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਡੋਲੀ ਹੁਸਨ ਦੀ ਆ ਪਹੁੰਚੀ ਝੰਗ ਸਿਆਲਾਂ
s3.author: Waris Shah
s3.why_dense: This band stands alone because it is one of the most anthologized husn descriptions, built from vivid similes that need little plot context. A learner gains descriptive idiom, body-and-ornament vocabulary, and exposure to the hyperbolic aesthetics of classical Punjabi praise poetry.
s3.cultural_signal: mehfil singing; school anthologies; widely quoted in discussions of Punjabi beauty imagery
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਡੋਲੀ ਹੁਸਨ ਦੀ ਆ ਪਹੁੰਚੀ ਝੰਗ ਸਿਆਲਾਂ
ਨਾਲੇ ਆਣ ਬਹਾਲਿਆ ਨੂਰ ਦਾ ਹੈ।
ਵਾਰਿਸ ਸ਼ਾਹ ਮੀਆਂ ਖ਼ੁਸ਼ੀਆਂ ਕਰਨ ਲੋਕੀਂ
ਮੁੱਲ ਹੋਇਆ ਅਨਮੋਲ ਤੇ ਤੋਲ ਦਾ ਹੈ।
ਹੀਰ ਆਖਦੇ ਸਿਆਲਾਂ ਦੀ ਧੀ ਸੁਣੀਂਦੀ
ਸਾਨੂਂ ਸਾਹਿਬ ਜਮਾਲ ਨੇ ਕੋਲ ਦਾ ਹੈ।
s3.romanization:
Doli husn di aa pahunchi Jhang Siyaalaan
Naale aan bahaalia noor da hai.
Waris Shah miyaan khushiaan karan lokin
Mull hoia anmol te tol da hai.
Heer aakhde Siyaalaan di dhee suneendi
Saanoon sahib-jamaal ne kol da hai.
s3.english_meaning: The palanquin of beauty has arrived in Jhang of the Sials, and with it light itself has come to dwell there. Waris Shah says the people rejoice, for something beyond all price and measure has appeared: they say this is Heer, the daughter of the Sials, and sheer divine beauty seems to have drawn near.

s3.title: Heer as queenly presence
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਨੈਨਾਂ ਤੀਰ ਤੇ ਭੌਂਹ ਕਮਾਨ ਹੋਈ
s3.author: Waris Shah
s3.why_dense: This passage is dense because it compresses the classical beloved into a chain of martial and regal metaphors that are intelligible even without story background. A learner gains stock imagery of South Asian love poetry and sees how Persianized metaphor enters Punjabi idiom naturally.
s3.cultural_signal: quoted in mehfils and stage recitations; echoed in modern song lyrics about eyes and brows
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਨੈਨਾਂ ਤੀਰ ਤੇ ਭੌਂਹ ਕਮਾਨ ਹੋਈ,
ਮੁਖੜੇ ਉੱਤੇ ਜ਼ੁਲਫ਼ਾਂ ਪਰੇਸ਼ਾਨ ਹੋਈ।
ਲੱਜਾ ਨੂਰ ਤੇ ਨਖਰਾ ਜਿਉਂ ਬਿਜਲੀ,
ਸੁਰਖ਼ੀ ਹੋਠਾਂ ਤੇ ਮਿਸਰੀ ਦੀ ਖਾਨ ਹੋਈ।
ਵਾਰਿਸ ਸ਼ਾਹ ਇਹ ਹੀਰ ਦੀ ਸ਼ਾਨ ਸਾਰੀ,
ਜਿਉਂ ਤਖ਼ਤ ਉੱਤੇ ਬੈਠੀ ਸੁਲਤਾਨ ਹੋਈ।
s3.romanization:
Nainaan teer te bhaunh kamaan hoee,
mukhre utte zulfan pareshaan hoee.
Lajja noor te nakhra jioon bijli,
surkhi hothaan te misri di khaan hoee.
Waris Shah ih Heer di shaan saari,
jioon takht utte baithi sultaan hoee.
s3.english_meaning: Her eyes are arrows and her brows a drawn bow; loose tresses fall in artful disarray across her face. Modesty shines from her even as coquetry flashes like lightning, and her reddened lips seem a mine of sugar. Waris Shah says this is Heer in her full majesty, as though a sovereign queen were seated upon a throne.

s3.title: Ranjha arrives at the river
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਆਇਆ ਰਾਂਝਾ ਝਨਾਂ ਦੇ ਕੰਢੇ ਉੱਤੇ
s3.author: Waris Shah
s3.why_dense: This riverbank moment stands alone because it stages arrival, landscape, and anticipation in a single visual scene. A learner gains travel and river vocabulary, plus the importance of the Chenab crossing as a threshold image in Punjabi romance.
s3.cultural_signal: widely performed in folk storytelling; referenced in modern songs invoking Heer-Ranjha’s first meeting
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਆਇਆ ਰਾਂਝਾ ਝਨਾਂ ਦੇ ਕੰਢੇ ਉੱਤੇ,
ਵੇਖਿਆ ਚੁਚਕ ਦਾ ਬਾਗ਼ ਸੁਹਾਵਣਾ ਜੀ।
ਨਾਲੇ ਵੇਖਿਆ ਬੇੜੀ ਹੈ ਖੜੀ ਬੰਨੀ,
ਆਖੇ ਬੇੜੀਵਾਲਿਆ ਬੇੜੀ ਆਣ ਲਾ ਜੀ।
ਮੈਨੂੰ ਪਾਰ ਲੰਘਾ ਚੁਚਕ ਦੇ ਬਾਗ਼ ਅੰਦਰ,
ਜਿੱਥੇ ਯਾਰ ਦਾ ਦੇਸ ਤੇ ਠਾਂਵਣਾ ਜੀ।
s3.romanization:
Aaiyaa Raanjhaa Jhanaan de kandhe utte,
Vekhiaa Chuchak da baagh suhaavnaa jee.
Naale vekhiaa beri hai kharhi banni,
Aakhe beriwaaliyaa beri aan laa jee.
Mainoon paar langhaa Chuchak de baagh andar,
Jitthe yaar da des te thaanvnaa jee.
s3.english_meaning: Ranjha came to the bank of the Chenab and saw Chuchak’s lovely garden before him. Nearby a ferry stood moored, and he called to the boatman to bring it over and carry him across into Chuchak’s orchard, toward the land and dwelling-place of his beloved.

s3.title: First meeting in the boat
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਵੇਖ ਰਾਂਝੇ ਨੂੰ ਹੀਰ ਨੇ ਨਜ਼ਰ ਭਰੀ
s3.author: Waris Shah
s3.why_dense: This is standalone-dense because the emotional spark of first sight is complete in itself and needs no prior explanation to resonate. A learner gains courtly-romantic vocabulary of gaze, modesty, and attraction that recurs across Punjabi love literature.
s3.cultural_signal: mehfil singing; dramatized in folk theatre; widely recognized as the iconic first-meeting scene
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਵੇਖ ਰਾਂਝੇ ਨੂੰ ਹੀਰ ਨੇ ਨਜ਼ਰ ਭਰੀ,
ਹੱਥੀਂ ਖੇਸੜਾ ਧਰਿਆ ਲੁਕਾਇ ਕੇ ਜੀ।
ਨਾਲੇ ਲੱਜਿਆਈ ਤੇ ਨਾਲ ਖਿੜੀ,
ਨਾਲੇ ਚਾਉ ਦੇ ਨਾਲ ਸਮਾਇ ਕੇ ਜੀ।
ਵਾਰਿਸ ਸ਼ਾਹ ਮੀਆਂ ਏਹ ਪਹਿਲਾ ਮਿਲਾਪ ਹੋਇਆ,
ਦਿਲਾਂ ਵਿਚ ਇਸ਼ਕ ਜਗਾਇ ਕੇ ਜੀ।
s3.romanization:
Vekh Raanjhe noo Heer ne nazar bhari,
Hathheen khesrra dharia lukaae ke jee.
Naale lajjiaaee te naal khirri,
Naale chaao de naal samaae ke jee.
Waris Shah miyaan eh pahila milaap hoiaa,
Dilan vich ishq jagaae ke jee.
s3.english_meaning: When Heer filled her gaze with Ranjha, she half-concealed herself behind the shawl in her hands. At once she blushed and blossomed, shy yet inwardly delighted; and, as Waris Shah says, in that first meeting love was kindled in both their hearts.

s3.title: Village girls in full adornment
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਵੱਜਦੀਆਂ ਸੱਜ ਧੱਜ ਕੇ ਮੁਟਿਆਰਾਂ
s3.author: Waris Shah
s3.why_dense: This passage stands alone because it captures village social life, dress, movement, and flirtation in a compact tableau. A learner gains everyday cultural vocabulary around adornment, gendered space, and rural festivity rather than only high romance.
s3.cultural_signal: quoted in folk performance and wedding singing; familiar in heritage memory for its lively village imagery
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਵੱਜਦੀਆਂ ਸੱਜ ਧੱਜ ਕੇ ਮੁਟਿਆਰਾਂ,
ਮੱਥੇ ਚਮਕਣ ਟਿੱਕੇ ਤੇ ਜੁਗਨੀਆਂ ਨੇ।
ਕੰਨਾਂ ਵਿੱਚ ਮੁੰਦ੍ਰਾਂ ਤੇ ਨੱਕ ਵਿੱਚ ਨੱਥਾਂ,
ਗਲਾਂ ਵਿੱਚ ਹਾਰ ਤੇ ਹੱਥਾਂ ਚੂੜੀਆਂ ਨੇ।
ਪੈਰੀਂ ਝਾਂਝਰਾਂ ਛਣਕਦੀਆਂ ਨਾਲ ਚਾਲ ਦੇ,
ਜਿਉਂ ਬਾਗ਼ ਵਿੱਚ ਫਿਰਦੀਆਂ ਹੂਰਾਂ ਪਰੀਆਂ ਨੇ।
s3.romanization:
Vajjdiaan sajj dhajj ke mutiyaaraan,
mathe chamkan tikke te jugniyaan ne.
Kannan vich mundraan te nakk vich natthaan,
galaan vich haar te hatthaan choorhiyaan ne.
Pairin jhanjhraan chhanakdian naal chaal de,
jiun baagh vich phirdian hooraan pariaan ne.
s3.english_meaning: The village maidens come forth fully adorned, their foreheads lit with ornaments that sparkle like fireflies. Earrings, nose-rings, necklaces, bangles, and anklets complete them; as they move, they seem less like mortal girls than houris and fairies wandering through a garden.

s3.title: True love cannot be hidden
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਸੱਚੀ ਪ੍ਰੀਤ ਨਾ ਕਦੇ ਲੁਕਾਈ ਜਾਵੇ
s3.author: Waris Shah
s3.why_dense: This couplet is maxim-like and fully self-contained, making it ideal as a proverb even outside the narrative. A learner gains a famous line of general Punjabi usage and sees how kissa poetry enters everyday speech as ethical wisdom.
s3.cultural_signal: proverbial speech; widely quoted in diaspora; referenced in modern songs and social media captions
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਸੱਚੀ ਪ੍ਰੀਤ ਨਾ ਕਦੇ ਲੁਕਾਈ ਜਾਵੇ,
ਮਸਤ ਹੋਇ ਕੇ ਇਸ਼ਕ਼ ਦੀ ਘਾਤੀ ਹੈ ਜੀ।
s3.romanization:
sachchi preet naa kade lukaai jaave,
mast hoi ke ishq di ghaati hai jee.
s3.english_meaning: True love can never be concealed; it reveals itself despite every veil. Once one is intoxicated by love, one has entered its perilous pass, where the heart can no longer pretend safety.

s3.title: Love ruins both worlds
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਦੋਵੇਂ ਜਹਾਨ ਇਸ਼ਕ ਨੇ ਮਾਰ ਦਿੱਤੇ
s3.author: Waris Shah
s3.why_dense: This band stands alone because it states the total claim of ishq in aphoristic language, balancing worldly loss with spiritual intensity. A learner gains the Sufi-romantic idea that love overturns ordinary success and hears one of the most quotable philosophical tones in Heer.
s3.cultural_signal: widely quoted in literary discussion; mehfil recitation; referenced in modern Punjabi ghazal-style songs
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਦੋਵੇਂ ਜਹਾਨ ਇਸ਼ਕ ਨੇ ਮਾਰ ਦਿੱਤੇ,
ਨਾਲੇ ਇੱਜਤਾਂ ਨਾਲ ਨਮੋਸੀਆਂ ਨੇ।
ਲੱਗੀ ਅੱਗ ਤੇ ਫੂਕਿਆ ਘਰ ਸਾੜਿਆ ਈ,
ਕੀਹ ਖਾਕ ਇਨ੍ਹਾਂ ਨੇ ਮੋਸੀਆਂ ਨੇ।
ਵਾਰਸ ਸ਼ਾਹ ਮੀਆਂ ਜੰਗਲੇ ਜਾਣ ਲੱਗਾ,
ਲੱਗੀਆਂ ਖ਼ੂਬ ਇਹ ਖ਼ਾਮੋਸ਼ੀਆਂ ਨੇ।
s3.romanization:
dovein jahaan ishq ne maar ditte,
naale ijjataan naal namosiaan ne.
laggi agg te phookia ghar saarria ee,
keeh khaak inhan ne mosiaan ne.
Waris Shah miyaan jangle jaan lagga,
laggian khoob eh khaamoshiaan ne.
s3.english_meaning: Love has laid waste to both worlds, and with them honor and good name have been struck down as well. A fire has caught and blown through the house till it is burned to ash—what dust is left for these mourners to gather? Waris Shah says: the lover goes off toward the wilderness, wrapped now in a deep and terrible silence.

s3.title: Those who loved were doomed
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਜਿਨ੍ਹਾਂ ਪ੍ਰੇਮ ਕੀਤਾ ਉਹ ਫਾਂਸੀ ਦੇ ਭਾਗੀ
s3.author: Waris Shah
s3.why_dense: This line is standalone-dense because it condenses the tragic law of love into a proverb that survives independently of the plot. A learner gains a culturally ubiquitous saying and a direct example of how Punjabi compresses fatalism, irony, and emotional truth into one memorable line.
s3.cultural_signal: proverbial speech; quoted in songs, films, and diaspora conversation; one of the best-known lines associated with Heer
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਜਿਨ੍ਹਾਂ ਪ੍ਰੇਮ ਕੀਤਾ ਉਹ ਫਾਂਸੀ ਦੇ ਭਾਗੀ,
ਜਿਨ੍ਹਾਂ ਪ੍ਰੇਮ ਕੀਤਾ ਸੋ ਰੋਣ ਸਿਆਪੇ।
s3.romanization:
jinhaan prem keetaa oh faansi de bhaagee,
jinhaan prem keetaa so ron siyaape.
s3.english_meaning: Those who gave themselves to love were marked for the noose; whoever loved inherited lamentation and grief. The voice is bitterly knowing: love is not reward but ordeal, a fate tied to suffering.

s3.title: Sehti speaks against custom
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਸੇਹਤੀ ਆਖਦੀ ਲੋਕਾਂ ਦੇ ਨੇਮ ਝੂਠੇ
s3.author: Waris Shah
s3.why_dense: This passage stands alone because Sehti’s voice turns the romance into critique of social rules, especially around women’s desire and family control. A learner gains argumentative Punjabi, social vocabulary, and a sense that Heer is not only a love story but also a sharp commentary on custom.
s3.cultural_signal: valued in classroom discussion and literary circles; quoted in feminist readings of Heer
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਸੇਹਤੀ ਆਖਦੀ ਲੋਕਾਂ ਦੇ ਨੇਮ ਝੂਠੇ,
ਇਹਨਾਂ ਨੇਹਾਂ ਦੇ ਨਾਲ ਨਾ ਸਾਥ ਕੋਈ।
ਜਿਹੜੇ ਇਸ਼ਕ ਦੇ ਰਾਹ ਵਿਚ ਪੈਰ ਪਾਉਣ,
ਉਹਨਾਂ ਲੋਕਾਂ ਦੀ ਪਰਵਾਹ ਨਾ ਕਾਥ ਕੋਈ।
ਵਾਰਿਸ ਸ਼ਾਹ ਮੀਆਂ ਇਸ਼ਕ ਦੇ ਮਰਤਬੇ ਵਿੱਚ,
ਮਾਸ਼ੂਕ ਬਾਝ ਨਾ ਹੋਰ ਨਿਬਾਹ ਕੋਈ।
s3.romanization:
Sehti aakhdi lokaan de nem jhoothe,
ihnaan nehaan de naal na saath koee.
Jihre ishq de raah vich pair paaun,
uhnaan lokaan di parvaah na kaath koee.
Waris Shah miyaan ishq de martabe vich,
maashooq baajh na hor nibaah koee.
s3.english_meaning: Sehti says the customs of people are false; in the bonds of love, society offers no true companionship. Whoever steps onto the path of ishq can no longer live by public approval. Waris Shah adds that in love’s high station, nothing endures except fidelity to the beloved.

s3.title: Murad and Sehti’s bold exchange
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਮੁਰਾਦ ਨਾਲ ਸੇਹਤੀ ਦੀ ਗੱਲ ਚਲੀ
s3.author: Waris Shah
s3.why_dense: This scene is dense because flirtation, wit, and social negotiation all appear in a short exchange that can be enjoyed independently. A learner gains colloquial-dialogic Punjabi and sees how side characters broaden the emotional and social world of the kissa.
s3.cultural_signal: mehfil recitation among readers of full Heer; appreciated for lively dialogue and secondary-character color
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਮੁਰਾਦ ਨਾਲ ਸੇਹਤੀ ਦੀ ਗੱਲ ਚਲੀ,
ਜਿਵੇਂ ਲਾਲ ਨਾਲ ਲਾਲ ਮਿਲਾਇਆਂ ਨੀ।
ਦੋਵੇਂ ਆਸ਼ਕ ਤੇ ਮਸਤ ਅਲਸਤ ਹੋਏ,
ਕੀਤੀ ਗੱਲ ਨਾ ਜਾਇ ਸੁਣਾਇਆਂ ਨੀ।
ਵਾਰਿਸ ਸ਼ਾਹ ਮੀਆਂ ਇਉਂ ਆਖਦਾ ਏ,
ਜਿਵੇਂ ਅੱਗ ਨਾਲ ਫੂਸ ਭਿੜਾਇਆਂ ਨੀ।
s3.romanization:
Muraad naal Sehti di gall chali,
jivein laal naal laal milaaiyaan ni.
Dovein aashiq te mast alast hoye,
keeti gall na jaae sunaaiyaan ni.
Waris Shah miyaan iun aakhda ae,
jivein agg naal foos bhiraaiyaan ni.
s3.english_meaning: Murad and Sehti fell into talk like one ruby meeting another, equal in glow and heat. Both were lovers, intoxicated and self-forgetting; what passed between them is not the sort of speech that can be fully told. Waris Shah says it was like dry straw brought against fire.

s3.title: Kaido the informer
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਕੈਦੋ ਚੜ੍ਹਿਆ ਚੁਗਲੀ ਦੇ ਰਾਹ ਉੱਤੇ
s3.author: Waris Shah
s3.why_dense: This band stands alone because Kaido embodies surveillance, gossip, and moral policing in a way instantly recognizable beyond the story. A learner gains vocabulary of accusation and kinship politics while understanding a recurring Punjabi cultural type: the meddling informer.
s3.cultural_signal: widely referenced in speech when comparing someone to Kaido; common in stage adaptations and folk commentary
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਕੈਦੋ ਚੜ੍ਹਿਆ ਚੁਗਲੀ ਦੇ ਰਾਹ ਉੱਤੇ,
ਜਿਵੇਂ ਕਾਂ ਉਜਾੜ ਦੇ ਵਾਸ ਮੀਆਂ।
ਪਿੱਠ ਲਾ ਕੇ ਰਾਂਝੇ ਨੂੰ ਹੇਰ ਦੇਵੇ,
ਜਿਵੇਂ ਚੋਰ ਨੂੰ ਲੋਕੀ ਭਾਲਦੇ ਨਾਸ ਮੀਆਂ।
ਵਾਰਿਸ ਸ਼ਾਹ ਮੀਆਂ ਕੈਦੋਂ ਵਾਂਗ ਸੜੀਏ,
ਜਿਵੇਂ ਲੂਣ ਨੂੰ ਖਾਂਦਾ ਹੈ ਘਾਸ ਮੀਆਂ।
s3.romanization:
Kaido charhia chugli de raah utte,
jivein kaan ujaarr de vaas miyaan.
Pitth laa ke Raanjhe noo her deve,
jivein chor noo loki bhaalde naas miyaan.
Waris Shah miyaan Kaidon vaang sarriye,
jivein loon noo khaanda hai ghaas miyaan.
s3.english_meaning: Kaido takes to the path of slander the way a crow settles over desolation. He dogs Ranjha from behind, hunting him down as people track a thief to ruin. Waris Shah adds a bitter curse: may one burn like Kaido, as salt eats into and withers the grass.

s3.title: God’s paths are not for cows
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਰੱਬ ਦੀਆਂ ਰਾਹਾਂ ਗਊਆਂ ਤੇ ਨਹੀਂ
s3.author: Waris Shah
s3.why_dense: This proverb-like line is fully portable outside the narrative and works as a sharp rebuke against blind following. A learner gains one of the most cited sayings linked to Heer and sees how metaphor in Punjabi can be earthy, humorous, and philosophical at once.
s3.cultural_signal: proverbial speech; widely quoted in diaspora conversation; used in commentary on conformity and herd behavior
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਰੱਬ ਦੀਆਂ ਰਾਹਾਂ ਗਊਆਂ ਤੇ ਨਹੀਂ, ਭਾਵੇਂ ਸੋ ਸੋ ਚਾਰਵਾਹੀਏ ਜੀ,
ਜਿਨ੍ਹਾਂ ਇਸ਼ਕ ਖਰੀਦ ਨਾ ਕੀਤੋਈ, ਸੁੱਤੇ ਪਏ ਨੇ ਜਿਉਂ ਮਾਹੀਏ ਜੀ।
s3.romanization:
Rabb diaan raahaan gaauaan te nahin, bhaaven so so chaarvaahie jee,
Jinhaan ishq khareed na keetoi, sutte pae ne jiun maahie jee.
s3.english_meaning: The ways that lead to God are not for cattle, even if hundreds of herdsmen drive them along. Those who have never purchased love lie asleep like fish, untouched by the living current of longing.

s3.title: Heer’s forced wedding
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਹੀਰ ਨੂੰ ਖੇੜਿਆਂ ਨਾਲ ਤੋਰ ਦਿੱਤਾ
s3.author: Waris Shah
s3.why_dense: This passage stands alone because the violence of arranged separation is emotionally legible without needing the full preceding plot. A learner gains wedding and departure vocabulary while confronting a central Punjabi literary theme: marriage as social coercion rather than fulfillment.
s3.cultural_signal: widely dramatized in folk theatre; quoted in discussions of Heer as critique of forced marriage
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਹੀਰ ਨੂੰ ਖੇੜਿਆਂ ਨਾਲ ਤੋਰ ਦਿੱਤਾ, ਜਿਵੇਂ ਕੈਦ ਵਿਚੋਂ ਚੋਰ ਕੱਢਿਆਈ ਏ।
ਨਾਲੇ ਬਾਬੁਲ ਨੇ ਦਿੱਤੀਆਂ ਵਿਦਾਈਆਂ, ਨਾਲੇ ਨੈਣਾਂ ਵਿਚੋਂ ਨੀਰ ਵਗਾਇਆਈ ਏ।
ਰਾਂਝੇ ਯਾਰ ਬਗੈਰ ਨਾ ਜੀਉਂਦੀ ਏ, ਅੰਦਰੋਂ ਦੁੱਖ ਨੇ ਬਹੁਤ ਸਤਾਇਆਈ ਏ।
ਵਾਰਿਸ ਸ਼ਾਹ ਮੀਆਂ ਕਹੇ ਖੇੜਿਆਂ ਨੇ, ਹੀਰ ਸਿਆਲਾਂ ਦੀ ਘਰੋਂ ਲਿਆਈ ਏ।
s3.romanization:
Heer noon Kherian naal tor dittaa, jiwen qaid vichon chor kaddhiaaee ae.
Naale baabul ne dittiaan vidaaeeaan, naale nainaan vichon neer vagaaiaaee ae.
Raanjhe yaar bagair na jeeundi ae, andron dukh ne bahut sataiaaee ae.
Waris Shah mian kahe Kherian ne, Heer Siaalaan di gharon liaaee ae.
s3.english_meaning: Heer was sent off with the Kheras as though a thief were being dragged out of prison. Her father gave the formal farewells even as tears streamed from every eye; without her beloved Ranjha she could not truly live, and inward grief tormented her. Waris Shah says: the Kheras carried Heer away from the house of the Sials.

s3.title: Ranjha renounces the world
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਰਾਂਝਾ ਜਾ ਕੇ ਜੋਗੀ ਭੇਖ ਧਰਿਆ
s3.author: Waris Shah
s3.why_dense: This transformation scene is self-contained because the change of dress, identity, and spiritual posture carries its own dramatic force. A learner gains ascetic vocabulary such as jogi, bhes, and renunciation, and sees how earthly love shades into mystical seeking in Punjabi poetics.
s3.cultural_signal: mehfil singing; often referenced in songs about jogi imagery; central to popular retellings of Heer-Ranjha
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਰਾਂਝਾ ਜਾ ਕੇ ਜੋਗੀ ਭੇਖ ਧਰਿਆ,
ਸਿਰ ਮੁੰਡ ਕੇ ਕੰਨ ਪੜਵਾ ਲਏ ਨੇ।
ਗਲ ਵਿਚ ਸੇਲੀ ਤੇ ਹੱਥ ਵਿਚ ਡੰਡਾ,
ਦੇਹੀ ਭਸਮ ਚੜ੍ਹਾ ਕੇ ਆਏ ਨੇ।
ਵਾਰਿਸ ਸ਼ਾਹ ਮੀਆਂ ਐਸ ਜੋਗੀਆਂ ਨੇ,
ਹੀਰ ਵਾਸਤੇ ਜੋਗ ਕਮਾਏ ਨੇ।
s3.romanization:
Raanjha jaa ke jogi bhekh dharia,
Sir mund ke kann parvaa lae ne.
Gal vich seli te hath vich danda,
Dehi bhasam charhaa ke aae ne.
Waris Shah miyaan ais jogiaan ne,
Heer vaaste jog kamaae ne.
s3.english_meaning: Ranjha goes and assumes the garb of a yogi: his head is shaved, his ears are pierced, a sacred cord hangs at his neck, a staff rests in his hand, and ash is smeared over his body. Waris Shah remarks with quiet irony that this is the kind of yogi he has become—one who practices renunciation not for God alone, but for Heer.

s3.title: The jogi at Khera village
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਜੋਗੀ ਆਇਆ ਖੇੜਿਆਂ ਦੇ ਦੇਸ ਅੰਦਰ
s3.author: Waris Shah
s3.why_dense: This band stands alone because disguise, suspense, and irony are all present in the simple image of the jogi entering hostile territory. A learner gains place and movement vocabulary and experiences how Punjabi narrative builds tension through public disguise and hidden identity.
s3.cultural_signal: folk recitation; stage adaptations; recognized in retellings of the reunion arc
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਜੋਗੀ ਆਇਆ ਖੇੜਿਆਂ ਦੇ ਦੇਸ ਅੰਦਰ,
ਦੇਖਿਆ ਹੀਰ ਦਾ ਤਖ਼ਤ ਹਜ਼ਾਰਿਆਂ ਨੇ।
ਵਾਰਸ ਸ਼ਾਹ ਮੀਆਂ ਰਾਂਝੇ ਨੂੰ ਵੇਖ ਕੇ ਜੀ,
ਜਿਉਂ ਲੁਤਫ਼ ਬਹਿਸ਼ਤ ਦੇ ਯਾਰਿਆਂ ਨੇ।
s3.romanization:
Jogi aaia Kherian de des andar,
Dekhya Heer da takht hazaarian ne.
Waris Shah mian Ranjhe noo vekh ke jee,
Jioon lutf bahisht de yaarian ne.
s3.english_meaning: The jogi came into the land of the Kheras and beheld Heer’s throne, famed among multitudes. Waris Shah says: to see Ranjha there was like tasting the delight of paradise among beloved companions.

s3.title: Heer recognizes Ranjha
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਵੇਖ ਜੋਗੀ ਨੂੰ ਹੀਰ ਪਛਾਣ ਗਈ
s3.author: Waris Shah
s3.why_dense: This reunion moment is standalone-dense because recognition itself is the whole emotional event, requiring almost no explanation to move the listener. A learner gains language of recognition, concealment, and emotional shock, all in one of the great scenes of Punjabi romance.
s3.cultural_signal: widely performed in mehfils and stage versions; often cited as a peak romantic scene
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਵੇਖ ਜੋਗੀ ਨੂੰ ਹੀਰ ਪਛਾਣ ਗਈ,
ਮੱਥੇ ਮਾਰ ਕੇ ਧਾਹਾਂ ਉਹ ਰੋਈ ਏ।
ਫੜ ਕੇ ਹੱਥ ਰਾਂਝੇ ਦੇ ਨਾਲ ਲੱਗੀ,
ਜਿਵੇਂ ਬਿਜਲੀ ਦੇ ਸੰਗ ਬੱਦਲੋਈ ਏ।
ਵਾਰਿਸ ਸ਼ਾਹ ਮੀਆਂ ਏਹ ਜੋਗੀਆ ਜੀ,
ਹੀਰ ਆਖਦੀ ਨਾਲ ਕੀਹ ਲੋਈ ਏ।
s3.romanization:
Vekh jogi nu Heer pachhaan gayi,
mathe maar ke dhaahaan oh roee ae.
Pharr ke hath Raanjhe de naal laggi,
jiven bijli de sang baddaloi ae.
Waris Shah miyaan eh jogia ji,
Heer aakhdi naal keeh loee ae.
s3.english_meaning: The moment Heer sees the jogi, she knows him; striking her forehead, she breaks into sobs. She catches Ranjha’s hand and clings to him like lightning fastening itself to a rain-cloud. Waris Shah says: Heer addresses the ascetic tenderly, asking what covering or bond now remains between them.

s3.title: Poison prepared for Heer
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਜ਼ਹਿਰ ਹੀਰ ਦੇ ਵਾਸਤੇ ਘੋਲਿਆ ਗਿਆ
s3.author: Waris Shah
s3.why_dense: This passage stands alone because the act of poisoning is a complete tragic image and one of the best-known climactic turns in the story. A learner gains vocabulary of betrayal and death while seeing how Punjabi tragedy often arrives through domestic treachery rather than battlefield violence.
s3.cultural_signal: widely dramatized in folk theatre and television adaptations; familiar in heritage retellings of Heer
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਜ਼ਹਿਰ ਹੀਰ ਦੇ ਵਾਸਤੇ ਘੋਲਿਆ ਗਿਆ,
ਕੈਦੋ ਲੈ ਗਿਆ ਚੂਚਕ ਦੇ ਕੋਲ ਮੀਆਂ।
ਚੂਚਕ ਆਖਿਆ ਇਹ ਕੀਹ ਬਲਾ ਲਿਆਈ,
ਕੈਦੋ ਆਖਿਆ ਏਹੀ ਹੈ ਠੀਕ ਮੀਆਂ।
ਹੀਰ ਖਾਏਗੀ ਤਾਂ ਛੁੱਟੇਗੀ ਇਸ਼ਕ ਵਾਲੋਂ,
ਨਹੀਂ ਤਾਂ ਹੋਵੇਗੀ ਖ਼ਾਨਦਾਨ ਨੂੰ ਠੀਕ ਮੀਆਂ।
s3.romanization:
Zahir Heer de vaaste gholia giya,
Kaido lai giya Choochak de kol miyaan.
Choochak aakhia ih keeh balaa liaaee,
Kaido aakhia ehi hai theek miyaan.
Heer khaayegi taan chhuttegi ishq vaalon,
nahin taan hovegi khandaan noo theek miyaan.
s3.english_meaning: Poison was mixed for Heer, and Kaido carried it to Choochak. When Choochak asked what calamity he had brought, Kaido coldly replied that this alone was the proper remedy: if Heer consumed it, she would be freed from love; otherwise, the family’s honor would never be set right.

s3.title: Ranjha’s lament over Heer
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਰਾਂਝਾ ਰੋਇਆ ਹੀਰ ਦੇ ਮਰਨ ਉੱਤੇ
s3.author: Waris Shah
s3.why_dense: This lament stands alone because grief speech is immediately intelligible and emotionally complete even without surrounding narrative detail. A learner gains elegiac vocabulary and hears the high tragic register that made Heer a touchstone for later Punjabi mourning and longing songs.
s3.cultural_signal: mehfil singing; quoted in literary mourning contexts; echoed in modern tragic-romantic lyrics
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਰਾਂਝਾ ਰੋਇਆ ਹੀਰ ਦੇ ਮਰਨ ਉੱਤੇ,
ਜਿਵੇਂ ਰੋਣਦੇ ਆਸ਼ਕਾਂ ਦੇ ਨੈਣ ਮੀਆਂ।
ਪਏ ਪੈਰਾਂ ਤੇ ਮੂੰਹ ਨਿਵਾਇ ਕੈ ਉਹ,
ਜਿਵੇਂ ਸਜਦੇ ਵਿਚ ਪੜ੍ਹਦੇ ਨੇ ਮੋਮਨ ਮੀਆਂ।
ਵਾਰਿਸ ਸ਼ਾਹ ਮੀਆਂ ਰਾਂਝੇ ਨੂੰ ਸਬਰ ਨਾ ਹੀ,
ਰੋਵੇ ਹੀਰ ਦੇ ਵਾਂਗ ਪਰੈਣ ਮੀਆਂ।
s3.romanization:
Raanjha roia Heer de maran utte,
Jivein ronde aashkaan de nain miyaan.
Pae pairaan te moonh nivaa-e kai oh,
Jivein sajde vich parhde ne moman miyaan.
Waris Shah miyaan Raanjhe noo sabar naa hee,
Rove Heer de vaang parain miyaan.
s3.english_meaning: Ranjha wept over Heer’s death the way lovers’ eyes are made to weep. He fell at her feet with his face bowed low, like a believer bent in prostration at prayer; and, says Waris Shah, no patience remained in him—he cried out over Heer as one utterly undone, cast down in grief.

s3.title: Waris Shah closes the book
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਵਾਰਸ ਸ਼ਾਹ ਇਹ ਕਿਤਾਬ ਤਮਾਮ ਹੋਈ
s3.author: Waris Shah
s3.why_dense: This closing is standalone-dense because it returns the listener from story to poet, making the act of composition itself visible. A learner gains meta-literary vocabulary and a sense of how classical Punjabi texts end with humility, signature, and prayerful closure.
s3.cultural_signal: quoted in literary events and editions of Heer; used to mark completion in recitation settings
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਵਾਰਸ ਸ਼ਾਹ ਇਹ ਕਿਤਾਬ ਤਮਾਮ ਹੋਈ
ਲੱਗੀ ਮੁਕਣ ਤੇ ਜਿੰਦੜੀ ਝੂਰਦੀ ਏ
ਰੋਣ ਅੱਖੀਆਂ ਤੇ ਨਾਲੇ ਦਿਲ ਰੋਵੇ
ਅੱਖੀਂ ਮਿਲੇ ਨਹੀਂ ਸੰਗਤਾਂ ਦੂਰਦੀ ਏ
ਲੱਗੀ ਵਲ ਫ਼ਰਾਕ ਦੇ ਜ਼ਖ਼ਮ ਕਾਰੀ
ਸਾਂਈਂ ਸੂਲ ਕੀ ਜਾਣਦਾ ਹੂਰ ਦੀ ਏ
s3.romanization:
Waris Shah ih kitaab tamaam hoee
Laggi mukan te jindri jhoordi ae
Ron akkhian te naale dil rove
Akkheen mile nahin sangatān doordi ae
Laggi val firaaq de zakhm kaari
Saaeen sool kee jaanda hoor di ae
s3.english_meaning: Waris Shah says: this book has come to its end, and as it closes, the soul itself withers in sorrow. The eyes weep and the heart weeps with them; companions are no longer met face to face, for separation has driven them far apart. The wound of parting cuts deep and cruelly—what would the lordly, carefree one know of the piercing pain borne by the beautiful beloved?

s3.title: Mirza’s horse Bakki
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਬਾਕੀ ਘੋੜੀ ਮਿਰਜ਼ੇ ਦੀ ਬੇਮਿਸਾਲ
s3.author: Peelu
s3.why_dense: This passage stands alone because Bakki is more than a horse: it is a cultural emblem of speed, nobility, and heroic mobility. A learner gains equestrian vocabulary and the background to the famous expression 'ਮਿਰਜ਼ੇ ਦੀ ਬਾਕੀ' in everyday Punjabi speech.
s3.cultural_signal: proverbial speech; widely referenced in folk song and casual comparison for speed or excellence
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਬਾਕੀ ਘੋੜੀ ਮਿਰਜ਼ੇ ਦੀ ਬੇਮਿਸਾਲ,
ਜਿਹਦੀ ਸਿਫ਼ਤ ਕਰੇ ਸਭ ਜਹਾਨ ਸਾਰ।
ਹਵਾ ਦੇ ਨਾਲ ਗੱਲਾਂ ਕਰਦੀ ਚਾਲ,
ਬਿਜਲੀ ਵਾਂਗ ਲੰਘੇ ਮੈਦਾਨ ਅਪਾਰ।
ਜੰਗ ਵਿਚ ਜਾਵੇ ਤਾਂ ਸ਼ੇਰ ਦੀ ਦਹਾੜ,
ਪੀਲੂ ਆਖੇ ਐਸੀ ਨਾਹੀ ਦੂਜੀ ਸਵਾਰ।
s3.romanization:
Baaki ghori Mirze di bemisaal,
Jihdi sifat kare sab jahaan saar.
Havaa de naal gallaan kardi chaal,
Bijli vaang langhe maidaan apaar.
Jang vich jaave taan sher di dahaad,
Peelu aakhe aisi naahee dooji savaar.
s3.english_meaning: Bakki, Mirza’s mare, is beyond compare; all the world speaks of her excellence. Her gait seems to converse with the wind, and she crosses vast plains like lightning. In battle she carries the force of a lion’s roar—Peelu says no second mount like her exists.

s3.title: Mirza the archer
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਤੀਰ ਕਮਾਨ ਦਾ ਮਿਰਜ਼ਾ ਮਾਹਰ ਸੀ
s3.author: Peelu
s3.why_dense: This scene is dense because martial skill, masculine prestige, and public reputation are all compressed into one display moment. A learner gains vocabulary of weapons and contest while seeing how romance in Punjabi kissa often intersects with warrior culture.
s3.cultural_signal: folk performance; school and popular retellings of Mirza as the archetypal archer-lover
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਤੀਰ ਕਮਾਨ ਦਾ ਮਿਰਜ਼ਾ ਮਾਹਰ ਸੀ
ਜਿਹਦਾ ਕੰਬਦਾ ਹੱਥ ਨ ਆਵਦਾ ਸੀ
ਨਿਸ਼ਾਨੇ ਉੱਤੇ ਤੀਰ ਜੋ ਛੱਡਦਾ ਸੀ
ਵਾਂਗ ਬਿਜਲੀ ਦੇ ਜਾ ਲਗਾਵਦਾ ਸੀ
s3.romanization:
teer kamaan da Mirza maahar see
jihdaa kambdaa hath na aavdaa see
nishaane utte teer jo chhadddaa see
vaang bijlee de jaa lagaavdaa see
s3.english_meaning: Mirza was a consummate master of bow and arrow; his hand never trembled. Whenever he loosed an arrow at the mark, it flew and struck like a flash of lightning.

s3.title: Sahiban breaks the arrows
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਸਾਹਿਬਾਂ ਨੇ ਤੀਰ ਤਰਕਸ਼ ਤੋਂ ਤੋੜ ਦਿੱਤੇ
s3.author: Peelu
s3.why_dense: This passage stands alone because one irreversible action carries the whole tragedy: love tries to prevent bloodshed and instead invites disaster. A learner gains dramatic vocabulary of choice, hesitation, and fate, and encounters the most discussed moral turning point in Mirza Sahiban.
s3.cultural_signal: widely dramatized in folk theatre and songs; commonly referenced when discussing fatal hesitation
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਸਾਹਿਬਾਂ ਨੇ ਤੀਰ ਤਰਕਸ਼ ਤੋਂ ਤੋੜ ਦਿੱਤੇ,
ਮਿਰਜ਼ੇ ਦੇ ਹੱਥੋਂ ਹਥਿਆਰ ਸਭ ਖੋਹ ਲਏ।
ਆਖੇ ਜੇ ਜਾਗ ਪਿਆ ਤੇ ਭਰਾ ਆ ਪਹੁੰਚੇ,
ਖੂਨ ਦੇ ਨਾਲ ਅੱਜ ਮੈਦਾਨ ਰੋਹ ਲਏ।
ਇਸ਼ਕ ਦੇ ਵਾਸਤੇ ਜੰਗ ਨਾ ਹੋਵੇ ਘਰ ਦੀ,
ਸਾਹਿਬਾਂ ਐਸੇ ਵਿਚਾਰ ਮਨ ਮੋਹ ਲਏ।
s3.romanization:
Sahibaan ne teer tarkash ton torr ditte,
Mirze de hatthon hathiyaar sabh khoh lae.
Aakhe je jaag pia te bhraa aa pahunchhe,
khoon de naal ajj maidaan roh lae.
Ishq de vaaste jang na hove ghar di,
Sahibaan aise vichaar man moh lae.
s3.english_meaning: Sahiban snapped the arrows from the quiver and took every weapon from Mirza’s hands. She thinks: if he wakes and her brothers arrive, the field will be drenched in blood; for love’s sake, let there not be a war within the family. In that fatal tenderness, Sahiban lets her heart be ruled by such thoughts.

s3.title: Death of Mirza and Sahiban
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਡਿੱਗੇ ਮਿਰਜ਼ਾ ਸਾਹਿਬਾਂ ਖੂਨ ਵਿਚ ਰੁਲਦੇ
s3.author: Peelu
s3.why_dense: This death scene is self-contained because the lovers’ end is the emotional and moral conclusion of the whole kissa in one image. A learner gains lament and battlefield-death vocabulary while comparing Mirza’s heroic tragedy with Heer’s more domestic tragedy.
s3.cultural_signal: folk singing; stage adaptations; widely known in Punjabi oral retellings of doomed love
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਡਿੱਗੇ ਮਿਰਜ਼ਾ ਸਾਹਿਬਾਂ ਖੂਨ ਵਿਚ ਰੁਲਦੇ,
ਥਈਆਂ ਲੱਗੀਆਂ ਜਾ ਕੇ ਜੰਡ ਦੇ ਮੁੱਢ ਮੀਆਂ।
ਲੱਗੀ ਸਾਹਿਬਾਂ ਮਿਰਜ਼ੇ ਦੇ ਨਾਲ ਕਹਿਣ,
ਜਿਵੇਂ ਲੱਗੀਆਂ ਲੈਲਾ ਮਜਨੂੰ ਦੇ ਨਾਲ ਮੀਆਂ।
ਪੀਲੂ ਖੂਨੀਂ ਆਸ਼ਕਾਂ ਦੀਆਂ ਕਿਆ ਬਾਤਾਂ,
ਲੱਗੀ ਮਰਣ ਦੇ ਨਾਲ ਹੀ ਮਰਣ ਮੀਆਂ।
s3.romanization:
Digge Mirzaa Sahibaan khoon vich rulde,
Thaiyaan laggian jaa ke jand de mudd miyaan.
Laggi Sahibaan Mirze de naal kahin,
Jiven laggian Laila Majnoon de naal miyaan.
Peelu khoonee aashkaan diaan kia baataan,
Laggi maran de naal hee maran miyaan.
s3.english_meaning: Mirza and Sahiban fell, rolling in blood, collapsing at the foot of the jand tree. Sahiban clung to Mirza as Laila to Majnun, and Peelu says: what can one even say of lovers blood-soaked in love? Once joined to death, they die together.

s3.title: Sohni the potter’s daughter
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਸੋਹਣੀ ਕੁੰਭਾਰਣ ਰੂਪ ਦੀ ਰਾਣੀ
s3.author: Hashim Shah
s3.why_dense: This passage stands alone because it introduces Sohni through beauty, craft lineage, and social setting in a compact portrait. A learner gains artisan-caste and domestic vocabulary and sees how Punjabi romance often roots beauty in everyday occupational worlds.
s3.cultural_signal: widely quoted in folk singing; referenced in modern songs about Sohni’s iconic beauty
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਸੋਹਣੀ ਕੁੰਭਾਰਣ ਰੂਪ ਦੀ ਰਾਣੀ
ਜਿਉਂ ਚੜ੍ਹੇ ਅਸਮਾਨੀਂ ਪੂਰਨ ਚਾਨਣੀ
ਨੈਨਾਂ ਦੇ ਤੀਰ ਲਗਣ ਦਿਲ ਅੰਦਰ
ਮੁਖੜੇ ਉੱਤੇ ਖਿੜੀ ਹੋਈ ਜਵਾਨੀ
ਹਾਸ਼ਮ ਸ਼ਾਹ ਆਖੇ ਜਹਾਨ ਵਿਚ ਅਜਬ
ਨਾਹੀਂ ਵੇਖੀ ਐਸੀ ਸੂਰਤ ਸੁਹਾਣੀ
s3.romanization:
Sohni kumbhaaran roop di raani
Jioon charhe asmaaneen pooran chaanani
Nainaan de teer lagan dil andar
Mukhre utte khirri hoi jawaani
Hashim Shah aakhe jahaan vich ajab
Naaheen vekhi aisi soorat suhaani
s3.english_meaning: Sohni, the potter’s daughter, is a queen of beauty, like the full moon rising into the sky and pouring out its light. The arrows of her eyes strike straight into the heart, and youth blooms openly upon her face; Hashim Shah says the world has scarcely seen a form so wondrous and lovely.

s3.title: Sohni crosses on the earthen pot
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਘੜਾ ਲੈ ਕੇ ਸੋਹਣੀ ਚਨਾਬ ਵਿਚ ਉਤਰੀ
s3.author: Hashim Shah
s3.why_dense: This is standalone-dense because the image of a woman crossing a river on a clay pot is instantly visual, symbolic, and unforgettable. A learner gains river and vessel vocabulary and one of the most culturally ubiquitous images in all Punjabi love lore.
s3.cultural_signal: constantly referenced in modern songs; folk painting and stage performance; universally recognized in Punjabi culture
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਘੜਾ ਲੈ ਕੇ ਸੋਹਣੀ ਚਨਾਬ ਵਿਚ ਉਤਰੀ,
ਇਸ਼ਕ ਅੱਲਾਹ ਦੀ ਜਾਤ ਦਾ ਰੰਗ ਚੜ੍ਹਿਆ।
ਲਹਿਰਾਂ ਝਾਕਦੀਆਂ ਵਾਂਗ ਮੁਹੱਬਤ ਵਾਲੀਆਂ,
ਰਾਤ ਅੰਧੇਰੀ ਤੇ ਪਾਣੀ ਡੂੰਘਾ ਵੜਿਆ।
ਮਾਹੀਵਾਲ ਦੇ ਵਾਸਤੇ ਜਾਨ ਹਥੇਲੀ ਧਰੀ,
ਹਾਸ਼ਿਮ ਆਖੇ ਇਸ਼ਕ ਨੇ ਸਭ ਕੁਝ ਸੜਿਆ।
s3.romanization:
Gharha lai ke Sohni Chanaab vich utri,
Ishq Allah di zaat da rang charhia.
Lahiraan jhaakdian vaang muhabbat vaalian,
Raat andheri te paani doongha varhia.
Maahiwaal de vaaste jaan hatheli dhari,
Hashim aakhe ishq ne sabh kujh sarhia.
s3.english_meaning: Taking the earthen pot, Sohni stepped down into the Chenab, steeped in the color of a love that feels god-given. The waves peer at her like fellow lovers; the night is dark and the water deep, yet for Mahiwal she carries her life on her palm. Hashim says that love burns away everything else.

s3.title: The unbaked pot breaks
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਕੱਚਾ ਘੜਾ ਸੀ ਵਿਚ ਪਾਣੀ ਦੇ ਘੁਲਿਆ
s3.author: Hashim Shah
s3.why_dense: This passage stands alone because the breaking pot is both literal event and perfect symbol of fragile trust betrayed. A learner gains the contrast of ਕੱਚਾ and ਪੱਕਾ in a memorable context and understands why Sohni’s death scene is so often invoked in Punjabi metaphor.
s3.cultural_signal: widely quoted in songs and proverbial comparison; central image in visual and musical retellings
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਕੱਚਾ ਘੜਾ ਸੀ ਵਿਚ ਪਾਣੀ ਦੇ ਘੁਲਿਆ,
ਸੋਹਣੀ ਵਿਚ ਦਰਿਆ ਦੇ ਡੁੱਬ ਮੋਈ।
ਮਾਹੀਵਾਲ ਨੇ ਸੁਣ ਕੇ ਜ਼ੋਰ ਕੀਤਾ,
ਲੱਗਾ ਛਾਲ ਤੇ ਜਾਇ ਉਸ ਢੋਈ।
ਦੋਵੇਂ ਆਣ ਕੇ ਵਿਚ ਦਰਿਆ ਦੇ ਡੁੱਬੇ,
ਲੋਕੀਂ ਆਖਦੇ ਇਸ਼ਕ ਦੀ ਹੋਈ।
s3.romanization:
Kachcha gharaa see vich paanee de ghuliaa,
Sohni vich dariyaa de dubb moee.
Maahiwaal ne sun ke zor keetaa,
Laggaa chhaal te jaae us dhoee.
Dovein aan ke vich dariyaa de dubbe,
Lokeein aakhde ishq dee hoee.
s3.english_meaning: The unbaked pitcher dissolved in the river water, and Sohni sank and died in midstream. Hearing of it, Mahiwal hurled himself after her; both were swallowed by the river together, and people say this was the final consummation of love.

s3.title: Luna tempts Puran
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਲੂਣਾ ਨੇ ਪੂਰਨ ਅੱਗੇ ਗੱਲ ਚਲਾਈ
s3.author: Qadar Yaar
s3.why_dense: This scene is dense because desire, taboo, power, and impending accusation all gather in a short exchange that needs little setup. A learner gains kinship and honor vocabulary and sees how Punjabi kissa can probe ethics and gendered power beyond romance.
s3.cultural_signal: school and literary discussion; folk retellings of Puran Bhagat as a moral drama
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਲੂਣਾ ਨੇ ਪੂਰਨ ਅੱਗੇ ਗੱਲ ਚਲਾਈ,
ਪੁੱਤਰ! ਸੁਣੀਂ ਤੂੰ ਗੱਲ ਨਿਆਰੀ।
ਤੇਰੇ ਰੂਪ ਉੱਤੇ ਮੈਂ ਹੋਈ ਕੁਰਬਾਨ,
ਅੱਖੀਂ ਵੇਖ ਕੇ ਗਈ ਹਾਂ ਹਾਰੀ।
ਛੱਡ ਪਰਹੇਜ਼ ਤੇ ਨਾਲ ਮੇਰੇ ਲੱਗ,
ਕਾਹਨੂੰ ਕਰੇਂ ਤੂੰ ਸ਼ਰਮ ਸਾਰੀ।
s3.romanization:
Loona ne Pooran agge gall chalaai,
puttar! suneen toon gall niaari.
Tere roop utte main hoee qurbaan,
akkheen vekh ke gaee haan haari.
Chhadd parhez te naal mere lagg,
kaahnoon karein toon sharam saari.
s3.english_meaning: Luna opens the forbidden conversation before Puran and calls him close with a voice made intimate and dangerous. She says she is undone by his beauty, defeated by what her own eyes have seen, and urges him to cast aside restraint and shame and come to her.

s3.title: Gorakhnath raises Puran to sainthood
s3.first_line_gurmukhi: ਗੋਰਖਨਾਥ ਨੇ ਪੂਰਨ ਨੂੰ ਬਖ਼ਸ਼ ਲਿਆ
s3.author: Qadar Yaar
s3.why_dense: This passage stands alone because rescue, grace, and spiritual rebirth are complete themes in themselves without requiring the whole royal backstory. A learner gains Nath-panthi and saintly vocabulary and sees how Punjabi narrative poetry links suffering with transformation into bhagat-hood.
s3.cultural_signal: devotional recitation; bridge text in curricula connecting folklore, ethics, and saint traditions
s3.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਗੋਰਖਨਾਥ ਨੇ ਪੂਰਨ ਨੂੰ ਬਖ਼ਸ਼ ਲਿਆ,
ਕੱਢ ਕੂਏਂ ਵਿਚੋਂ ਬਾਹਰ ਲਿਆਇਆ ਏ।
ਨਾਥ ਜੀ ਨੇ ਕਰਮ ਦੀ ਨਜ਼ਰ ਕੀਤੀ,
ਨਾਮ ਅੱਲਾਹ ਦਾ ਜਾਪ ਜਪਾਇਆ ਏ।
ਕਾਦਰ ਯਾਰ ਹੁਣ ਪੂਰਨ ਭਗਤ ਬਣਿਆ,
ਗੁਰੂ ਗੋਰਖ ਦਾ ਚੇਲਾ ਸਦਾਇਆ ਏ।
s3.romanization:
Gorakhnath ne Pooran noo bakhsh lia,
kaDDh kooe vichon baahar liaaiaa e.
Naath jee ne karam dee nazar keetee,
naam Allaah da jaap japaiaa e.
Qadar Yaar hun Pooran Bhagat baniyaa,
guroo Gorakh da chela sadaaiaa e.
s3.english_meaning: Gorakhnath grants Puran grace and draws him up out of the well into a new life. With the compassionate glance of a true master, he sets him on the path of sacred remembrance; thus, says Qadar Yaar, Puran is transformed into Bhagat Puran, known now as Gorakh’s disciple.

s4.title: Birha as Sovereign
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਬਿਰਹਾ ਬਿਰਹਾ ਆਖੀਐ ਬਿਰਹਾ ਤੂ ਸੁਲਤਾਨੁ
s4.why_dense: This salok teaches that longing is not weakness but the highest spiritual condition. A learner gains the key Punjabi-Sufi idea of birha as a force that refines the self and deepens devotion.
s4.cultural_signal: SGGS kirtan / widely quoted in diaspora / Sufi mehfil
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਬਿਰਹਾ ਬਿਰਹਾ ਆਖੀਐ ਬਿਰਹਾ ਤੂ ਸੁਲਤਾਨੁ ॥
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਜਿਤੁ ਤਨਿ ਬਿਰਹੁ ਨ ਊਪਜੈ ਸੋ ਤਨੁ ਜਾਣੁ ਮਸਾਨੁ ॥
s4.romanization:
Fareedaa birhaa birhaa aakheeai birhaa too sultaan.
Fareedaa jit tan birahu na oopjai so tan jaan masaan.
s4.english_meaning: Farid says: men speak of separation and longing, yet longing itself is the sovereign lord. Know that body in which no pang of divine separation arises to be but a cremation-ground.

s4.title: Dark Clothes, Dark Self
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਫਰੀਦਾ ਕਾਲੇ ਮੈਡੇ ਕਪੜੇ ਕਾਲਾ ਮੈਡਾ ਵੇਸੁ
s4.why_dense: This salok teaches radical self-examination by contrasting outward appearance with inward moral state. A learner gains a memorable idiom for humility and the Punjabi habit of ethical introspection through image.
s4.cultural_signal: SGGS kirtan / ethical discourse / widely quoted in diaspora
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਕਾਲੇ ਮੈਡੇ ਕਪੜੇ ਕਾਲਾ ਮੈਡਾ ਵੇਸੁ ॥
ਗੁਨਹੀ ਭਰਿਆ ਮੈ ਫਿਰਾ ਲੋਕੁ ਕਹੈ ਦਰਵੇਸੁ ॥੧੩੧॥
s4.romanization:
Fareedaa kaale maide kaprhe kaalaa maida ves.
Gunhee bhariaa mai phiraa lok kahai darves. ||131||
s4.english_meaning: Farid says: black are my garments, and black is my attire. Laden with sins I wander, yet the people call me a dervish.

s4.title: Do Not Slander Dust
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਫਰੀਦਾ ਖਾਕੁ ਨ ਨਿੰਦੀਐ ਖਾਕੂ ਜੇਡੁ ਨ ਕੋਇ
s4.why_dense: This salok teaches humility by reminding us that dust is both our origin and our end. A learner gains one of the most proverb-like Farid lines, useful for understanding Punjabi speech about ego and mortality.
s4.cultural_signal: SGGS kirtan / folk proverbs / ethical discourse
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਖਾਕੁ ਨ ਨਿੰਦੀਐ ਖਾਕੂ ਜੇਡੁ ਨ ਕੋਇ ॥
ਜੀਵਦਿਆ ਪੈਰ ਤਲੇ ਮੁਇਆ ਉਪਰਿ ਹੋਇ ॥
s4.romanization:
Fareedaa khaak na nindeeai khaakoo jed na koe.
Jeevadiyaa pair tale muiaa upar hoe.
s4.english_meaning: Farid, speak not ill of dust, for nothing is as great as dust. While one lives, it lies beneath the feet; when one is dead, it rises above him.

s4.title: The Muddy Lane
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਫਰੀਦਾ ਗਲੀਐ ਚਿਕੜੁ ਦੂਰਿ ਘਰੁ ਨਾਲਿ ਪਿਆਰੇ ਨੇਹੁ
s4.why_dense: This salok teaches that the path of love is difficult, messy, and physically costly. A learner gains a classic Punjabi metaphor for spiritual effort in which ordinary village terrain becomes mystical language.
s4.cultural_signal: SGGS kirtan / ethical discourse / Sufi mehfil
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਗਲੀਐ ਚਿਕੜੁ ਦੂਰਿ ਘਰੁ ਨਾਲਿ ਪਿਆਰੇ ਨੇਹੁ ॥
ਚਲਾ ਤ ਭਿਜੈ ਕੰਬਲੀ ਰਹਾਂ ਤ ਤੁਟੈ ਨੇਹੁ ॥੨੪॥
s4.romanization:
Fareedaa galeeai chikarr door ghar naal piaare nehu.
Chalaa ta bhijai kambalee rahaan ta tuttai nehu. ||24||
s4.english_meaning: Farid says: the lane is mire, the house lies far, and with the Beloved there is love. If I go, my blanket is drenched; if I stay behind, the bond of love is broken.

s4.title: The Broken Vessel
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਫਰੀਦਾ ਭੰਨੀ ਘੜੀ ਸੁਵੰਨੀ ਟੂਟੀ ਨਾਗਰ ਲਜੁ
s4.why_dense: This salok teaches the fragility of the body through the image of a broken clay vessel. A learner gains a compact lesson in impermanence and the earthy material metaphors central to early Punjabi verse.
s4.cultural_signal: SGGS kirtan / ethical discourse
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਭੰਨੀ ਘੜੀ ਸੁਵੰਨੀ ਟੂਟੀ ਨਾਗਰ ਲਜੁ ॥
ਅਜਰਾਈਲੁ ਫਰੇਸਤਾ ਕੈ ਘਰਿ ਨਾਠੀ ਅਜੁ ॥
s4.romanization:
Fareedaa bhannee gharhee suvannee tootee naagar laj.
Ajraaeel faresataa kai ghar naathee aj.
s4.english_meaning: Farid says: the fair water-pot is broken; the city-bride’s modest pride is shattered. Today Azraeel, the angel of death, has entered the house and seized his due.

s4.title: Subtle Intelligence
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਫਰੀਦਾ ਜੇ ਤੂ ਅਕਲਿ ਲਤੀਫੁ ਕਾਲੇ ਲਿਖੁ ਨ ਲੇਖ
s4.why_dense: This salok teaches that true intelligence lies in avoiding moral stain rather than displaying cleverness. A learner gains a concise ethical maxim that still circulates as practical wisdom in Punjabi discourse.
s4.cultural_signal: SGGS kirtan / ethical discourse / widely quoted in diaspora
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਜੇ ਤੂ ਅਕਲਿ ਲਤੀਫੁ ਕਾਲੇ ਲਿਖੁ ਨ ਲੇਖ ॥
ਆਪਨੜੇ ਗਿਰੀਵਾਨ ਮਹਿ ਸਿਰੁ ਨੀਵਾਂ ਕਰਿ ਦੇਖੁ ॥
s4.romanization:
Fareedaa je too akal lateef kaale likh na lekh.
Aapanrre gireevaan meh sir neevaan kar dekh.
s4.english_meaning: Farid says: if thou possess subtle intelligence, then write not the black account of others' faults. Bow thy head and look within thine own collar-fold; behold thine own self in humility.

s4.title: Seasons Pass Beautifully
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਫਰੀਦਾ ਰੁਤੀ ਰੁਤਿ ਸੁਹਾਵੜੀ ਜਿਤੁ ਤੂੰ ਸਜਣੁ ਮਿਲਿਆ
s4.why_dense: This salok teaches that season is not merely weather but the inner condition created by union or remembrance. A learner gains access to Farid's distinctive seasonal poetics, where climate and emotion are inseparable.
s4.cultural_signal: SGGS kirtan / Sufi mehfil / literary citation
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਰੁਤੀ ਰੁਤਿ ਸੁਹਾਵੜੀ ਜਿਤੁ ਤੂੰ ਸਜਣੁ ਮਿਲਿਆ ॥
ਤਉ ਨੇਹੁ ਨ ਲਗਈ ਜੇ ਰੁਤਿ ਜਾਇ ਤ ਮਿਲਿਆ ॥
s4.romanization:
Fareedaa rutee rut suhaavarhee jit toon sajan mili-aa.
Tau nehu na lag-ee je rut jaae ta mili-aa.
s4.english_meaning: Farid says: fair is that season, that very season, wherein thou meetest the Beloved. Yet set not thy heart upon the season alone, for if the season pass, then the meeting too is lost.

s4.title: The Heron by the River
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਫਰੀਦਾ ਦਰਿਆਵੈ ਕੰਨੈ ਬਗੁਲਾ ਬੈਠਾ ਕੇਲ ਕਰੇ
s4.why_dense: This salok teaches vigilance through a nature image that appears calm but conceals appetite and calculation. A learner gains practice reading Punjabi symbolic animals, a major feature of medieval devotional expression.
s4.cultural_signal: SGGS kirtan / ethical discourse / literary citation
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਦਰਿਆਵੈ ਕੰਨੈ ਬਗੁਲਾ ਬੈਠਾ ਕੇਲ ਕਰੇ ॥
ਕੇਲ ਕਰੇਦੇ ਹੰਝੁ ਨੋ ਅਚਿੰਤੇ ਬਾਜ ਪਏ ॥੯੪॥
s4.romanization:
Fareedaa dariyaavai kannai bagulaa baithaa kel kare.
Kel karede hanjh no achinte baaj pae. ||94||
s4.english_meaning: Farid, by the river-bank the heron sits at play; while it sports, the hawk falls unawares upon the swan. Thus in heedless ease, sudden doom descends without warning.

s4.title: Worthless Deeds
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਫਰੀਦਾ ਜਿਨ੍ਹੀ ਕੰਮੀ ਨਾਹਿ ਗੁਣ ਤੇ ਕੰਮੜੇ ਵਿਸਾਰਿ
s4.why_dense: This salok teaches discernment by rejecting actions that produce no virtue or inner worth. A learner gains a compact vocabulary of moral evaluation that is central to both scriptural and everyday Punjabi usage.
s4.cultural_signal: SGGS kirtan / ethical discourse / folk proverb-like usage
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਜਿਨ੍ਹੀ ਕੰਮੀ ਨਾਹਿ ਗੁਣ ਤੇ ਕੰਮੜੇ ਵਿਸਾਰਿ ॥
ਮਤੁ ਸਰਮਿੰਦਾ ਥੀਵਹੀ ਸਾਈ ਦੇ ਦਰਬਾਰਿ ॥
s4.romanization:
Fareedaa jinhee kammee naahi gun te kammrre visaar.
Mat saramindaa theevhee saaee de darbaar.
s4.english_meaning: Farid says: Forsake those deeds wherein no virtue abides. Lest thou be put to shame in the Court of the Lord.

s4.title: The World's Applause
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਫਰੀਦਾ ਦੁਨੀ ਵਜਾਈ ਵਾਜੜੀ ਤੂੰ ਭੀ ਵਜਹਿ ਨਾਲਿ
s4.why_dense: This salok teaches suspicion toward public praise and social noise. A learner gains a sharp distinction between worldly reputation and spiritual accountability, a recurring Punjabi ethical contrast.
s4.cultural_signal: SGGS kirtan / ethical discourse / widely quoted in diaspora
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਦੁਨੀ ਵਜਾਈ ਵਾਜੜੀ ਤੂੰ ਭੀ ਵਜਹਿ ਨਾਲਿ ॥
ਸੋਈ ਜੀਉ ਨ ਵਜਦਾ ਜਿਤੁ ਵਜਹਿ ਖਸਮੈ ਨਾਲਿ ॥
s4.romanization:
Fareedaa dunee vajaaee vaajhree toon bhee vajeh naal.
Soee jeeau na vajdaa jit vajeh khasmai naal.
s4.english_meaning: Farid, the world hath struck up its instrument, and thou too soundest along with it. That life alone maketh no true music which soundeth not in accord with the Lord, the Master.

s4.title: The Boat Not Readied
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਫਰੀਦਾ ਬੇੜਾ ਬੰਨ੍ਹਿ ਨ ਸਕਿਓ ਬੰਧਨ ਕੀ ਵੇਲਾ
s4.why_dense: This salok teaches preparedness before the crossing of life becomes urgent and irreversible. A learner gains a classic river-and-boat metaphor that links Punjabi landscape with spiritual urgency.
s4.cultural_signal: SGGS kirtan / ethical discourse / Sufi mehfil
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਬੇੜਾ ਬੰਨ੍ਹਿ ਨ ਸਕਿਓ ਬੰਧਨ ਕੀ ਵੇਲਾ ॥
ਭਰਿ ਸਰਵਰੁ ਜਬ ਊਛਲੈ ਤਬ ਤਰਣੁ ਦੁਹੇਲਾ ॥
s4.romanization:
Fareedaa berraa bannh na sakio bandhan kee velaa.
Bhar sarvar jab oochhalai tab taran duhelaa.
s4.english_meaning: Farid, thou couldst not make fast the boat in the season for binding it. When the tank is filled and its waters surge high, then the crossing is grievous and hard.

s4.title: Failing Limbs
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਏਨੀ ਅਖੀ ਨੀਦ ਨ ਆਵਈ ਸਰੀਰੁ ਨ ਰਹੈ ਸਮਾਇ
s4.why_dense: This salok teaches impermanence from within the body's own decline rather than from abstract doctrine. A learner gains vocabulary for aging, frailty, and embodied reflection that appears often in Punjabi devotional literature.
s4.cultural_signal: SGGS kirtan / ethical discourse / literary citation
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਏਨੀ ਅਖੀ ਨੀਦ ਨ ਆਵਈ ਜੀਉ ਸਰੀਰੁ ਨ ਰਹੈ ॥
ਵਖੁ ਰੋਵੈ ਮਾਂ ਪਿਰੁ ਵਿਛੁੜਿਆ ਨੀਦ ਨ ਪਈਆ ਰਾਤਿ ॥
s4.romanization:
Fareedaa enee akhee need na aavee jeeo sareer na rahai.
Vakh rovai maan pir vichhurhiaa need na paeeaa raat.
s4.english_meaning: Farid says: into these eyes no sleep comes; the life within and the body can no longer remain at ease. Like a separated bride lamenting for her beloved, the mother-heart weeps, and through the night no sleep descends.

s4.title: Life Passes Sweetly
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਫਰੀਦਾ ਉਮਰ ਸੁਹਾਵੜੀ ਸੰਗਿ ਭਲੇ ਦਿਨ ਚਾਰ
s4.why_dense: This salok teaches that life's sweetness depends on companionship and vanishes quickly. A learner gains a folk-inflected expression of time and relationship that broadens Farid beyond the canonical scriptural register.
s4.cultural_signal: folk proverbs / Sufi mehfil / widely quoted in Punjabi speech
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਉਮਰ ਸੁਹਾਵੜੀ ਸੰਗਿ ਭਲੇ ਦਿਨ ਚਾਰ
ਜਿਨ੍ਹਾਂ ਲੰਮੀ ਰਾਤਿ ਕਟੀ ਸੇ ਥੋੜੇ ਦਿਨ ਵਿਸਾਰ
s4.romanization:
Fareedaa umar suhaavri sang bhale din chaar
Jinhaan lammi raat kati se thorre din visaar
s4.english_meaning: Farid says: life seems fair and lovely, and with it come but four good days. Those who have endured the long night soon forget these few brief days, for they pass away swiftly.

s4.title: White Hair Appears
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਫਰੀਦਾ ਕਾਲੀ ਜਿਨੀ ਨ ਰਾਵਿਆ ਧਉਲੀ ਰਾਵੈ ਕੋਇ
s4.why_dense: This salok teaches regret through the image of youth wasted before old age arrives. A learner gains a powerful medieval Punjabi way of speaking about time, desire, and delayed awakening.
s4.cultural_signal: folk proverbs / ethical discourse / Sufi mehfil
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਕਾਲੀ ਜਿਨੀ ਨ ਰਾਵਿਆ ਧਉਲੀ ਰਾਵੈ ਕੋਇ ।
ਕਰਿ ਸਾਈ ਸਿਉ ਪਿਰਹੜੀ ਰੰਗ ਨਵੇਲਾ ਹੋਇ ॥
s4.romanization:
Fareedaa kaalee jinee na raaviaa dhaulaee raavai koe.
Kar saaee sio pirharree rang naveelaa hoe.
s4.english_meaning: Farid says: those who loved not in the blackness of youth—will any love in the whiteness of age? Make thy bridal bond with the Lord; then the colour of love is ever-fresh.

s4.title: Bird on the Branch
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਫਰੀਦਾ ਪੰਖੀ ਬੈਠਾ ਡਾਲ ਤੇ ਜਿਉਂ ਦਿਨ ਚਾਰਾ ਜੀਉ
s4.why_dense: This salok teaches the precariousness of life through a bird resting only briefly on a branch. A learner gains a vivid nature metaphor that shows how Punjabi verse turns ordinary rural scenes into existential insight.
s4.cultural_signal: Sufi mehfil / folk recitation / literary citation
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਪੰਖੀ ਬੈਠਾ ਡਾਲ ਤੇ ਜਿਉਂ ਦਿਨ ਚਾਰਾ ਜੀਉ ।
ਸਾਹਿਬ ਅਜਬ ਖਿਡਾਇਆ ਵਿਚਿ ਵਿਛੋੜੇ ਰੋਵੈ ਜੀਉ ।
s4.romanization:
Fareedaa pankhee baithaa daal te jio(n) din chaaraa jeeo.
Saahib ajab khidaaiaa vich vichhore rovai jeeo.
s4.english_meaning: Farid says: the bird sits upon the branch, and the soul abides but for a few days. The Lord has staged a wondrous play; amid separation, the living soul weeps.

s4.title: Tree in Autumn
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਫਰੀਦਾ ਰੁਖੁ ਸੁਕਾਇਆ ਪਤ ਝੜੇ ਵੇਖਿ ਮਨੁ ਡਰਾਇ
s4.why_dense: This salok teaches mortality through seasonal decay rather than direct preaching. A learner gains seasonal vocabulary and sees how autumnal imagery carries emotional and spiritual meaning in Punjabi poetics.
s4.cultural_signal: Sufi mehfil / folk recitation
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਰੁਖੁ ਸੁਕਾਇਆ ਪਤ ਝੜੇ ਵੇਖਿ ਮਨੁ ਡਰਾਇ ।
ਐਸਾ ਜੀਉ ਨ ਜਾਣੀਐ ਜਿਉਂ ਰੁਖੁ ਪਤ ਝੜਿ ਜਾਇ ।
s4.romanization:
Fareedaa rukh sukaaiaa pat jharre vekh man daraai.
Aisaa jeeo na jaaneeai jio(n) rukh pat jhar jai.
s4.english_meaning: Farid says: seeing the tree withered and its leaves fallen, the mind is seized with dread. Deem not the soul to be otherwise, for it too is as a tree whose leaves fall away and perish.

s4.title: The Body Grows Weak
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਫਰੀਦਾ ਤਨੁ ਸੁਕਾ ਪਿੰਜਰੁ ਥੀਆ ਅਖੀ ਦੇਖਣ ਹਾਰ
s4.why_dense: This salok teaches the nearness of death through the body's visible wasting. A learner gains direct access to the stark physical realism that made Farid memorable in oral and folk transmission.
s4.cultural_signal: Sufi mehfil / ethical discourse / folk recitation
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਤਨੁ ਸੁਕਾ ਪਿੰਜਰੁ ਥੀਆ ਅਖੀ ਦੇਖਣ ਹਾਰ ।
ਅਜੈ ਸੁ ਰਬ ਨ ਬਾਹੁੜਿਓ ਦੇਖੁ ਬੰਦੇ ਕੇ ਭਾਗ ॥
s4.romanization:
Fareedaa tan sukaa pinjar theeaa akhee dekhan haar.
Ajai su Rabb na baahurhio dekh bande ke bhaag.
s4.english_meaning: Farid says: the body has withered and become a cage of bones; the eyes themselves grow weary of beholding it. Yet still the Lord has not turned back toward me—behold, such is the portion allotted to His servant.

s4.title: Do Not Delay Love
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਫਰੀਦਾ ਅਜੁ ਨ ਰਾਵੀਐ ਕਲਿ ਕਉਣੁ ਜਾਣੈ ਹੋਇ
s4.why_dense: This salok teaches urgency by refusing to postpone devotion or love until tomorrow. A learner gains a proverb-like structure that is easy to remember and reveals the immediacy prized in Sufi-inflected Punjabi verse.
s4.cultural_signal: folk proverbs / Sufi mehfil / ethical discourse
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਅਜੁ ਨ ਰਾਵੀਐ ਕਲਿ ਕਉਣੁ ਜਾਣੈ ਹੋਇ ।
ਅਜੁ ਹੀ ਰਾਵਹੁ ਪ੍ਰੀਤਮਾ ਟੁਟੈ ਸਾਹੁ ਨ ਕੋਇ ।
s4.romanization:
Fareedaa aj na raaveeai kal kaun jaanai hoi.
Aj hee raavahu preetamaa tuttai saahu na koi.
s4.english_meaning: Farid says: delay not union till tomorrow, for who knows what the morrow shall become. Embrace the Beloved this very day, for no one knows at what breath the life-thread may break.

s4.title: Rain and Separation
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਫਰੀਦਾ ਬਰਸਨ ਮੇਘ ਅਸਾੜ ਦੇ ਵਿਛੁੜਿਆ ਮਨੁ ਰੋਇ
s4.why_dense: This salok teaches how monsoon intensifies longing rather than relieving it. A learner gains emotional weather-language that is central to Punjabi birha traditions in both devotional and folk settings.
s4.cultural_signal: Sufi mehfil / folk singing / literary citation
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਬਰਸਨ ਮੇਘ ਅਸਾੜ ਦੇ ਵਿਛੁੜਿਆ ਮਨੁ ਰੋਇ ।
ਜਿਨ੍ਹਾ ਸਜਣ ਵਿਛੁੜੇ ਘਣੇ ਸੇ ਕਿਉਂ ਜੀਵਣਿ ਹੋਇ ॥
s4.romanization:
Fareedaa barsan megh asaarr de vichhurhiaa man roee.
Jinhaa sajjan vichhurhe ghane se kio(n) jeevan hoi.
s4.english_meaning: Farid says: in the rains of Asaarr, the severed heart weeps. They whose beloved companions are deeply parted from them—how indeed can life continue for such as these?

s4.title: Speak Gently
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਫਰੀਦਾ ਮਿਠੁ ਬੋਲਣੁ ਨੀਵਾਂ ਚਲਣੁ ਏਹੁ ਸਚਾ ਸਿੰਗਾਰ
s4.why_dense: This salok teaches that humility and gentle speech are the true ornaments of a person. A learner gains highly usable ethical Punjabi that connects medieval wisdom with everyday conversational values.
s4.cultural_signal: folk proverbs / ethical discourse / widely quoted in Punjabi speech
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਮਿਠੁ ਬੋਲਣੁ ਨੀਵਾਂ ਚਲਣੁ ਏਹੁ ਸਚਾ ਸਿੰਗਾਰੁ ।
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਗਰਬੁ ਨ ਕੀਜਈ ਰਾਜੁ ਜੋਬਨੁ ਧਨੁ ਮਾਲੁ ॥
s4.romanization:
Fareedaa mith bolann neevaan chalann eh sachaa singaar.
Fareedaa garab na keejai raaj joban dhan maal.
s4.english_meaning: Farid says: to speak sweetly and to walk in humility—this is the true adornment. Farid says: be not proud of kingdom, youth, wealth, or possessions, for such splendour does not abide.

s4.title: The Nest Empties
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਫਰੀਦਾ ਘੋਸਲਾ ਖਾਲੀ ਹੋਇਆ ਪੰਖੀ ਉਡਿ ਗਏ ਦੂਰ
s4.why_dense: This salok teaches loss and transience through the image of birds leaving an empty nest. A learner gains a simple but resonant rural metaphor that helps decode Punjabi emotional symbolism.
s4.cultural_signal: folk recitation / Sufi mehfil
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਘੋਸਲਾ ਖਾਲੀ ਹੋਇਆ ਪੰਖੀ ਉਡਿ ਗਏ ਦੂਰਿ ।
ਇਹੁ ਤਨੁ ਡਾਢਾ ਢਹਿ ਪਇਆ ਜਿਉਂ ਸੁੰਞਾ ਪਿਆ ਮਕੂਰਿ ।
s4.romanization:
Fareedaa ghosla khaalee hoiaa pankhee udd gaye doori.
Ihu tanu daaddhaa dhahi paiaa jioon soonjhaa pia makoori.
s4.english_meaning: Farid says: the nest is emptied; the birds have flown far away. This body, once stout, has fallen in ruin, like a forsaken and desolate dwelling left void.

s4.title: Look Within
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਫਰੀਦਾ ਜੇ ਤੂੰ ਆਪੁ ਨ ਵੇਖਸੀ ਦੂਜਾ ਦੋਸੁ ਨ ਦੇਹਿ
s4.why_dense: This salok teaches self-accountability before blaming others. A learner gains a direct aphoristic style common in the extended Farid tradition and useful for understanding Punjabi moral speech.
s4.cultural_signal: folk proverbs / ethical discourse / Sufi mehfil
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਜੇ ਤੂੰ ਆਪੁ ਨ ਵੇਖਸੀ ਦੂਜਾ ਦੋਸੁ ਨ ਦੇਹਿ ।
ਆਪਣੜੇ ਗਿਰਿਵਾਨ ਮਹਿ ਸਿਰੁ ਨੀਵਾਂ ਕਰਿ ਦੇਖੁ ॥
s4.romanization:
Fareedaa je toon aap na vekhsee doojaa dos na deh.
Aapnre girivaan meh sir neevaan kar dekh.
s4.english_meaning: Farid says: if thou look not upon thine own self, then lay not blame upon another. Bow thy head and peer within thine own collar-fold; there behold what is in thee.

s4.title: Night of Longing
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਫਰੀਦਾ ਲੰਮੀ ਲੰਮੀ ਰਾਤ ਹੈ ਸਜਣ ਬਾਝੁ ਨ ਸੌਂ
s4.why_dense: This salok teaches how separation stretches time and turns night into a spiritual ordeal. A learner gains a classic birha mood in a more folk and intimate register than the SGGS selections.
s4.cultural_signal: Sufi mehfil / folk singing / diaspora literary circles
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫਰੀਦਾ ਲੰਮੀ ਲੰਮੀ ਰਾਤ ਹੈ ਸਜਣ ਬਾਝੁ ਨ ਸੌਂ ।
ਜੇ ਜਾਣਾ ਤਿਲ ਥੋੜੜੇ ਸੰਮਲਿ ਬੁੱਕ ਭਰੀਂ ॥
s4.romanization:
Fareedaa lammee lammee raat hai sajjan baajh na saun.
Je jaanaa til thorhde, sammal bukk bhareen.
s4.english_meaning: Farid says: long, long is the night; without the Beloved, I do not sleep. If I had known the sesame-seeds were so few, I would have gathered them with care and filled my fold—an image of husbanding the scant moments of union and grace.

s4.title: Opening Invocation
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਸ਼ਾਹ ਮੁਹੰਮਦਾ ਇਕ ਸਰਕਾਰ ਬਾਝੋਂ ਫੌਜਾਂ ਜਿੱਤ ਨ ਆਵਦੀਆਂ ਨੇ
s4.why_dense: This band teaches the poem's governing political idea that armies fail without rightful command and order. A learner gains entry into the diction of Punjabi historical lament and the formal opening style of the var tradition.
s4.cultural_signal: historical discourse / Punjabi literary study / widely quoted in diaspora
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਸ਼ਾਹ ਮੁਹੰਮਦਾ ਇਕ ਸਰਕਾਰ ਬਾਝੋਂ ਫੌਜਾਂ ਜਿੱਤ ਨ ਆਵਦੀਆਂ ਨੇ,
ਜਿਨ੍ਹਾਂ ਦੇਸ਼ ਦੀ ਖ਼ਾਤਰ ਜੰਗ ਕੀਤੀ ਉਨ੍ਹਾਂ ਦੀਆਂ ਨਾਮ ਧਿਆਵਦੀਆਂ ਨੇ,
ਜਿਨ੍ਹਾਂ ਰਾਜਪੁਤ੍ਰਾਂ ਦੇ ਸਿਰ ਉੱਤੇ ਤਾਜ ਸਨ ਓਹ ਵੀ ਧਰਤੀ ਤੇ ਆਵਦੀਆਂ ਨੇ,
ਅੰਤ ਵੇਲੇ ਸਭ ਮਿੱਟੀ ਹੋ ਜਾਂਦੇ ਦੁਨੀਆ ਝੂਠੀਆਂ ਛਾਵਦੀਆਂ ਨੇ।
s4.romanization:
Shaah Muhammadā ik sarkaar baajhon faujaan jitt na aavdian ne,
Jinhaan desh di khaatar jang keeti unhaan diaan naam dhiaavdian ne,
Jinhaan raajputraan de sir utte taaj san oh vee dhartee te aavdian ne,
Ant vele sabb mittee ho jaande duniyaa jhoothiaan chhaavdian ne.
s4.english_meaning: Shah Muhammad says: without one sovereign command, armies do not come to victory. Those who fought for the sake of the land are remembered by name; yet even the princes who wore crowns upon their heads come at last down to the dust, for the world’s shadows are false and passing.

s4.title: Crossing the Sutlej
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਸਤਲੁਜ ਪਾਰ ਕਰ ਗਈ ਫੌਜ ਸਿੰਘਾਂ ਦੀ ਸ਼ਾਨ ਨਿਰਾਲੀ ਸੀ
s4.why_dense: This band teaches martial movement and collective pride at the threshold of war. A learner gains historical vocabulary tied to geography, army, and sovereignty in nineteenth-century Punjabi narrative verse.
s4.cultural_signal: historical discourse / Punjabi literary study
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਸਤਲੁਜ ਪਾਰ ਕਰ ਗਈ ਫੌਜ ਸਿੰਘਾਂ ਦੀ ਸ਼ਾਨ ਨਿਰਾਲੀ ਸੀ
ਨਗਾਰੇ ਮਾਰਦੇ ਚੜ੍ਹੇ ਸਰਦਾਰਾਂ, ਧਾਕ ਉਨ੍ਹਾਂ ਦੀ ਭਾਰੀ ਸੀ
ਘੋੜੇ, ਤੋਪਾਂ, ਝੰਡੇ, ਨਿਸ਼ਾਨ, ਸਭ ਸਾਜ਼ ਸਮੇਤ ਤਿਆਰੀ ਸੀ
ਸ਼ਾਹ ਮੁਹੰਮਦਾ ਆਖੇ ਵੇਖੋ, ਖਾਲਸੇ ਦੀ ਫਤਹਿ ਕਰਾਰੀ ਸੀ
s4.romanization:
Satluj paar kar gayi fauj Singhhan di shaan niraali si
Nagaare maarde charhe sardaraan, dhaak unhaan di bhaari si
Ghode, topaan, jhande, nishaan, sabh saaz samet tiaari si
Shah Muhammad aakhe vekho, Khalse di fateh karaari si
s4.english_meaning: The army of the Singhs crossed the Sutlej, and their splendour was singular. The chiefs advanced with kettledrums resounding; their awe lay heavy upon all, with horses, cannon, standards, and ensigns arrayed in full preparation. Shah Muhammad says: behold, the Khalsa’s victory was sharp and decisive.

s4.title: Battle of Ferozeshah
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਫਿਰੋਜ਼ਸ਼ਾਹ ਦੇ ਮੈਦਾਨ ਅੰਦਰ ਘਮਸਾਨ ਦਾ ਰੰਗ ਬਣਾਇਆ ਸੀ
s4.why_dense: This band teaches how Shah Muhammad compresses battle, spectacle, and terror into forceful narrative rhythm. A learner gains exposure to martial Punjabi and the sonic energy of war description in var form.
s4.cultural_signal: historical discourse / Punjabi literary study / recitation
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫਿਰੋਜ਼ਸ਼ਾਹ ਦੇ ਮੈਦਾਨ ਅੰਦਰ ਘਮਸਾਨ ਦਾ ਰੰਗ ਬਣਾਇਆ ਸੀ,
ਸ਼ਾਹ ਮੁਹੰਮਦਾ ਇੱਕ ਸਰਕਾਰ ਬਾਝੋਂ ਫੌਜਾਂ ਜਿੱਤ ਕੇ ਅੰਤ ਨੂੰ ਹਾਰੀਆਂ ਨੇ।
s4.romanization:
Firozshaah de maidaan andar ghamsaan da rang banaaiaa see,
Shaah Muhammad aaik sarkaar baajhon faujaan jitt ke ant noo haariyaan ne.
s4.english_meaning: In the field of Ferozeshah there was raised the dread spectacle of close and furious battle. Shah Muhammad says: without a sovereign command, armies may win in onset, yet in the end they are undone.

s4.title: Treachery of Commanders
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਲਾਲ ਸਿੰਘ ਤੇ ਤੇਜ ਸਿੰਘ ਨੇ ਰਾਜ ਦਾ ਭੇਦ ਗਵਾਇਆ ਸੀ
s4.why_dense: This band teaches that political betrayal, not only battlefield defeat, caused Punjab's collapse. A learner gains a crucial interpretive frame for the Jangnama and a vocabulary of accusation central to Punjabi historical memory.
s4.cultural_signal: historical discourse / widely quoted in Punjabi political commentary
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਲਾਲ ਸਿੰਘ ਤੇ ਤੇਜ ਸਿੰਘ ਨੇ ਰਾਜ ਦਾ ਭੇਦ ਗਵਾਇਆ ਸੀ,
ਲਿਖ ਕੇ ਖ਼ਤ ਫਿਰੰਗੀਆਂ ਨੂੰ ਨਿੱਤ ਭੇਤ ਦਾ ਭੇਤ ਦਸਾਇਆ ਸੀ।
ਫੌਜਾਂ ਵਿਚ ਦਰਾਰ ਪਵਾ ਕੇ ਕੰਮ ਸਭਨਾਂ ਦਾ ਲੁਟਾਇਆ ਸੀ,
ਸ਼ਾਹ ਮੁਹੰਮਦਾ ਏਹਨਾਂ ਮੀਰ ਜਾਫ਼ਰਾਂ ਪੰਥ ਦਾ ਨਾਸ ਕਰਾਇਆ ਸੀ।
s4.romanization:
Laal Singh te Tej Singh ne raaj da bhed gavaayaa see,
likh ke khat phirangiaan noon nit bhet da bhet dasaayaa see.
Faujaan vich daraar pavaa ke kamm sabhnaan da lutaayaa see,
Shaah Muhammad aaehnaan Meer Jaafaraan panth da naas karaayaa see.
s4.english_meaning: Lal Singh and Tej Singh let slip the secret of the kingdom; writing letters to the foreigners, they daily disclosed the innermost counsels. Sowing division within the armies, they brought all enterprise to ruin. Shah Muhammad says: these Mir Jafars caused the destruction of the Panth.

s4.title: After Chilianwala
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਚਿਲੀਆਂਵਾਲੇ ਦੇ ਰਣ ਵਿਚ ਮਾਵਾਂ ਨੇ ਪੁੱਤ ਗਵਾਏ ਸੀ
s4.why_dense: This band teaches the human cost of war by shifting from armies to grieving households. A learner gains the emotional register that makes the Jangnama more than chronicle, turning history into communal mourning.
s4.cultural_signal: historical discourse / elegiac recitation / diaspora remembrance
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਚਿਲੀਆਂਵਾਲੇ ਦੇ ਰਣ ਵਿਚ ਮਾਵਾਂ ਨੇ ਪੁੱਤ ਗਵਾਏ ਸੀ,
ਸ਼ਾਹ ਮੁਹੰਮਦਾ ਸਿੰਘਾਂ ਨੇ ਜੰਗ ਵਿਚ ਕਮਾਲ ਕੀਤੀ।
ਘਰ ਘਰ ਪਈਆਂ ਵਿਲਾਪ ਦੀਆਂ ਧੁਨੀਆਂ, ਰੋਣ ਅੱਖੀਆਂ ਲਾਲ ਕੀਤੀ,
ਕਈਆਂ ਭੈਣਾਂ ਦੇ ਵੀਰ ਨਾ ਮੁੜੇ, ਕਈਆਂ ਰੰਡੀਆਂ ਹਾਲ ਕੀਤੀ।
s4.romanization:
Chiliyaanwaale de ran vich maavaan ne putt gavaae see,
Shaah Muhammadaa singhaan ne jang vich kamaal keetee.
Ghar ghar paeeaan vilaap deeaan dhuneeaan, ron akkhiaan laal keetee,
Kaeeaan bhainaan de veer naa murre, kaeeaan randeeaan haal keetee.
s4.english_meaning: In the battle of Chilianwala, mothers lost their sons; Shah Muhammad says, the Singhs wrought a terrible marvel in war. From house to house arose the sounds of lamentation, and weeping made the eyes red; many sisters saw their brothers return no more, and many women were left widowed and undone.

s4.title: Punjab in Ruin
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਪੰਜਾਬ ਦੀ ਹਾਲਤ ਵੇਖ ਕੇ ਦਿਲ ਅੰਦਰ ਖੂਨ ਰੁਲਦਾ ਏ
s4.why_dense: This band teaches collective loss as a civilizational wound rather than a single military event. A learner gains the emotional-political language through which Punjabi memory narrates the fall of the Sikh kingdom.
s4.cultural_signal: historical discourse / Punjabi literary study / diaspora remembrance
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਪੰਜਾਬ ਦੀ ਹਾਲਤ ਵੇਖ ਕੇ ਦਿਲ ਅੰਦਰ ਖੂਨ ਰੁਲਦਾ ਏ,
ਸ਼ਾਹ ਮੁਹੰਮਦਾ ਅੱਖੀਂ ਵੇਖੀਏ, ਭੈੜਾ ਵਕਤ ਨਾ ਭੁੱਲਦਾ ਏ।
ਜਿਹਨਾਂ ਰਾਜ ਕਮਾਇਆ ਸੀ, ਓਹਨਾਂ ਦਾ ਨਾਮ ਨਿਸ਼ਾਨ ਨਾ ਲੱਭਦਾ ਏ,
ਧੀਆਂ ਪੁੱਤ ਰੁਲ ਗਏ ਵਤਨਾਂ ਵਿਚ, ਕੋਈ ਕਿਸੇ ਦਾ ਹਾਲ ਨਾ ਪੁੱਛਦਾ ਏ।
s4.romanization:
Panjaab dee haalat vekh ke dil andar khoon ruldaa ae,
Shaah Muhammad aaNkheen vekheeai, bhairaa vakt naa bhulldaa ae.
JihnaaN raaj kamaaiaa see, ohnaaN daa naam nishaan naa labhdaa ae,
DheeaaN putt rul gaye vatnaaN vich, koee kise daa haal naa puchhdaa ae.
s4.english_meaning: Beholding the condition of Punjab, the heart weeps blood within. Shah Muhammad says: what the eyes have seen is such an evil time that it cannot be forgotten; those who once held dominion have left no trace of name or mark, and daughters and sons wander ruined in their own homeland, with none left to ask after another's state.

s4.title: Closing Lament
s4.first_line_gurmukhi: ਸ਼ਾਹ ਮੁਹੰਮਦਾ ਇਕ ਸਰਕਾਰ ਬਾਝੋਂ ਮੁਲਕ ਨਹੀਂ ਰਹਿੰਦੇ
s4.why_dense: This band teaches the poem's final lesson that political disunity destroys a land from within. A learner gains one of the most cited conclusions in Punjabi historical verse and a durable phrase for discussing sovereignty and loss.
s4.cultural_signal: widely quoted in diaspora / historical discourse / Punjabi literary study
s4.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਸ਼ਾਹ ਮੁਹੰਮਦਾ ਇਕ ਸਰਕਾਰ ਬਾਝੋਂ ਮੁਲਕ ਨਹੀਂ ਰਹਿੰਦੇ,
ਜਿਹੜੀਆਂ ਰਾਜਪੁਤਰੀਆਂ ਸਨ, ਓਹ ਪਾਣੀ ਭਰਦੀਆਂ ਨੇ।
ਜਿਹੜੀਆਂ ਘੋੜਿਆਂ ਤੇ ਚੜ੍ਹਦੀਆਂ ਸਨ, ਓਹ ਪੈਦਲ ਤੁਰਦੀਆਂ ਨੇ,
ਜਿਹੜੀਆਂ ਸੋਨੇ ਦੇ ਪਲੰਗਾਂ ਤੇ ਸੌਂਦੀਆਂ ਸਨ, ਓਹ ਧਰਤੀ ਤੇ ਸੁੱਤੀਆਂ ਨੇ।
s4.romanization:
Shaah Muhammadā ik sarkaar baajhon mulak nahin rahinde,
jihriyaan raajputriyaan san, oh paanee bhardiyaan ne.
jihriyaan ghoriyaan te charhdiyan san, oh paidal turdiyaan ne,
jihriyaan sone de palangaan te saundiyaan san, oh dhartee te suttiyaan ne.
s4.english_meaning: Shah Muhammad says: without a sovereign power, realms do not endure. Those who were princesses now draw water; those who rode upon horses now go on foot; those who slept upon beds of gold now lie sleeping on the bare earth.

s5.title: ਅੱਜ ਆਖਾਂ ਵਾਰਸ ਸ਼ਾਹ ਨੂੰ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਅੱਜ ਆਖਾਂ ਵਾਰਸ ਸ਼ਾਹ ਨੂੰ, ਕਿੱਥੋਂ ਕਬਰਾਂ ਵਿਚੋਂ ਬੋਲ
s5.why_important: This is the defining Punjabi poem of Partition and one of the most culturally recognized poems in the language. A heritage learner gains a key reference for Punjabi collective grief and a phrase still invoked whenever Punjab faces tragedy.
s5.phrases_in_general_usage: ਅੱਜ ਆਖਾਂ ਵਾਰਸ ਸ਼ਾਹ ਨੂੰ; ਕਿੱਥੋਂ ਕਬਰਾਂ ਵਿਚੋਂ ਬੋਲ
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi:
ਅੱਜ ਆਖਾਂ ਵਾਰਸ ਸ਼ਾਹ ਨੂੰ, ਕਿੱਥੋਂ ਕਬਰਾਂ ਵਿਚੋਂ ਬੋਲ
ਤੇ ਅੱਜ ਕਿਤਾਬੇ ਇਸ਼ਕ ਦਾ, ਕੋਈ ਅਗਲਾ ਵਰਕਾ ਫੋਲ
ਇੱਕ ਰੋਈ ਸੀ ਧੀ ਪੰਜਾਬ ਦੀ, ਤੂੰ ਲਿਖ ਲਿਖ ਮਾਰੇ ਵੈਣ
ਅੱਜ ਲੱਖਾਂ ਧੀਆਂ ਰੋਂਦੀਆਂ, ਤੈਨੂੰ ਵਾਰਸ ਸ਼ਾਹ ਨੂੰ ਕਹਿਣ
s5.romanization:
Ajj aakhaan Waris Shah nu, kitthon kabraan vichon bol
Te ajj kitaab-e-ishq da, koi agla varka phol
Ikk roi si dhi Punjab di, toon likh likh maare vain
Ajj lakhhan dhiyan rondiyan, tainu Waris Shah nu kahin
s5.cultural_summary: Amrita Pritam’s nazm is the most iconic Punjabi poetic response to the Partition of 1947, transforming the legendary voice of Waris Shah into a witness for modern collective grief. It matters in the Punjabi canon because it links classical qissa tradition with twentieth-century trauma, especially foregrounding women’s suffering as the moral center of history. Heritage learners encounter it whenever Punjab’s pain is discussed—Partition remembrance, cross-border Punjabi identity, feminist readings of violence, and later moments of communal tragedy.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage:
ਅੱਜ ਆਖਾਂ ਵਾਰਸ ਸ਼ਾਹ ਨੂੰ — invoked as a shorthand for calling on Punjab’s conscience in times of catastrophe or mourning.
ਕਿੱਥੋਂ ਕਬਰਾਂ ਵਿਚੋਂ ਬੋਲ — used rhetorically to summon a dead moral witness or ancestral voice.
ਇੱਕ ਰੋਈ ਸੀ ਧੀ ਪੰਜਾਬ ਦੀ / ਅੱਜ ਲੱਖਾਂ ਧੀਆਂ ਰੋਂਦੀਆਂ — cited in discussions of Partition, gendered violence, and the suffering of Punjabi women.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the stated expiry, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2065.

s5.title: ਕਾਗਜ਼ ਤੇ ਕੈਨਵਸ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਕਾਗਜ਼ ਤੇ ਕੈਨਵਸ ਦੇ ਵਿਚਕਾਰ ਮੈਂ ਆਪਣਾ ਦਿਲ ਰੱਖ ਦਿੱਤਾ
s5.why_important: This later Amrita Pritam poem is central to understanding her mature voice, where love, memory, and artistic creation merge. A heritage learner gains vocabulary for modern Punjabi literary self-expression and the recurring metaphor of life becoming art.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi: ਕਾਗਜ਼ ਤੇ ਕੈਨਵਸ ਦੇ ਵਿਚਕਾਰ ਮੈਂ ਆਪਣਾ ਦਿਲ ਰੱਖ ਦਿੱਤਾ
s5.romanization: kāgaz te kainvas de vichkār maiṅ āpṇā dil rakh dittā
s5.cultural_summary: ਇਹ ਅਮ੍ਰਿਤਾ ਪ੍ਰੀਤਮ ਦੀ ਪੱਕੀ, ਅੰਦਰਮੁਖੀ ਆਵਾਜ਼ ਦਾ ਮਹੱਤਵਪੂਰਨ ਨਮੂਨਾ ਮੰਨਿਆ ਜਾਂਦਾ ਹੈ, ਜਿੱਥੇ ਇਸ਼ਕ, ਯਾਦ ਅਤੇ ਰਚਨਾ ਇਕੋ ਅਨੁਭਵ ਵਿੱਚ ਗੁੰਥ ਜਾਂਦੇ ਹਨ। ਪੰਜਾਬੀ ਸਾਹਿਤ ਵਿੱਚ ਇਹ ਕਵਿਤਾ ਇਸ ਗੱਲ ਲਈ ਯਾਦ ਕੀਤੀ ਜਾਂਦੀ ਹੈ ਕਿ ਜੀਵਨ ਨੂੰ ਕਲਾ ਵਿੱਚ ਬਦਲਣ ਦੀ ਪ੍ਰਕਿਰਿਆ ਕਿਵੇਂ ਨਿੱਜੀ ਸਵੀਕਾਰੋਕਤੀ ਅਤੇ ਇਸਤ੍ਰੀ-ਸਵਰ ਨਾਲ ਜੁੜਦੀ ਹੈ; ਇਸ ਦਾ ਹਵਾਲਾ ਅਕਸਰ ਅਮ੍ਰਿਤਾ ਪ੍ਰੀਤਮ ਦੀ ਪਿੱਛਲੀ ਕਵਿਤਾ, ਆਤਮ-ਅਭਿਵੈਕਤੀ, ਅਤੇ ਲੇਖਕ-ਕਲਾਕਾਰ ਦੀ ਸੰਵੇਦਨਾ ਬਾਰੇ ਚਰਚਾਵਾਂ ਵਿੱਚ ਆਉਂਦਾ ਹੈ।
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: ਕਾਗਜ਼ ਤੇ ਕੈਨਵਸ — ਲਿਖਤ, ਚਿੱਤਰਕਲਾ, ਜਾਂ ਕਿਸੇ ਡੂੰਘੇ ਨਿੱਜੀ ਅਨੁਭਵ ਨੂੰ ਕਲਾ ਵਿੱਚ ਢਾਲਣ ਲਈ ਰੂਪਕ ਵਜੋਂ ਵਰਤਿਆ ਜਾਂਦਾ ਹੈ; ਅਮ੍ਰਿਤਾ ਪ੍ਰੀਤਮ ਦੀ ਰਚਨਾਤਮਕ ਦੁਨੀਆ ਦਾ ਸੰਕੇਤਕ ਸਿਰਲੇਖ ਵੀ ਬਣ ਗਿਆ ਹੈ।
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on Amrita Pritam's 2005 death, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2066 (often cited approximately as 2065–2066 depending on jurisdictional calculation).
