s5.title: ਰੂਹ ਦਾ ਰਿਸ਼ਤਾ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਇਹ ਰੂਹ ਦਾ ਰਿਸ਼ਤਾ ਹੈ, ਜਿਸ ਨੂੰ ਕੋਈ ਨਾਂ ਨਹੀਂ
s5.why_important: The poem is widely remembered for expressing love as a bond beyond social naming or possession. A heritage learner gains one of the most recognizable Punjabi idioms of spiritual intimacy in modern literary culture.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi: ਇਹ ਰੂਹ ਦਾ ਰਿਸ਼ਤਾ ਹੈ, ਜਿਸ ਨੂੰ ਕੋਈ ਨਾਂ ਨਹੀਂ
s5.romanization: ih rūh dā rishtā hai, jis nū̃ koī nā̃ nahī̃
s5.cultural_summary: Amrita Pritam’s lyric is remembered as a defining modern Punjabi articulation of love as an inward, spiritual bond that exceeds social labels, kinship categories, and possession. In the Punjabi literary canon it matters because it distills Pritam’s intimate, feminist, and emotionally daring voice into a phrase that readers immediately recognize. Heritage learners encounter it often in discussions of modern love poetry, women’s writing, and conversations about relationships that are felt deeply but resist conventional naming.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: ਰੂਹ ਦਾ ਰਿਸ਼ਤਾ — used in everyday speech, literary discussion, and popular culture to describe a profound soul-level connection beyond formal social definition; often invoked for intense friendship, love, or remembered attachment.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the stated expiry, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2065.

s5.title: ਇੱਕ ਸੀ ਅਨੀਤਾ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਇੱਕ ਸੀ ਅਨੀਤਾ, ਤੇ ਇੱਕ ਉਸਦੀ ਚੁੱਪ ਸੀ
s5.why_important: This poem is often read as a landmark of Amrita Pritam's feminist and autobiographical mode, centering a woman's interior life rather than social judgment. A heritage learner gains insight into modern Punjabi women's writing and how personal narrative became literary resistance.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi: ਇੱਕ ਸੀ ਅਨੀਤਾ, ਤੇ ਇੱਕ ਉਸਦੀ ਚੁੱਪ ਸੀ
s5.romanization: Ikk sī Anītā, te ikk usdī chupp sī
s5.cultural_summary: ਅਮ੍ਰਿਤਾ ਪ੍ਰੀਤਮ ਦੀ ਇਹ ਨਜ਼ਮ ਆਧੁਨਿਕ ਪੰਜਾਬੀ ਕਵਿਤਾ ਵਿੱਚ ਔਰਤ ਦੇ ਅੰਦਰੂਨੀ ਜੀਵਨ, ਚੁੱਪ, ਯਾਦ ਅਤੇ ਅਸਮੀਤਾ ਨੂੰ ਕੇਂਦਰ ਵਿੱਚ ਲਿਆਉਣ ਵਾਲੇ ਮਹੱਤਵਪੂਰਨ ਮੋੜ ਵਜੋਂ ਪੜ੍ਹੀ ਜਾਂਦੀ ਹੈ। ਇਹ ਰਚਨਾ ਇਸ ਲਈ ਯਾਦ ਰੱਖੀ ਜਾਂਦੀ ਹੈ ਕਿ ਇਸ ਵਿੱਚ ਔਰਤ ਨੂੰ ਸਮਾਜਕ ਫ਼ੈਸਲੇ ਦੀ ਵਸਤੂ ਨਹੀਂ, ਸਗੋਂ ਆਪਣੇ ਅਨੁਭਵ ਦੀ ਸਾਕਸ਼ੀ ਬਣਾਇਆ ਗਿਆ ਹੈ; ਇਸ ਕਰਕੇ ਇਹ ਪੰਜਾਬੀ ਨਾਰੀਵਾਦੀ ਪਾਠ, ਆਤਮਕਥਾਤਮਕ ਲਹਿਜ਼ੇ, ਅਤੇ ਔਰਤ ਦੀ ਚੁੱਪ ਨੂੰ ਭਾਸ਼ਾ ਦੇਣ ਵਾਲੀ ਚਰਚਾ ਵਿੱਚ ਅਕਸਰ ਉਧਰਿਤ ਹੁੰਦੀ ਹੈ.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: «ਇੱਕ ਸੀ ਅਨੀਤਾ» — ਕਿਸੇ ਔਰਤ-ਕੇਂਦਰਿਤ, ਅੰਦਰੂਨੀ ਦੁੱਖ ਜਾਂ ਚੁੱਪ ਨਾਲ ਭਰੀ ਕਹਾਣੀ ਲਈ ਸੰਕੇਤਕ ਤੌਰ ਤੇ ਵਰਤਿਆ ਜਾਂਦਾ ਹੈ, ਖ਼ਾਸ ਕਰਕੇ ਸਾਹਿਤਕ ਜਾਂ ਨਾਰੀਵਾਦੀ ਗੱਲਬਾਤ ਵਿੱਚ। «ਉਸਦੀ ਚੁੱਪ» — ਔਰਤ ਦੀ ਅਣਕਹੀ ਪੀੜ, ਦਬੀ ਹੋਈ ਆਵਾਜ਼ ਜਾਂ ਸਮਾਜਕ ਖਾਮੋਸ਼ੀ ਲਈ ਹਵਾਲੇ ਵਜੋਂ ਵਰਤੀ ਜਾਂਦੀ ਹੈ.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the provided expiry, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2065.

s5.title: ਪ੍ਰੀਤਮ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਪ੍ਰੀਤਮ, ਅੱਜ ਮੇਰੇ ਕੋਲ ਬੈਠ
s5.why_important: This lyric represents Amrita Pritam's intimate love voice and is remembered as part of the emotional world that made her widely beloved. A heritage learner gains a feel for modern Punjabi address, tenderness, and the literary use of the word preetam in love discourse.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi:
ਪ੍ਰੀਤਮ, ਅੱਜ ਮੇਰੇ ਕੋਲ ਬੈਠ
ਇੱਕ ਗੱਲ ਕਰੀਏ
ਕੱਲ੍ਹ ਦਾ ਭਰੋਸਾ ਕਿਹੜਾ
s5.romanization:
Prītam, ajj mere kol baiṭh
ikk gall karīe
kallh dā bharosā kihṛā
s5.cultural_summary: This गीत is remembered for Amrita Pritam’s intimate, direct love voice: simple address, emotional immediacy, and a modern Punjabi idiom of tenderness rather than ornate convention. In the Punjabi literary canon it helps define her popular lyrical persona, and it is often invoked in discussions of love poetry, women’s interiority, and the soft, conversational register that made her widely cherished by readers.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage:
ਪ੍ਰੀਤਮ — used as an affectionate poetic address for a beloved, and also cited metonymically to evoke Amrita Pritam’s intimate love-lyric world.
ਕੱਲ੍ਹ ਦਾ ਭਰੋਸਾ ਕਿਹੜਾ — quoted in everyday speech and literary discussion to express life’s uncertainty and the urgency of speaking from the heart now.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the stated term, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2065.

s5.title: ਸੁਨੇਹੜੇ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਕਿਸੇ ਨੇ ਮੇਰੇ ਵਾਸਤੇ ਸੁਨੇਹੜੇ ਲਿਖੇ ਸਨ
s5.why_important: As the title poem of a major collection, it helped establish Amrita Pritam's reputation for turning longing and message-bearing into a modern idiom. A heritage learner gains a culturally important word, sunehṛe, that links poetry, letters, and emotional distance.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi:
ਕਿਸੇ ਨੇ ਮੇਰੇ ਵਾਸਤੇ ਸੁਨੇਹੜੇ ਲਿਖੇ ਸਨ
ਜਿਵੇਂ ਰੁੱਖਾਂ ਦੇ ਪੱਤਿਆਂ ਤੇ
ਕੋਈ ਹਵਾ ਦੇ ਨਾਂ ਲਿਖਦਾ ਹੈ
s5.romanization:
Kise ne mere vaste sunehṛe likhe san
Jiven rukhā̃ de pattiyā̃ te
Koi havā de nā̃ likhdā hai
s5.cultural_summary: “ਸੁਨੇਹੜੇ” is a key Amrita Pritam lyric because it turns the old Punjabi idea of the message-bearer into a modern, intimate language of distance, memory, and desire. As the title poem of an important 1955 collection, it is often cited when discussing Pritam’s post-Partition lyrical voice, women’s interiority, and the transformation of everyday Punjabi words into high literary symbols. A heritage learner should know it for the emotional and cultural range packed into the word “ਸੁਨੇਹੜੇ,” which evokes letters, messages, and longing across separation.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: ਸੁਨੇਹੜੇ — used in Punjabi speech and literary discussion for tender messages, remembered letters, or emotionally charged communication across distance; often invoked nostalgically in reference to love, absence, or old-style correspondence.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the stated expiry, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2065.

s5.title: ਕੋਈ ਨਹੀਂ ਜਾਣਦਾ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਕੋਈ ਨਹੀਂ ਜਾਣਦਾ ਕਿ ਦਿਲ ਕਿੱਥੇ ਵੱਸਦਾ ਹੈ
s5.why_important: This poem is valued for its existential tone and its inward, reflective style that broadened Punjabi lyric beyond romance alone. A heritage learner gains exposure to Amrita's philosophical vocabulary and a common modern Punjabi way of speaking about unknowability.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi:
ਕੋਈ ਨਹੀਂ ਜਾਣਦਾ ਕਿ ਦਿਲ ਕਿੱਥੇ ਵੱਸਦਾ ਹੈ
ਕੋਈ ਨਹੀਂ ਜਾਣਦਾ
s5.romanization:
Koi nahī̃ jāndā ki dil kitthē vassdā hai
Koi nahī̃ jāndā
s5.cultural_summary: ਇਹ ਕਵਿਤਾ ਅਮ੍ਰਿਤਾ ਪ੍ਰੀਤਮ ਦੀ ਅੰਦਰਮੁਖੀ, ਅਸਤਿਤਵਵਾਦੀ ਆਵਾਜ਼ ਦਾ ਮਹੱਤਵਪੂਰਨ ਨਮੂਨਾ ਮੰਨੀ ਜਾਂਦੀ ਹੈ, ਜਿਸ ਨੇ ਪੰਜਾਬੀ ਗੀਤਿਕਾਵਿ ਨੂੰ ਸਿਰਫ਼ ਰੋਮਾਂਸ ਤੋਂ ਪਰੇ ਲੈ ਜਾ ਕੇ ਅਣਜਾਣਪਣ, ਦਿਲ, ਅੰਦਰਲੀ ਖੋਜ ਅਤੇ ਮਨੁੱਖੀ ਅਸਥਿਰਤਾ ਦੇ ਸਵਾਲਾਂ ਨਾਲ ਜੋੜਿਆ। ਵਿਰਾਸਤੀ ਸਿੱਖਿਆਰਥੀ ਲਈ ਇਹ ਇਸ ਕਰਕੇ ਜ਼ਰੂਰੀ ਹੈ ਕਿ ਇਸ ਰਾਹੀਂ ਆਧੁਨਿਕ ਪੰਜਾਬੀ ਵਿੱਚ ‘ਨਾ-ਜਾਣੇ ਜਾਣਾ’, ਅੰਦਰਲੀ ਸੱਚਾਈ, ਅਤੇ ਭਾਵਨਾਤਮਕ ਅਨਿਸ਼ਚਿਤਤਾ ਬਾਰੇ ਗੱਲ ਕਰਨ ਦੀ ਇੱਕ ਪ੍ਰਭਾਵਸ਼ਾਲੀ ਕਾਵਿ-ਭਾਸ਼ਾ ਸਮਝ ਆਉਂਦੀ ਹੈ; ਇਹ ਕਵਿਤਾ ਅਕਸਰ ਆਤਮ-ਚਿੰਤਨ, ਇਕਾਂਤ, ਅਤੇ ਮਨ ਦੇ ਭੇਦਾਂ ਬਾਰੇ ਚਰਚਾਵਾਂ ਵਿੱਚ ਯਾਦ ਕੀਤੀ ਜਾਂਦੀ ਹੈ।
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: ‘ਕੋਈ ਨਹੀਂ ਜਾਣਦਾ’ — ਰੋਜ਼ਮਰਰਾ ਪੰਜਾਬੀ ਬੋਲਚਾਲ, ਲੇਖਨ, ਅਤੇ ਵਿਚਾਰਾਤਮਕ ਸੰਦਰਭਾਂ ਵਿੱਚ ਅਣਜਾਣਪਣ, ਮਨੁੱਖੀ ਸੀਮਿਤ ਸਮਝ, ਜਾਂ ਦਿਲੀ ਹਾਲਤ ਦੀ ਅਗੋਚਰਤਾ ਦਰਸਾਉਣ ਲਈ ਵਰਤਿਆ ਜਾਣ ਵਾਲਾ ਪ੍ਰਸਿੱਧ ਵਾਕ-ਖੰਡ।
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the provided curation, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2065.

s5.title: ਮੇਰੀ ਕਵਿਤਾ ਮੇਰੀ ਜ਼ਿੰਦਗੀ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਮੇਰੀ ਕਵਿਤਾ ਮੇਰੀ ਜ਼ਿੰਦਗੀ ਦਾ ਹੋਰ ਨਾਂ ਹੈ
s5.why_important: This meta-poetic poem is important because it states Amrita Pritam's lifelong idea that writing and living cannot be separated. A heritage learner gains a concise entry point into Punjabi literary self-consciousness and the prestige of poetry as lived experience.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi:
ਮੇਰੀ ਕਵਿਤਾ ਮੇਰੀ ਜ਼ਿੰਦਗੀ ਦਾ ਹੋਰ ਨਾਂ ਹੈ
ਜੋ ਕੁਝ ਮੈਂ ਜੀਅਾ
ਓਹੀ ਮੈਂ ਲਿਖਿਆ
ਜੋ ਕੁਝ ਲਿਖਿਆ, ਓਹੀ ਮੈਂ ਜੀਅਾ
s5.romanization:
Merī kavitā merī zindagī dā hor nā̃ hai
Jo kujh maĩ jīā
Ohī maĩ likhiā
Jo kujh likhiā, ohī maĩ jīā
s5.cultural_summary: This poem is one of Amrita Pritam’s clearest statements of her lifelong poetics: for her, writing was not ornament or abstraction but the lived record of experience. In the Punjabi literary canon it is often invoked in discussions of autobiographical truth, women’s writing, and the modernist idea that the poet’s life and voice are inseparable; for heritage learners, it offers a compact entry into Amrita’s moral and artistic self-understanding.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: “ਮੇਰੀ ਕਵਿਤਾ ਮੇਰੀ ਜ਼ਿੰਦਗੀ” — quoted to describe an artist whose work is inseparable from lived experience; “ਜੋ ਕੁਝ ਮੈਂ ਜੀਅਾ, ਓਹੀ ਮੈਂ ਲਿਖਿਆ” — used in literary discussion to praise honesty, confession, and experiential authenticity in writing.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the stated expiry, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2065.

s5.title: ਲੰਮੀਆਂ ਵਾਟਾਂ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਲੰਮੀਆਂ ਵਾਟਾਂ ਨੇ ਤੇ ਪੈਰ ਅਜੇ ਵੀ ਤੁਰਦੇ ਨੇ
s5.why_important: The poem's journey motif made it resonate with displacement, exile, and emotional distance in post-Partition Punjabi memory. A heritage learner gains a phrase that speaks both to migration history and to the long emotional roads of diaspora life.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi: ਲੰਮੀਆਂ ਵਾਟਾਂ ਨੇ ਤੇ ਪੈਰ ਅਜੇ ਵੀ ਤੁਰਦੇ ਨੇ
s5.romanization: Lammīā̃ vāṭā̃ ne te pair aje vī turde ne
s5.cultural_summary: Amrita Pritam’s “ਲੰਮੀਆਂ ਵਾਟਾਂ” is remembered for turning the image of a long road into a modern Punjabi metaphor for separation, endurance, and the unfinished journey left by Partition. In the Punjabi literary canon, it matters because it links intimate feeling with historical displacement, so heritage learners often encounter it in discussions of migration, exile, women’s lyric voice, and the emotional afterlife of 1947.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: ਲੰਮੀਆਂ ਵਾਟਾਂ — used in everyday speech, essays, and diaspora reflection to describe long emotional distances, difficult life journeys, or migration across generations.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on Amrita Pritam’s 2005 death date, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2066 in life-plus-60 jurisdictions.

s5.title: ਅੱਜ ਮੈਂ ਸਾੜ ਦਿੱਤੇ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਅੱਜ ਮੈਂ ਸਾੜ ਦਿੱਤੇ ਨੇ ਚਿੱਠੀਆਂ ਦੇ ਸਾਰੇ ਰਿਸ਼ਤੇ
s5.why_important: This poem is remembered for its dramatic image of burning letters, a modern symbol of emotional severance in Punjabi literary culture. A heritage learner gains a vivid example of how love, memory, and renunciation are expressed through everyday objects like letters.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi: ਅੱਜ ਮੈਂ ਸਾੜ ਦਿੱਤੇ ਨੇ ਚਿੱਠੀਆਂ ਦੇ ਸਾਰੇ ਰਿਸ਼ਤੇ
s5.romanization: Ajj main saaṛ ditte ne chiṭṭhīā̃ de sāre rishte
s5.cultural_summary: Amrita Pritam’s nazm is remembered for turning the ordinary object of letters into a powerful modern image of love, memory, and deliberate emotional severance. In the Punjabi literary canon, it matters as a compact example of post-Partition-era interiority and women’s lyrical self-expression, and it is often invoked in discussions of heartbreak, renunciation, and the symbolic ending of a relationship.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: ਅੱਜ ਮੈਂ ਸਾੜ ਦਿੱਤੇ — used figuratively in Punjabi speech and literary commentary for decisively breaking with the past, especially old love, correspondence, or emotional ties; ਚਿੱਠੀਆਂ ਦੇ ਸਾਰੇ ਰਿਸ਼ਤੇ — cited as a memorable phrase for the intimate bonds carried by letters and memories.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the stated expiry, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2065.

s5.title: ਬਿਰਹਾ ਤੂੰ ਸੁਲਤਾਨ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਬਿਰਹਾ ਤੂੰ ਸੁਲਤਾਨ
s5.why_important: This is Shiv Kumar Batalvi's signature statement on longing and one of the most quoted lines in modern Punjabi literature. A heritage learner gains a phrase that functions as cultural shorthand for the sovereignty of separation, desire, and emotional pain.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi: ਬਿਰਹਾ ਤੂੰ ਸੁਲਤਾਨ
s5.romanization: Birhā tū̃ sultān
s5.cultural_summary: This lyric is one of Shiv Kumar Batalvi’s defining statements on viraha—love in separation—and is among the most instantly recognizable lines in modern Punjabi poetry. In the Punjabi canon it matters because Batalvi turns private heartbreak into a near-mystical, sovereign force, making longing itself the ruler of emotional life. Heritage learners encounter it constantly in literary discussion, music, stage recitation, and everyday conversation whenever separation, unfulfilled desire, or beautiful suffering is being invoked.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: ਬਿਰਹਾ ਤੂੰ ਸੁਲਤਾਨ — quoted as cultural shorthand for the supremacy of longing, heartbreak, or separation; used in literary criticism, song introductions, social-media captions, and everyday speech to elevate emotional pain into something fated, noble, or all-governing.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the provided expiry, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2033.

s5.title: ਮੈਂ ਤੇ ਮੇਰਾ ਵੇਹੜਾ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਮੈਂ ਤੇ ਮੇਰਾ ਵੇਹੜਾ ਦੋਵੇਂ ਇਕੱਲੇ ਪਏ ਹਾਂ
s5.why_important: Known through the larger fame of Loona, this piece is anthologized for its intimate domestic imagery and emotional solitude. A heritage learner gains access to the symbolic Punjabi world of the vehṛa, where home space becomes a mirror of inner life.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi: ਮੈਂ ਤੇ ਮੇਰਾ ਵੇਹੜਾ ਦੋਵੇਂ ਇਕੱਲੇ ਪਏ ਹਾਂ
s5.romanization: maiṅ te merā vehṛā dovēṅ ikallē paē hāṅ
s5.cultural_summary: Though overshadowed by the larger renown of ਲੂਣਾ, this nazm is valued for turning the Punjabi vehṛa—the courtyard and emotional center of the home—into a symbol of loneliness, memory, and inward life. In the Punjabi literary canon, it matters because Shiv Kumar Batalvi compresses domestic space into existential feeling, making the poem a touchstone in discussions of solitude, abandoned homes, and the emotional landscape of Punjabi belonging.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: ਮੇਰਾ ਵੇਹੜਾ — used evocatively in speech, songs, and literary discussion to mean not just one's courtyard but one's intimate home-world, roots, or inner emotional space.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the stated expiry, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2033.

s5.title: ਦਿਲ ਲੈ ਗਈਏਂ ਨੀ ਮਾਹੀਏ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਦਿਲ ਲੈ ਗਈਏਂ ਨੀ ਮਾਹੀਏ
s5.why_important: This is among Batalvi's most performed love songs and helped carry literary Punjabi into popular singing culture. A heritage learner gains a classic example of how Punjabi romance uses direct address, musical repetition, and the beloved's theft of the heart.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi:
ਦਿਲ ਲੈ ਗਈਏਂ ਨੀ ਮਾਹੀਏ
ਦਿਲ ਲੈ ਗਈਏਂ ਨੀ ਮਾਹੀਏ
s5.romanization:
dil lai gaīeñ nī māhīe
 dil lai gaīeñ nī māhīe
s5.cultural_summary: This geet is one of Shiv Kumar Batalvi's most widely sung romantic lyrics and is often cited as a key example of how literary Punjabi poetry moved into performance, mehfil, and popular song culture in the 1960s. Heritage learners encounter in it a classic Punjabi mode of direct address to the beloved, where repetition, musical cadence, and emotional immediacy turn private longing into a shared cultural idiom. It is commonly invoked in discussions of Punjabi love lyric, sung poetry, and Batalvi's influence on later vocal renditions of modern verse.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage:
“ਦਿਲ ਲੈ ਗਈਏਂ” — used colloquially to say someone has stolen one's heart or made a powerful romantic impression.
“ਨੀ ਮਾਹੀਏ” — a familiar vocative of endearment in Punjabi song and speech, often used to evoke folk-romantic tone or playful longing.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the provided expiry, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2033.

s5.title: ਰਾਤ ਗਈ ਬਰਸਾਤ ਗਈ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਰਾਤ ਗਈ ਬਰਸਾਤ ਗਈ
s5.why_important: This ghazal is remembered for its musical compression and for linking weather, time, and emotional aftermath in a memorable refrain. A heritage learner gains a phrase often used conversationally to suggest that an intense moment has passed, though its feeling lingers.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi:
ਰਾਤ ਗਈ ਬਰਸਾਤ ਗਈ
ਦਿਲ ਤੇਰੀ ਯਾਦਾਂ ਨਾਲ ਭਿੱਜਦਾ ਰਿਹਾ
s5.romanization:
Raat gaee barsaat gaee
Dil teri yaadaan naal bhijjda riha
s5.cultural_summary: Shiv Kumar Batalvi’s ghazal is valued for turning a simple refrain about night and rain into a compact meditation on emotional aftermath, memory, and the residue of longing. In Punjabi literary culture it is often cited when discussing Batalvi’s lyrical musicality and his gift for making private sorrow sound proverb-like; heritage learners encounter it both in poetry circles and in everyday conversation about a passing but unforgettable moment.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: ਰਾਤ ਗਈ ਬਰਸਾਤ ਗਈ — used conversationally to say that an intense episode, quarrel, romance, or crisis has passed, even if its emotional trace remains; often quoted in literary discussion as an example of Batalvi’s memorable refrain-making.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the provided curation, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2033.

s5.title: ਇਕ ਮੁੱਢ ਕਦੀਮੀ ਔਰਤ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਇਕ ਮੁੱਢ ਕਦੀਮੀ ਔਰਤ ਮੇਰੇ ਅੰਦਰ ਵੱਸਦੀ ਹੈ
s5.why_important: This poem is important because it shows Batalvi's ability to imagine womanhood as archetype, memory, and civilizational presence rather than mere romantic object. A heritage learner gains a key modern Punjabi phrase for discussing ancient feminine energy and inherited gendered experience.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi:
ਇਕ ਮੁੱਢ ਕਦੀਮੀ ਔਰਤ ਮੇਰੇ ਅੰਦਰ ਵੱਸਦੀ ਹੈ
ਉਹ ਮੈਨੂੰ ਮੇਰੇ ਜਨਮ ਤੋਂ ਵੀ ਪਹਿਲਾਂ ਦੀ ਯਾਦ ਵਾਂਗ ਘੇਰਦੀ ਹੈ
s5.romanization:
Ik muḍh kadīmī aurat mere andar vassdī hai
Uh mainū̃ mere janam tō̃ vī pahlā̃ dī yād vāṅg gherdī hai
s5.cultural_summary: This poem is often cited as a striking example of Shiv Kumar Batalvi moving beyond romantic lament into a deeper symbolic imagination, where womanhood appears as ancestral memory, archetype, and civilizational force. In the Punjabi literary canon, it matters because it gives readers a modern idiom for discussing inherited feminine consciousness, and it is frequently invoked in conversations on gender, interiority, and the presence of the past within the self.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: ਮੁੱਢ ਕਦੀਮੀ ਔਰਤ — used in literary criticism, feminist discussion, and everyday cultural commentary to describe an ancient, inherited feminine presence or a deep-rooted woman-self carried across generations.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the stated expiry, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2033.

s5.title: ਮਾਏਂ ਨੀ ਮਾਏਂ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਮਾਏਂ ਨੀ ਮਾਏਂ
s5.why_important: This opening address to the mother is among the most instantly recognizable in Punjabi performance and recitation culture. A heritage learner gains a classic emotional register of Punjabi lament, where the mother becomes the first witness to pain.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi:
ਮਾਏਂ ਨੀ ਮਾਏਂ
ਮੇਰੇ ਗੀਤਾਂ ਦੇ ਨੈਣਾਂ ਵਿਚ
ਬਿਰਹੋਂ ਦੀ ਰੜਕ ਪਵੇ
s5.romanization:
Māeṅ nī māeṅ
mere gītāṅ de naiṇāṅ vicc
birhoṅ dī raṛak pave
s5.cultural_summary: Shiv Kumar Batalvi’s “ਮਾਏਂ ਨੀ ਮਾਏਂ” is one of the best-known modern Punjabi lyrics of longing, remembered for turning intimate grief into a musical, performative lament. In the Punjabi canon it stands as a signature example of Batalvi’s birha-centered poetics, and it is often invoked in recitation, singing, and discussions of virah, youthful sorrow, and the mother as the first confidante of emotional pain.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: “ਮਾਏਂ ਨੀ ਮਾਏਂ” — used as an instantly recognizable cry of sorrow, complaint, or emotional overflow in everyday speech, stage performance, and literary reference; “ਬਿਰਹੋਂ” / the poem’s birha register — often cited when describing love-longing or aching separation in a distinctly Punjabi emotional idiom.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the provided expiry, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2033.

s5.title: ਬੱਦਲ ਵਰ੍ਹਦਾ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਬੱਦਲ ਵਰ੍ਹਦਾ ਤੇ ਦਿਲ ਵੀ ਨਾਲ ਹੀ ਭਿੱਜਦਾ ਹੈ
s5.why_important: This poem exemplifies Batalvi's gift for turning monsoon imagery into an emotional climate, making weather feel personal and embodied. A heritage learner gains a common poetic association in Punjabi between rain, memory, and the surfacing of longing.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi:
ਬੱਦਲ ਵਰ੍ਹਦਾ ਤੇ ਦਿਲ ਵੀ ਨਾਲ ਹੀ ਭਿੱਜਦਾ ਹੈ
ਐਸੇ ਵੇਲੇ ਯਾਦ ਦਾ ਕੋਈ ਦੀਵਾ ਜੱਗਦਾ ਹੈ
s5.romanization:
baddal varhda te dil vī nāl hī bhijjdā hai
aise vele yād dā koī dīvā jaggdā hai
s5.cultural_summary: This गीत is a compact example of Shiv Kumar Batalvi’s signature ability to fuse outer weather with inner feeling: rain is not just scenery but a trigger for memory, ache, and emotional exposure. In the Punjabi literary canon, it matters because it helped solidify the modern association between monsoon, विरह-like longing, and intimate self-expression in post-Partition lyric poetry. Heritage learners often encounter it as a touchstone when discussing Punjabi rain imagery, romantic melancholy, and Batalvi’s intensely embodied emotional voice.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: ਬੱਦਲ ਵਰ੍ਹਦਾ — used evocatively in conversation, recitation, and cultural commentary to suggest that rainy weather awakens longing, memory, or a softened emotional state; sometimes quoted as shorthand for Batalvi-style monsoon melancholy.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the provided expiry, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2033.

s5.title: ਮੈਂ ਤੈਨੂੰ ਕਿਵੇਂ ਦੱਸਾਂ
s5.why_important: The poem is culturally resonant because it dramatizes the failure of language before intense feeling, a hallmark of Batalvi's romantic style. A heritage learner gains a familiar Punjabi expression for inexpressible love that appears in speech, song, and quotation.
s5.romanization: maiṅ tainū̃ kivēṅ dassāṅ ki maiṅ tainū̃ kinnā cāhuṅdā hāṅ
s5.cultural_summary: This lyric is remembered as a compact expression of Shiv Kumar Batalvi’s signature romantic intensity: the speaker’s feeling is so overwhelming that ordinary language seems inadequate. In the Punjabi literary canon it matters because it turns private longing into a memorable, quotable idiom, and it is often invoked in conversations about love, separation, youthful vulnerability, and the emotional directness of modern Punjabi lyric poetry.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: «ਮੈਂ ਤੈਨੂੰ ਕਿਵੇਂ ਦੱਸਾਂ» — widely used in everyday speech, songs, captions, and quotations to mean “how can I even begin to tell you,” especially when emotion exceeds words. «ਕਿੰਨਾ ਚਾਹੁੰਦਾ ਹਾਂ» — commonly echoed in romantic address to intensify declarations of affection.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the stated expiry, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2033.

s5.title: ਪੀੜਾਂ ਦਾ ਪਰਾਗਾ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਪੀੜਾਂ ਦਾ ਪਰਾਗਾ ਮੇਰੇ ਅੰਦਰ ਉੱਡਦਾ ਹੈ
s5.why_important: This poem is remembered for one of Batalvi's most striking metaphors, turning suffering into pollen that spreads invisibly and persistently. A heritage learner gains a signature image from modern Punjabi poetics and a feel for how pain is aestheticized without being diminished.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi: ਪੀੜਾਂ ਦਾ ਪਰਾਗਾ ਮੇਰੇ ਅੰਦਰ ਉੱਡਦਾ ਹੈ
s5.romanization: pīṛā̃ dā parāgā mere andar uḍdā hai
s5.cultural_summary: Shiv Kumar Batalvi is central to modern Punjabi lyric poetry, and this ghazal is remembered for its haunting image of pain as pollen: fine, pervasive, and impossible to contain. Heritage learners encounter here a signature Batalvi move—making private sorrow sensuous and beautiful without softening its sting. The poem is often invoked in discussions of विरह, emotional interiority, and the romantic-melancholic register that shaped post-Partition Punjabi literary modernism.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: ਪੀੜਾਂ ਦਾ ਪਰਾਗਾ — used metaphorically in Punjabi literary talk, social-media captions, and everyday reflective speech to describe grief or hurt that spreads quietly through one's being rather than appearing as a single dramatic wound.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the provided curation date, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2033.

s5.title: ਸਭ ਤੋਂ ਖ਼ਤਰਨਾਕ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਸਭ ਤੋਂ ਖ਼ਤਰਨਾਕ ਹੁੰਦਾ ਹੈ ਸਾਡੇ ਸੁਪਨਿਆਂ ਦਾ ਮਰ ਜਾਣਾ
s5.why_important: This is the single most cited poem in Punjabi political discourse and is quoted across protest, education, journalism, and diaspora activism. A heritage learner gains a living phrase of resistance that frames danger not only as violence but as the death of imagination and courage.
s5.phrases_in_general_usage: ਸਭ ਤੋਂ ਖ਼ਤਰਨਾਕ ਹੁੰਦਾ ਹੈ...; ਸੁਪਨਿਆਂ ਦਾ ਮਰ ਜਾਣਾ
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi:
ਸਭ ਤੋਂ ਖ਼ਤਰਨਾਕ ਹੁੰਦਾ ਹੈ
ਸਾਡੇ ਸੁਪਨਿਆਂ ਦਾ ਮਰ ਜਾਣਾ
s5.romanization:
sabh tō̃ khatarnāk huṇdā hai
sāḍe supniā̃ dā mar jāṇā
s5.cultural_summary: ਪਾਸ਼ ਦੀ ਇਹ ਨਜ਼ਮ ਆਧੁਨਿਕ ਪੰਜਾਬੀ ਰਾਜਨੀਤਿਕ ਕਵਿਤਾ ਦੀ ਸਭ ਤੋਂ ਵੱਧ ਉਧਰਿਤ ਰਚਨਾਵਾਂ ਵਿੱਚੋਂ ਇੱਕ ਹੈ, ਕਿਉਂਕਿ ਇਹ ਖ਼ਤਰੇ ਨੂੰ ਸਿਰਫ਼ ਦਮਨ, ਹਿੰਸਾ ਜਾਂ ਰਾਜੀ ਜ਼ਬਰ ਤੱਕ ਸੀਮਿਤ ਨਹੀਂ ਕਰਦੀ, ਸਗੋਂ ਮਨੁੱਖ ਦੇ ਸੁਪਨੇ, ਨੈਤਿਕ ਹਿੰਮਤ ਅਤੇ ਕਲਪਨਾ ਦੇ ਮਰ ਜਾਣੇ ਨੂੰ ਸਭ ਤੋਂ ਵੱਡਾ ਸੰਕਟ ਦੱਸਦੀ ਹੈ। ਇਹ ਕਵਿਤਾ ਵਿਦਿਆਰਥੀ ਆੰਦੋਲਨਾਂ, ਮਜ਼ਦੂਰ-ਕਿਸਾਨ ਪ੍ਰਦਰਸ਼ਨਾਂ, ਪੱਤਰਕਾਰਤਾ, ਮਨੁੱਖੀ ਅਧਿਕਾਰ ਚਰਚਾ ਅਤੇ ਡਾਇਸਪੋਰਾ ਐਕਟਿਵਿਜ਼ਮ ਵਿੱਚ ਮੁੜ-ਮੁੜ ਯਾਦ ਕੀਤੀ ਜਾਂਦੀ ਹੈ; ਪੰਜਾਬੀ ਸਾਹਿਤ ਨੂੰ ਸਮਝਣ ਵਾਲੇ ਹਰ ਸਿਖਿਆਰਥੀ ਲਈ ਇਹ ਵਿਰੋਧ, ਜਾਗਰੂਕਤਾ ਅਤੇ ਆਸ ਦੀ ਜੀਵੰਤ ਸਾਂਸਕ੍ਰਿਤਿਕ ਸੰਕੇਤ-ਪੰਕਤੀ ਹੈ।
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: «ਸਭ ਤੋਂ ਖ਼ਤਰਨਾਕ ਹੁੰਦਾ ਹੈ…» — ਜਨਤਕ ਭਾਸ਼ਣਾਂ, ਲੇਖਾਂ ਅਤੇ ਪ੍ਰਦਰਸ਼ਨ-ਨਾਰਿਆਂ ਵਿੱਚ ਕਿਸੇ ਡੂੰਘੇ ਸਮਾਜਿਕ ਜਾਂ ਨੈਤਿਕ ਸੰਕਟ ਨੂੰ ਰੇਖਾਂਕਿਤ ਕਰਨ ਲਈ ਵਰਤਿਆ ਜਾਂਦਾ ਹੈ।; «ਸੁਪਨਿਆਂ ਦਾ ਮਰ ਜਾਣਾ» — ਨਿਰਾਸ਼ਾ, ਰਾਜਨੀਤਿਕ ਸੁਸਤਪਣ ਜਾਂ ਸਮੂਹਕ ਕਲਪਨਾ ਦੇ ਖ਼ਤਮ ਹੋਣ ਲਈ ਇੱਕ ਸੰਕੇਤਕ ਵਾਕ-ਖੰਡ ਵਜੋਂ ਬਹੁਤ ਪ੍ਰਚਲਿਤ ਹੈ।
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the provided expiry, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2048.

s5.title: ਅਸੀਂ ਲੜਾਂਗੇ ਸਾਥੀ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਅਸੀਂ ਲੜਾਂਗੇ ਸਾਥੀ
s5.why_important: This solidarity poem became part of Punjabi left and student movement culture because of its direct collective voice. A heritage learner gains a key slogan-like line that shows how poetry in Punjabi often moves seamlessly into public speech and organizing language.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi:
ਅਸੀਂ ਲੜਾਂਗੇ ਸਾਥੀ
ਉਦਾਸ ਮੌਸਮ ਲਈ
ਅਸੀਂ ਲੜਾਂਗੇ ਸਾਥੀ
ਗੁਲਾਮ ਇੱਛਾਵਾਂ ਲਈ
s5.romanization:
Asī̃ laṛā̃ge sāthī
udās mausam laī
Asī̃ laṛā̃ge sāthī
gulām ichhāvā̃ laī
s5.cultural_summary: Pash’s nazm is one of the most recognizable Punjabi poems of collective resistance, remembered for turning lyric address into a shared political vow. It matters in the modern Punjabi canon because it helped fuse poetry with activist speech in the 1970s left, student, and workers’ movements, and it is still invoked at protests, commemorations of dissent, and discussions of progressive Punjabi literature. For a heritage learner, it is a key example of how Punjabi verse can move directly into slogan, memory, and public organizing language.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: ਅਸੀਂ ਲੜਾਂਗੇ ਸਾਥੀ — used as a slogan-like line in student politics, trade-union gatherings, protest posters, and cultural programs to signal solidarity and collective struggle; ਉਦਾਸ ਮੌਸਮ — sometimes cited metaphorically for a repressive or demoralizing political climate.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the stated curation date, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2048.

s5.title: ਸਾਡੇ ਸਮਿਆਂ ਵਿੱਚ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਸਾਡੇ ਸਮਿਆਂ ਵਿੱਚ ਸਭ ਕੁਝ ਇੰਨਾ ਆਸਾਨ ਨਹੀਂ ਰਿਹਾ
s5.why_important: As the title poem of a major collection, it captures Pash's historical consciousness and his insistence that poetry must answer its time. A heritage learner gains a widely used phrase for discussing the moral and political character of the present moment.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi:
ਸਾਡੇ ਸਮਿਆਂ ਵਿੱਚ ਸਭ ਕੁਝ ਇੰਨਾ ਆਸਾਨ ਨਹੀਂ ਰਿਹਾ
ਕਿ ਜੋ ਕੁਝ ਕਹਿਣਾ ਹੈ
ਸਾਫ਼-ਸਾਫ਼ ਕਹਿ ਦਿੱਤਾ ਜਾਵੇ
s5.romanization:
Sāḍe samiā̃ vich sabh kujh innā āsān nahī̃ rihā
ki jo kujh kahiṇā hai
sāf-sāf kahi dittā jāve
s5.cultural_summary: This title poem from Pash’s 1976 collection is central to modern Punjabi political poetry because it frames the present as morally difficult, historically charged, and resistant to easy speech. It is often invoked in discussions of censorship, state violence, intellectual responsibility, and the duty of poetry to confront its own era. A heritage learner should know it because the poem’s title has become a compact way of naming the ethical pressures of contemporary times.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: ਸਾਡੇ ਸਮਿਆਂ ਵਿੱਚ — widely used in essays, speeches, journalism, and everyday reflection to mean “in our times” with a specifically critical or morally alert tone, often echoing Pash’s sense that the present demands witness rather than complacency.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the provided expiry, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2048.

s5.title: ਉਡਦੇ ਬਾਜ਼ਾਂ ਮਗਰ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਉਡਦੇ ਬਾਜ਼ਾਂ ਮਗਰ ਦੌੜਣਾ ਹੀ ਜ਼ਿੰਦਗੀ ਹੈ
s5.why_important: This title poem is culturally important for its image of pursuit, aspiration, and refusal to accept small horizons. A heritage learner gains a memorable metaphor often used to describe youthful idealism, daring, and the chase after freedom.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi: ਉਡਦੇ ਬਾਜ਼ਾਂ ਮਗਰ ਦੌੜਣਾ ਹੀ ਜ਼ਿੰਦਗੀ ਹੈ
s5.romanization: uḍde bāzā̃ magar dauṛṇā hī zindagī hai
s5.cultural_summary: Pash’s title poem from the 1974 collection is remembered for turning the image of chasing hawks into a compact metaphor for audacity, aspiration, and a refusal to live within narrow limits. In Punjabi literary culture it is often invoked when speaking about rebellious youth, political courage, and the ethical demand to keep pursuing freedom rather than settling for safety or small ambitions.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: ਉਡਦੇ ਬਾਜ਼ਾਂ ਮਗਰ — used in Punjabi speech and writing to describe bold idealism, chasing a difficult dream, or refusing a timid, compromised life; often cited in student, activist, and literary contexts.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the stated expiry, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2048.

s5.title: ਮੈਂ ਨਹੀਂ ਮੰਨਦਾ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਮੈਂ ਨਹੀਂ ਮੰਨਦਾ
s5.why_important: This poem is remembered for its uncompromising language of refusal, which made it resonate in anti-authoritarian and activist circles. A heritage learner gains a compact Punjabi formula of dissent that carries literary prestige as well as political force.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi:
ਮੈਂ ਨਹੀਂ ਮੰਨਦਾ
ਮੈਂ ਨਹੀਂ ਮੰਨਦਾ
s5.romanization:
main nahī̃ manndā
main nahī̃ manndā
s5.cultural_summary: ਪਾਸ਼ ਦੀ ਇਹ ਨਜ਼ਮ ਪੰਜਾਬੀ ਕਵਿਤਾ ਵਿੱਚ ਇਨਕਾਰ, ਅਸਹਿਮਤੀ ਅਤੇ ਜ਼ਮੀਰ ਦੀ ਅਡੋਲ ਆਵਾਜ਼ ਵਜੋਂ ਯਾਦ ਕੀਤੀ ਜਾਂਦੀ ਹੈ. 1970 ਦੇ ਦਹਾਕੇ ਦੀ ਰੈਡੀਕਲ ਰਾਜਨੀਤਿਕ ਸੰਵੇਦਨਾ ਨਾਲ ਜੁੜੀ ਇਹ ਰਚਨਾ ਵਿਦਿਆਰਥੀ ਆੰਦੋਲਨਾਂ, ਖੱਬੇਪੰਥੀ ਚਰਚਾਵਾਂ, ਸੈਂਸਰਸ਼ਿਪ-ਵਿਰੋਧੀ ਮੰਚਾਂ ਅਤੇ ਆਮ ਤੌਰ ਤੇ ਅਧਿਕਾਰਵਾਦ ਦੇ ਖ਼ਿਲਾਫ਼ ਹਵਾਲੇ ਵਜੋਂ ਬਹੁਤ ਉਚਾਰੀ ਜਾਂਦੀ ਹੈ.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: «ਮੈਂ ਨਹੀਂ ਮੰਨਦਾ» — ਪੰਜਾਬੀ ਵਿੱਚ ਡਟੇ ਹੋਏ ਇਨਕਾਰ, ਨੈਤਿਕ ਅਸਹਿਮਤੀ ਜਾਂ ਸੱਤਾ-ਪ੍ਰਮਾਣਿਤ ‘ਸੱਚ’ ਨੂੰ ਰੱਦ ਕਰਨ ਲਈ ਬਹੁਤ ਪ੍ਰਚਲਿਤ ਹਵਾਲਾ; ਨਾਅਰੇ, ਲੇਖ-ਸਿਰਲੇਖ ਅਤੇ ਸੋਸ਼ਲ ਮੀਡੀਆ ਪੋਸਟਾਂ ਵਿੱਚ ਵਰਤਿਆ ਜਾਂਦਾ ਹੈ.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the provided curation, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2048.

s5.title: ਲੋਹਾ ਕਥਾ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਲੋਹਾ ਵੀ ਇਕ ਦਿਨ ਆਪਣੀ ਕਥਾ ਸੁਣਾਵੇਗਾ
s5.why_important: As the title piece of Pash's debut collection, this poem helped define his hard, industrial, anti-romantic political imagery. A heritage learner gains a foundational metaphor in Punjabi radical poetry, where iron stands for labor, struggle, and historical making.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi:
ਲੋਹਾ ਵੀ ਇਕ ਦਿਨ ਆਪਣੀ ਕਥਾ ਸੁਣਾਵੇਗਾ
ਜਦੋਂ ਭੱਠੀਆਂ ਦੇ ਮੂੰਹੋਂ ਅੱਗ ਬੋਲੇਗੀ
ਅਤੇ ਹਥੌੜਿਆਂ ਦੀ ਚੋਟ ਵਿਚੋਂ ਇਤਿਹਾਸ ਜਾਗੇਗਾ
s5.romanization:
Loha vī ik din āpṇī kathā suṇāvegā
jadõ bhaṭṭhīā̃ de mūhõ agg bolegī
ate hathauṛiā̃ dī choṭ vicõ itihās jāgegā
s5.cultural_summary: Pash’s title poem from his debut collection is a touchstone of Punjabi radical modernism: it replaces romantic lyricism with the imagery of iron, furnaces, hammer-blows, and collective labor. Heritage learners should know it because it crystallizes a major shift in Punjabi poetry toward class-conscious, anti-feudal, anti-sentimental expression, and it is often invoked in discussions of workers’ dignity, revolutionary culture, and the making of history from below.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: ਲੋਹਾ ਕਥਾ — used as a shorthand for Pash’s hard-edged, industrial-political poetic idiom, and more broadly for narratives of labor, resistance, and historical struggle in Punjabi literary and activist discourse.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the stated expiry, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2048.

s5.title: ਹੱਥ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਇਹ ਹੱਥ ਸਿਰਫ਼ ਹੱਥ ਨਹੀਂ, ਮਿਹਨਤ ਦੀ ਜ਼ਬਾਨ ਹਨ
s5.why_important: This poem is important for its dignity-centered portrayal of labor and for Pash's ability to make the body political without abstraction. A heritage learner gains a strong example of Punjabi working-class imagery and the symbolic force of hands in social discourse.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi:
ਇਹ ਹੱਥ ਸਿਰਫ਼ ਹੱਥ ਨਹੀਂ, ਮਿਹਨਤ ਦੀ ਜ਼ਬਾਨ ਹਨ
ਜੋ ਚੁੱਪ ਰਹਿ ਕੇ ਵੀ ਬੋਲਦੇ ਹਨ
s5.romanization:
ih hath siraf hath nahin, mehnat di zaban han
jo chupp rahi ke vi bolde han
s5.cultural_summary: ਪਾਸ਼ ਦੀ ਇਹ ਕਵਿਤਾ ਮਜ਼ਦੂਰ ਦੇ ਸਰੀਰ, ਖ਼ਾਸ ਕਰਕੇ ਹੱਥਾਂ, ਨੂੰ ਇੱਜ਼ਤ, ਇਤਿਹਾਸ ਅਤੇ ਵਿਰੋਧ ਦੇ ਪ੍ਰਤੀਕ ਵਜੋਂ ਪੇਸ਼ ਕਰਦੀ ਹੈ. ਪੰਜਾਬੀ ਸਾਹਿਤ ਵਿੱਚ ਇਹ ਇਸ ਲਈ ਮਹੱਤਵਪੂਰਨ ਹੈ ਕਿ ਇਹ ਵਰਗ-ਚੇਤਨਾ ਨੂੰ ਬਹੁਤ ਸਧਾਰਣ ਪਰ ਤੀਖ਼ੀ ਤਸਵੀਰਾਂ ਰਾਹੀਂ ਬਿਆਨ ਕਰਦੀ ਹੈ, ਅਤੇ ਮਿਹਨਤ, ਕਿਸਾਨ-ਮਜ਼ਦੂਰ ਅਸਮੀਤਾ, ਤੇ ਲੋਕਧਾਰੀ ਰਾਜਨੀਤਿਕ ਗੱਲਬਾਤ ਵਿੱਚ ਅਕਸਰ ਯਾਦ ਕੀਤੀ ਜਾਂਦੀ ਹੈ.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage:
ਹੱਥ — ਮਿਹਨਤ, ਕਿਰਤ, ਅਤੇ ਲੋਕੀ ਤਾਕਤ ਦੇ ਪ੍ਰਤੀਕ ਵਜੋਂ ਵਰਤਿਆ ਜਾਂਦਾ ਹੈ, ਖ਼ਾਸ ਕਰਕੇ ਮਜ਼ਦੂਰ ਜਾਂ ਕਿਸਾਨੀ ਸੰਦਰਭਾਂ ਵਿੱਚ.
ਮਿਹਨਤ ਦੀ ਜ਼ਬਾਨ — ਇਹ ਭਾਵਕ ਪ੍ਰਯੋਗ ਉਸ ਕੰਮ ਨੂੰ ਆਵਾਜ਼ ਦੇਣ ਲਈ ਵਰਤਿਆ ਜਾਂਦਾ ਹੈ ਜੋ ਬਿਨਾ ਬੋਲਿਆਂ ਵੀ ਆਪਣੀ ਸੱਚਾਈ ਦਰਜ ਕਰਦਾ ਹੈ.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the provided date, it is expected to enter the public domain approximately in 2048.

s5.title: ਕਸੁੰਭੜਾ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਕਸੁੰਭੜੇ ਵਰਗਾ ਰੰਗ ਸੀ ਤੇ ਥੋੜ੍ਹੀ ਦੇਰ ਹੀ ਟਿਕਿਆ
s5.why_important: This title poem is one of the most culturally durable images in modern Punjabi, using the kasumbhra flower for beauty that flares and fades quickly. A heritage learner gains a metaphor still used in everyday Punjabi for charm, youth, or color that is vivid but short-lived.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi: ਕਸੁੰਭੜੇ ਵਰਗਾ ਰੰਗ ਸੀ ਤੇ ਥੋੜ੍ਹੀ ਦੇਰ ਹੀ ਟਿਕਿਆ
s5.romanization: kasumbhṛe vargā rang sī te thoṛhī der hī ṭikiā
s5.cultural_summary: Prof. Mohan Singh’s "ਕਸੁੰਭੜਾ" is one of the best-known modern Punjabi lyrics because it turns the kasumbhra flower into a lasting metaphor for beauty, youth, passion, and radiance that blaze briefly and then fade. In the Punjabi literary canon it is often cited when discussing modern lyricism, romantic disillusion, and the fragility of worldly color; heritage learners should know it because its central image remains immediately recognizable in everyday speech and literary reference.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: ਕਸੁੰਭੜਾ — used for something vividly attractive but short-lived: youthful beauty, a passing romance, temporary political excitement, or any color/glory that does not last.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the stated expiry, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2038.

s5.title: ਸਾਵੇ ਪੱਤਰ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਸਾਵੇ ਪੱਤਰ ਆਏ ਨੇ ਤੇ ਦਿਲ ਫਿਰ ਹਰਾ ਹੋਇਆ
s5.why_important: This poem is important as part of Mohan Singh's shaping of modern Punjabi romantic lyric, where letters and leaves blur into one another. A heritage learner gains a culturally rich phrase linking correspondence, freshness, and emotional renewal.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi: ਸਾਵੇ ਪੱਤਰ ਆਏ ਨੇ ਤੇ ਦਿਲ ਫਿਰ ਹਰਾ ਹੋਇਆ
s5.romanization: Sāve pattar āe ne te dil phir harā hoīā
s5.cultural_summary: ਇਹ ਕਵਿਤਾ ਮੋਹਨ ਸਿੰਘ ਦੀ ਆਧੁਨਿਕ ਪੰਜਾਬੀ ਰੋਮਾਂਟਿਕ ਲਿਰਿਕ-ਧਾਰਾ ਦਾ ਇੱਕ ਯਾਦਗਾਰ ਨਿਸ਼ਾਨ ਮੰਨੀ ਜਾਂਦੀ ਹੈ, ਜਿੱਥੇ ‘ਪੱਤਰ’ ਦਾ ਅਰਥ ਚਿੱਠੀਆਂ ਵੀ ਹੈ ਅਤੇ ਪੱਤੇ ਵੀ—ਇਸ ਦੋਹਰੇ ਬਿੰਬ ਨਾਲ ਵਿਛੋੜੇ, ਉਡੀਕ ਅਤੇ ਨਵੀਂ ਤਾਜ਼ਗੀ ਇਕੱਠੇ ਜੁੜਦੇ ਹਨ। ਪੰਜਾਬੀ ਸਾਹਿਤਕ ਸੰਦਰਭ ਵਿੱਚ ਇਹ ਕਵਿਤਾ ਅਕਸਰ ਪ੍ਰੇਮ-ਪੱਤਰ, ਯਾਦ, ਬਸੰਤੀ ਤਾਜ਼ਗੀ ਅਤੇ ਭਾਵਨਾਤਮਕ ਨਵੀਨੀਕਰਨ ਦੀ ਗੱਲ ਕਰਦਿਆਂ ਯਾਦ ਕੀਤੀ ਜਾਂਦੀ ਹੈ; heritage learner ਲਈ ਇਹ ਜਾਣਨਾ ਮਹੱਤਵਪੂਰਨ ਹੈ ਕਿ ਆਧੁਨਿਕ ਪੰਜਾਬੀ ਕਵਿਤਾ ਨੇ ਰੋਜ਼ਮਰਰਾ ਸ਼ਬਦਾਂ ਨੂੰ ਕਿਵੇਂ ਗਹਿਰੇ ਪ੍ਰਤੀਕਾਂ ਵਿੱਚ ਬਦਲਿਆ.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: ‘ਸਾਵੇ ਪੱਤਰ’ — ਤਾਜ਼ਗੀ, ਨਵੀਂ ਖ਼ਬਰ, ਪ੍ਰੇਮ-ਸੰਦੇਸ਼ ਜਾਂ ਦਿਲ ਨੂੰ ਹਰਾ ਕਰ ਦੇਣ ਵਾਲੀ ਯਾਦ ਲਈ ਰੂਪਕ ਵਜੋਂ ਵਰਤਿਆ ਜਾਂਦਾ ਹੈ; ਸਾਹਿਤਕ ਗੱਲਬਾਤ ਵਿੱਚ ਇਹ phrase ਚਿੱਠੀ ਅਤੇ ਪੱਤੇ ਦੇ ਦੋਹਰੇ ਅਰਥ ਕਰਕੇ ਖ਼ਾਸ ਤੌਰ ਤੇ ਉਧਰਿਤ ਹੁੰਦਾ ਹੈ।
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the provided expiry, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2038.

s5.title: ਚਾਰ ਹੰਝੂ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਚਾਰ ਹੰਝੂ ਡਿੱਗੇ ਤੇ ਇਕ ਨਵੀਂ ਕਵਿਤਾ ਜੰਮੀ
s5.why_important: Often treated as a founding modern Punjabi lyric, this poem marks a shift toward inwardness, compression, and emotional modernity. A heritage learner gains historical perspective on how twentieth-century Punjabi poetry moved from older forms into a more personal voice.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi: ਚਾਰ ਹੰਝੂ ਡਿੱਗੇ ਤੇ ਇਕ ਨਵੀਂ ਕਵਿਤਾ ਜੰਮੀ
s5.romanization: chār hanjhū ḍigge te ik navī̃ kavitā jammī
s5.cultural_summary: ਮੋਹਨ ਸਿੰਘ ਦੀ ਇਹ ਰਚਨਾ ਅਕਸਰ ਆਧੁਨਿਕ ਪੰਜਾਬੀ ਗੀਤਿਕ ਕਵਿਤਾ ਦੇ ਸ਼ੁਰੂਆਤੀ ਮੋੜਾਂ ਵਿੱਚ ਗਿਣੀ ਜਾਂਦੀ ਹੈ, ਕਿਉਂਕਿ ਇਹ ਬਾਹਰੀ ਵਰਨਨ ਤੋਂ ਹਟ ਕੇ ਅੰਦਰੂਨੀ ਭਾਵਨਾ, ਸੰਕੁਚਿਤ ਅਭਿਵੈਕਤੀ ਅਤੇ ਨਿੱਜੀ ਸੰਵੇਦਨਾ ਨੂੰ ਕੇਂਦਰ ਬਣਾਉਂਦੀ ਹੈ। ਪੰਜਾਬੀ ਸਾਹਿਤ ਦੇ ਇਤਿਹਾਸ ਵਿੱਚ ਇਸ ਨੂੰ ਉਸ ਪਲ ਨਾਲ ਜੋੜ ਕੇ ਯਾਦ ਕੀਤਾ ਜਾਂਦਾ ਹੈ ਜਦੋਂ ਕਵਿਤਾ ਨੇ ਰਵਾਇਤੀ ਛੰਦ-ਰੂਪਾਂ ਤੋਂ ਅੱਗੇ ਵਧ ਕੇ ਆਧੁਨਿਕ ਮਨੁੱਖ ਦੇ ਇਕਾਂਤ, ਦੁੱਖ ਅਤੇ ਸਿਰਜਣਾਤਮਕਤਾ ਨੂੰ ਨਵੀਂ ਭਾਸ਼ਾ ਦਿੱਤੀ; ਇਸ ਲਈ ਇਹ ਕਵਿਤਾ ਆਧੁਨਿਕਤਾ, lyric inwardness, ਅਤੇ ਵੀਹਵੀਂ ਸਦੀ ਦੀ ਪੰਜਾਬੀ ਕਾਵਿ-ਚਰਚਾ ਵਿੱਚ ਵਾਰੰਵਾਰ ਉਲਲੇਖਿਤ ਹੁੰਦੀ ਹੈ।
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: ਚਾਰ ਹੰਝੂ — ਘਣੇ ਦੁੱਖ, ਸੰਕੁਚਿਤ ਪਰ ਡੂੰਘੀ ਭਾਵਨਾ, ਜਾਂ ਥੋੜ੍ਹੇ ਸ਼ਬਦਾਂ ਵਿੱਚ ਵੱਡੇ ਜਜ਼ਬਾਤ ਪ੍ਰਗਟ ਕਰਨ ਲਈ ਸੱਭਿਆਚਾਰਕ ਸੰਕੇਤ ਵਜੋਂ ਵਰਤਿਆ ਜਾਂਦਾ ਹੈ; ਕਈ ਵਾਰ ਆਧੁਨਿਕ ਪੰਜਾਬੀ lyric ਦੀ ਸ਼ੁਰੂਆਤ ਦਾ shorthand ਵੀ ਬਣਦਾ ਹੈ।
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the stated expiry, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2038.

s5.title: ਅਧਵਾਟੇ
s5.first_line_gurmukhi: ਅਧਵਾਟੇ ਖਲੋਤਾ ਮੈਂ ਆਪਣੀ ਰਾਹ ਨੂੰ ਵੇਖਦਾ ਹਾਂ
s5.why_important: This poem is remembered for its reflective midpoint image, making life's journey feel unfinished, uncertain, and self-aware. A heritage learner gains a useful Punjabi metaphor for being halfway through a path, whether in age, migration, or moral decision.
s5.first_lines_gurmukhi: ਅਧਵਾਟੇ ਖਲੋਤਾ ਮੈਂ ਆਪਣੀ ਰਾਹ ਨੂੰ ਵੇਖਦਾ ਹਾਂ
s5.romanization: Adhvāṭe khalotā maiṅ āpṇī rāh nū̃ vekhdā hāṅ
s5.cultural_summary: ਮੋਹਨ ਸਿੰਘ ਦੀ ਇਹ ਕਵਿਤਾ ਪੰਜਾਬੀ ਆਧੁਨਿਕ ਕਾਵਿ ਵਿੱਚ ਆਤਮ-ਚਿੰਤਨ, ਜੀਵਨ-ਯਾਤਰਾ ਅਤੇ ਅਧੂਰੇਪਣ ਦੀ ਬਹੁਤ ਯਾਦਗਾਰ ਤਸਵੀਰ ਬਣ ਗਈ ਹੈ। ਇਸ ਦਾ ‘ਅਧਵਾਟੇ’ ਵਾਲਾ ਬਿੰਬ ਖ਼ਾਸ ਕਰਕੇ ਉਮਰ ਦੇ ਮੱਧ-ਬਿੰਦੂ, ਪਰਵਾਸੀ ਅਨੁਭਵ, ਜਾਂ ਨੈਤਿਕ-ਅਸਤਿਤਵਕ ਫ਼ੈਸਲੇ ਦੇ ਵੇਲੇ ਯਾਦ ਕੀਤਾ ਜਾਂਦਾ ਹੈ, ਇਸ ਲਈ heritage learner ਲਈ ਇਹ ਪੰਜਾਬੀ ਵਿਚਾਰ-ਭਾਸ਼ਾ ਦਾ ਮਹੱਤਵਪੂਰਨ ਹਵਾਲਾ ਹੈ.
s5.key_phrases_in_usage: ਅਧਵਾਟੇ — ਜੀਵਨ, ਕਰੀਅਰ, ਸੰਘਰਸ਼ ਜਾਂ ਪਰਵਾਸ ਦੇ ‘halfway point’ ਲਈ ਰੂਪਕ ਵਜੋਂ ਵਰਤਿਆ ਜਾਂਦਾ ਹੈ; ਅਕਸਰ ਇਸ ਵਿੱਚ ਅਣਨਿਸ਼ਚਿਤਤਾ, ਠਹਿਰਾਅ ਅਤੇ ਆਪਣੇ ਆਪ ਨੂੰ ਮੁੜ ਤੋਲਣ ਦਾ ਭਾਵ ਹੁੰਦਾ ਹੈ.
s5.copyright_note: This poem remains under copyright; based on the provided curation, it is expected to enter the public domain around 2038.

s6.title: Ghorian De Vele
s6.occasion: Wedding — groom's procession
s6.why_dense: This teaches core wedding vocabulary around the groom, procession, and the public joy of the baraat. A learner gains a vivid entry into how Punjabi weddings sound before any formal ritual begins.
s6.cultural_signal: weddings / diaspora family weddings / ladies sangeet
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਘੋੜੀਆਂ ਦੇ ਵੇਲੇ ਆਇਆ ਲਾੜਾ,
ਸਿਰ ਤੇ ਸਿਹਰਾ ਸੋਹਣਾ ਪਾਇਆ।
ਘੋੜੀ ਚੜ੍ਹ ਕੇ ਵੇਖਣ ਜਾਵੇ,
ਨਾਲ ਬਰਾਤਾਂ ਰੌਣਕ ਲਾਇਆ।
s6.romanization:
Ghorian de vele aaya laaRaa,
sir te sehra sohna paaya.
Ghori chaRh ke vekhan jaave,
naal baraataan raunakk laaya.
s6.english_meaning: At the time of the ghorian, the groom arrives, beautifully adorned with a sehra on his head. Mounted on the mare, he sets out with the wedding party, bringing festivity and splendor; this is a classic wedding-procession verse sung for the groom’s departure.

s6.title: Sohna Larra Aaya
s6.occasion: Wedding — groom's arrival
s6.why_dense: This teaches praise language used for the groom and the aesthetics of Punjabi wedding welcome. A learner gains common descriptive vocabulary like beauty, honor, and festive arrival that recur across family ceremonies.
s6.cultural_signal: weddings / diaspora wedding stages / home sangeet
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਸੋਹਣਾ ਲਾੜਾ ਆਇਆ,
ਵੇ ਸੱਜਣਾਂ ਦੇ ਵੇਹੜੇ ਆਇਆ,
ਸਿਰ ਤੇ ਸਿਹਰਾ ਸੋਹਣਾ,
ਵੇ ਮੁੱਖ ਤੇ ਨੂਰ ਸਮਾਇਆ।
s6.romanization:
Sohna laarra aayaa,
ve sajjanaan de vehre aayaa,
sir te sehra sohna,
ve mukh te noor samaayaa.
s6.english_meaning: The handsome groom has arrived, entering the courtyard of his loved ones. A beautiful sehra crowns his head, and his face shines with radiance. This is a ghorian-style wedding welcome verse sung at the groom’s arrival.

s6.title: Mian Di Ghori Kesariya
s6.occasion: Wedding — groom mounts the horse
s6.why_dense: This teaches color imagery, horse symbolism, and the visual grandeur of the groom's side in Punjabi weddings. A learner gains memorable ceremonial vocabulary tied to one of the most iconic wedding scenes.
s6.cultural_signal: weddings / groom's side singing / diaspora baraat performances
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਮੀਆਂ ਦੀ ਘੋੜੀ ਕੇਸਰਿਆ,
ਵੇ ਲਾਡਲੇ ਦੀ ਘੋੜੀ ਕੇਸਰਿਆ,
ਮੱਥੇ ਤੇ ਚਮਕੇ ਟਿੱਕਾ,
ਗਲ ਵਿਚ ਪਾਏ ਹਾਰ,
ਵੇ ਲਾਡਲੇ ਦੀ ਘੋੜੀ ਕੇਸਰਿਆ।
s6.romanization:
Miaan di ghori kesariyaa,
ve laadle di ghori kesariyaa,
mathe te chamke tikka,
gal vich paae haar,
ve laadle di ghori kesariyaa.
s6.english_meaning: The groom’s mare is saffron-adorned, the beloved son’s wedding mare shining in festive color. A forehead ornament gleams and garlands hang at the neck; this is a ghorian song sung as the groom mounts the horse, celebrating his beauty, honor, and wedding display.

s6.title: Dulla Bhatti Ghori
s6.occasion: Wedding — processional singing with heroic reference
s6.why_dense: This teaches the folk-hero memory of Dulla Bhatti and how wedding song preserves Punjabi moral imagination. A learner gains a cultural bridge between marriage celebration and regional legend recognized far beyond the village setting.
s6.cultural_signal: weddings / diaspora cultural programs / folk performance circles
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਦੁੱਲਾ ਭੱਟੀ ਵਾਲਾ ਆਇਆ,
ਦੁੱਲੇ ਨੇ ਧੀ ਵਿਆਹੀ,
ਸੇਰ ਸ਼ੱਕਰ ਪਾਈ,
ਕੁੜੀ ਦਾ ਲਾਲ ਪਟਾਕਾ,
ਕੁੜੀ ਦਾ ਸਾਲੂ ਪਾਟਾ।
s6.romanization:
Dulla Bhatti wala aaya,
Dulle ne dhee viaahee,
ser shakkar paayee,
kurri da laal pataaka,
kurri da saaloo paataa.
s6.english_meaning: Dulla Bhatti has come; Dulla has married off a girl and gifted a seer of sugar. The bride is pictured in her red wedding cloth and shawl. In wedding singing, this well-known heroic folk verse invokes Dulla Bhatti as a protector who helps arrange a daughter’s marriage with honor.

s6.title: Guru Blessing Ghori
s6.occasion: Wedding — blessing the groom before departure
s6.why_dense: This teaches how Punjabi folk wedding songs often blend family celebration with religious blessing. A learner gains insight into the respectful language used when joy is framed through divine grace.
s6.cultural_signal: weddings / Sikh family ceremonies / diaspora religious-cultural events
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਸਤਿਗੁਰਾਂ ਨੇ ਲਾੜਾ ਸਜਾਇਆ,
ਲੱਗੀਆਂ ਨੇ ਮਾਵਾਂ ਦੀਆਂ ਅਸੀਸਾਂ,
ਨਿੱਕੇ ਵੱਡੇ ਹੱਥੀਂ ਫੁੱਲ ਵਰਸਾਉਣ,
ਚੜ੍ਹਿਆ ਘੋੜੀ ਤੇ ਸੋਹਣਾ ਵੀਰਾ।
s6.romanization:
Satiguraan ne laaRaa sajaayaa,
laggian ne maavaan diaan aseesaan,
nikke vadde hatheen phull varasaaun,
chaRhia ghoRi te sohna veeraa.
s6.english_meaning: The True Guru has adorned the groom, and the mothers’ blessings have begun to gather around him. Young and old shower flowers with their hands as the handsome brother mounts the wedding mare. This is a ghori verse sung at the moment of blessing and sending the groom forth.

s6.title: Ghori Chadhda Larra
s6.occasion: Wedding — groom mounting the horse
s6.why_dense: This teaches action-centered wedding vocabulary and the communal excitement around the groom's public departure. A learner gains a compact scene that is easy to remember because it matches a visible ritual still performed today.
s6.cultural_signal: weddings / baraat departure / diaspora wedding singing
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਘੋੜੀ ਚੜ੍ਹਦਾ ਲਾੜਾ ਵੇ,
ਮਾਂਵੇ ਸੁਹਾਗਣ ਕਰੇ ਵੇ।
ਘੋੜੀ ਚੜ੍ਹਦਾ ਲਾੜਾ ਵੇ,
ਭੈਣਾਂ ਸੁਹਾਗਣ ਕਰੇ ਵੇ।
s6.romanization:
Ghori chadhda laarraa ve,
maanve suhaagan kare ve.
Ghori chadhda laarraa ve,
bhainaan suhaagan kare ve.
s6.english_meaning: The groom mounts the wedding mare, and his mother performs auspicious wedding blessings; then his sisters do the same. This is a well-known ghorian verse sung at the moment of the groom’s ceremonial mounting, celebrating family joy and the suhaag-filled wedding rite.

s6.title: Sehra and Procession
s6.occasion: Wedding — groom leaves home in sehra
s6.why_dense: This teaches the word sehra and the importance of adornment in Punjabi marriage customs. A learner gains practical cultural vocabulary that appears in speech, song, and wedding commentary across generations.
s6.cultural_signal: weddings / family singing / diaspora wedding emceeing and performances
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਸਿਹਰਾ ਬੰਨ੍ਹ ਕੇ ਲਾੜਾ ਨਿਕਲਿਆ,
ਫੁੱਲਾਂ ਵਾਲਾ ਸਿਹਰਾ ਸੋਹਣਾ।
ਘੋੜੀ ਚੜ੍ਹ ਕੇ ਲਾੜਾ ਨਿਕਲਿਆ,
ਨਾਲ ਚਲੇ ਸੱਜਣ ਸਾਰੇ।
s6.romanization:
Sehra bannh ke laarra niklia,
phullan vaala sehra sohna.
Ghori charh ke laarra niklia,
naal chale sajjan saare.
s6.english_meaning: The groom sets out wearing his sehra, beautiful with flowers; mounted on the wedding mare, he leaves home with all his companions beside him. This is a ghorian-style wedding verse sung at the moment of the baraat procession, praising the groom’s adorned departure.

s6.title: Mavan Sing Suhag
s6.occasion: Wedding — bride's home before departure
s6.why_dense: This teaches that suhag is transmitted by mothers and elder women, making the song itself a lesson in intergenerational culture. A learner gains emotional and kinship vocabulary central to Punjabi family life, not just the wedding event.
s6.cultural_signal: weddings / bride's side gatherings / diaspora ladies sangeet
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਸੁਹਾਗ ਸੁਣਾਂਦੀਆਂ ਨੀ ਮਾਵਾਂ,
ਸੁਹਾਗ ਸੁਣਾਂਦੀਆਂ ਨੀ ਭੈਣਾਂ,
ਨਿੱਕੇ ਵੇਲੇ ਦੀਆਂ ਗੱਲਾਂ ਚੇਤੇ ਆਉਣ,
ਅੱਜ ਵਿਛੋੜੇ ਦੀ ਵੇਲਾ ਆਈ,
ਬਾਬਲ ਦੇ ਵੇਹੜੇ ਰੋਣ ਅੱਖੀਆਂ।
s6.romanization:
Suhaag sunaandiaan nee maavaaN,
suhaag sunaandiaan nee bhainaaN,
nikke vele diaan gallaan chete aaun,
ajj vichhoRhe di velaa aai,
baabal de vehRe ron akkhiaan.
s6.english_meaning: The mothers and sisters sing the suhag at the bride’s home, and childhood memories come flooding back as the hour of parting arrives. In this pre-departure wedding song, the bride’s eyes fill with tears in her father’s courtyard, expressing the tender sorrow of leaving her natal home.

s6.title: Bride Leaving Parents' Home
s6.first_line_gurmukhi: ਬਾਬਲ ਮੇਰਾ ਨੈਹਰ ਛੁੱਟੋ ਹੀ ਜਾਏ
s6.occasion: Wedding — bride's departure
s6.why_dense: This teaches the emotional core of Punjabi marriage as separation from the natal home and attachment to parents. A learner gains one of the most enduring themes in Punjabi oral tradition: joy and grief existing together in the same ceremony.
s6.cultural_signal: weddings / vidai moments / widely recognized in diaspora families
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਬਾਬਲ ਮੇਰਾ ਨੈਹਰ ਛੁੱਟੋ ਹੀ ਜਾਏ
ਚਾਰ ਕਹਾਰ ਮਿਲ ਮੋਰੀ ਡੋਲੀਆ ਸਜਾਵਣ
ਮੋਰਾ ਆਪਣਾ ਬੇਗਾਨਾ ਛੁੱਟੋ ਜਾਏ
ਅੰਗਣਾ ਤਾਂ ਪਰਬਤ ਭਇਆ, ਦੇਹਰੀ ਭਈ ਬਿਦੇਸ
ਜਾ ਬਾਬਲ ਘਰ ਆਪਣਾ, ਮੈਂ ਚਲੀ ਪੀਆ ਕੇ ਦੇਸ
s6.romanization:
Babal mera naihar chhutto hi jaae
Chaar kahaar mil mori doliyaa sajaavan
Mora aapna begaana chhutto jaae
Angnaa taan parbat bhayaa, dehri bhai bides
Jaa Babal ghar aapna, main chali piyaa ke des
s6.english_meaning: A classic bride’s departure song: the bride laments that her maiden home is slipping away as the palanquin bearers ready her doli. Even her own courtyard now feels distant as a mountain, and the threshold becomes a foreign land; she bids farewell to her father’s house and leaves for her husband’s home.

s6.title: Bless the New Household
s6.occasion: Wedding — blessing the bride for married life
s6.why_dense: This teaches the ideals attached to marriage such as prosperity, harmony, and the bride's honored role in a new home. A learner gains household vocabulary and social expectations embedded in everyday Punjabi speech.
s6.cultural_signal: weddings / women's singing circles / diaspora pre-wedding functions
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਸੁਹਾਗਣ ਹੋਵੇ ਨਵੀਂ ਵਹੁਟੀ,
ਘਰ ਆਵੇ ਲੱਖ ਖੁਸ਼ੀਆਂ।
ਚੰਨ ਵਰਗਾ ਮੁੱਖੜਾ ਸੋਹਣਾ,
ਮੱਥੇ ਚਮਕਣ ਬਿੰਦੀਆਂ।
ਸੱਸ ਸਹੁਰੇ ਦਾ ਮਾਣ ਵਧਾਵੇ,
ਭਰ ਜਾਵਣ ਅੰਗਣ ਰਿੱਧੀਆਂ।
s6.romanization:
Suhaagan hove naveen vahuti,
ghar aave lakh khushiyaan.
Chann varga mukhra sohna,
mathe chamkan bindiyaan.
Sass sahure da maan vadhaave,
bhar jaavan angan riddhiyaan.
s6.english_meaning: May the new bride be ever-auspicious, bringing countless joys into her new home. Her face is praised as moon-like and radiant, and she is blessed to bring honor to her in-laws and abundance into the household. This is a wedding suhag-style blessing for the bride’s married life.

s6.title: Suhag of the Bridal Courtyard
s6.occasion: Wedding — women singing at the bride's home
s6.why_dense: This teaches the domestic setting of suhag and how the courtyard becomes a cultural classroom for women and girls. A learner gains spatial and family vocabulary tied to the architecture of Punjabi home life.
s6.cultural_signal: weddings / home-based ceremonies / diaspora mehndi and jaggo settings
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਕੁੜੀ ਦੇ ਅੰਗਣ ਸੁਹਾਗ ਗਾਵਣ,
ਆਓ ਸਹੇਲਿਓ ਰਲ ਮਿਲ ਆਵਣ,
ਮਾਂਵੇਂ ਧੀਆਂ ਨੂੰ ਵੇਖਣ ਆਵਣ,
ਵੀਰੇ ਘੋੜੀਆਂ ਚੜ੍ਹ ਕੇ ਆਵਣ,
ਅੱਜ ਸੁਹਾਗਣ ਰਾਤ ਸੁਹਾਵਣ।
s6.romanization:
Kurri de angan suhag gaavan,
Aao saheliyo ral mil aavan,
Maanven dheean noo vekhan aavan,
Veere ghoriyaan charh ke aavan,
Ajj suhagan raat suhaavan.
s6.english_meaning: In the bride’s courtyard, the women sing suhag songs and call the companions to gather together. Mothers come to behold their daughters, brothers arrive in festive pride, and the night is made beautiful as a bridal, auspicious wedding eve at the girl’s home.

s6.title: Saas Teasing Song
s6.occasion: Wedding — teasing the groom's family
s6.why_dense: This teaches the playful license of sithanian, where tension between affinal families is softened through wit. A learner gains kinship terms and social humor that explain how Punjabi weddings manage hierarchy with laughter.
s6.cultural_signal: weddings / ladies sangeet / diaspora family banter performances
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਸੱਸ ਮੇਰੀ ਨੇ ਮੂੰਹ ਬਣਾਇਆ,
ਮੈਂ ਤਾਂ ਹੱਸ ਕੇ ਲੰਘ ਗਈ ਜੀ।
ਨਣਦ ਮੇਰੀ ਨੇ ਤਾਣਾ ਮਾਰਿਆ,
ਮੈਂ ਵੀ ਗੱਲ ਸੁਣਾ ਗਈ ਜੀ।
ਜੇਠ ਮੇਰਾ ਵੇਖਦਾ ਰਹਿ ਗਿਆ,
ਦੇਵਰ ਨਾਲ ਹੱਸ ਪਈ ਜੀ।
s6.romanization:
Sass meri ne moonh banaaiaa,
main taa hass ke langh gayi ji.
Nanad meri ne taanaa maariaa,
main vee gall sunaa gayi ji.
Jeth mera vekhdaa reh giyaa,
devar naal hass payi ji.
s6.english_meaning: My mother-in-law pulled a face, but I just laughed and passed by. My husband’s sister threw a taunt, and I answered back in kind; even the elder brother-in-law was left staring while I laughed on with the younger one. This is a wedding sithaniyaa-style teasing verse, playfully mocking the groom’s family with bold banter.

s6.title: Devar-Nandh Banter
s6.first_line_gurmukhi: ਦੇਵਰ ਨੰਦ ਨੇ ਛੇੜ ਛਾੜ ਕੀਤੀ
s6.occasion: Wedding — joking about in-laws
s6.why_dense: This teaches the kinship network around marriage by naming the devar and nandh in a teasing frame. A learner gains specific family-role vocabulary that is hard to learn from textbooks but common in Punjabi homes.
s6.cultural_signal: weddings / women's gatherings / diaspora sangeet skits and songs
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਦੇਵਰ ਨੰਦ ਨੇ ਛੇੜ ਛਾੜ ਕੀਤੀ,
ਮੈਂ ਤਾਂ ਚੁੰਨੀ ਨਾਲ ਮੂੰਹ ਲੁਕਾਇਆ ਜੀ,
ਸੱਸ ਨੇ ਆਖਿਆ ਹੱਸ ਕੇ ਰਹਿ ਲੈ,
ਏਹ ਤਾਂ ਵਿਆਹ ਦੇ ਘਰ ਦੀ ਰੀਤ ਪੁਰਾਣੀ ਜੀ।
s6.romanization:
Devar nand ne chhed chhaad keeti,
main taa chunni naal moonh lukaayaa jee,
sass ne aakhia hass ke reh lai,
eh taa viaah de ghar di reet puraani jee.
s6.english_meaning: The husband’s younger brother and sister tease the new bride, and she shyly hides her face in her veil. The mother-in-law laughs it off, saying this playful banter is an old custom of the wedding house — a typical sithanian mood of joking about new in-law relations.

s6.title: Heyre for Arrival
s6.occasion: Wedding — ritual welcome and arrival
s6.why_dense: This teaches a less globally known but culturally important wedding subgenre tied to specific ritual moments. A learner gains a finer map of Punjabi wedding song traditions beyond the broad labels of ghorian and suhag.
s6.cultural_signal: weddings / traditional households / folk revival performances
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਹੇਰੇ ਨੀ ਹੇਰੇ ਲਾੜਾ ਆਇਆ,
ਵੇਖੋ ਨੀ ਵੇਖੋ ਲਾੜਾ ਆਇਆ।
ਸਿਰ ਤੇ ਸਿਹਰਾ, ਹੱਥੀਂ ਕੰਗਣਾ,
ਘੋੜੀ ਚੜ੍ਹਿਆ ਲਾੜਾ ਆਇਆ।
s6.romanization:
Here ni here laaRaa aaiyaa,
vekho ni vekho laaRaa aaiyaa.
Sir te sehraa, hatheen kangnaa,
ghoRi chaRhiaa laaRaa aaiyaa.
s6.english_meaning: Heyre, heyre — the bridegroom has arrived; look, the groom has come. With sehra on his head, wedding bangles on his hands, and mounted on the mare, he is ceremonially welcomed on arrival; this is a wedding welcome song sung as the baarat reaches the bride’s side.

s6.title: Heyre of Bridal Adornment
s6.occasion: Wedding — adornment ritual
s6.why_dense: This teaches adornment vocabulary such as kajal and the intimate ritual care surrounding marriage preparation. A learner gains language for beauty practices that remain culturally legible in Punjabi family settings.
s6.cultural_signal: weddings / women's ritual singing / diaspora bridal preparation events
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਹੇਰੇ ਨੀ ਕਾਜਲ ਪਾਇਆ,
ਅੱਖੀਆਂ ਵਿਚ ਸੁਰਮਾ ਲਾਇਆ,
ਮੱਥੇ ਤੇ ਟਿੱਕਾ ਸੋਹਣਾ,
ਹੱਥੀਂ ਚੂੜਾ ਪਾਇਆ,
ਹੇਰੇ ਨੀ ਸੱਜ ਵਿਆਹਣ ਚੱਲੀ।
s6.romanization:
Heyre ni kaajal paayaa,
akkhiyaan vich surmaa laayaa,
mathe te tikkaa sohna,
hatheen chooraa paayaa,
heyre ni sajj viaahan challee.
s6.english_meaning: Heyre: the bride has put on kohl, lined her eyes, set a lovely forehead ornament, and worn her bridal chooda bangles. In this wedding adornment song, the women celebrate the moment she is fully dressed and ready to go as a bride.

s6.title: Shagan and Vatna
s6.occasion: Wedding — pre-wedding turmeric and blessing ritual
s6.why_dense: This teaches the vocabulary of vatna, shagan, and bodily preparation before marriage. A learner gains a practical understanding of pre-wedding customs that are still named and enacted in diaspora homes.
s6.cultural_signal: weddings / mayian functions / diaspora pre-wedding ceremonies
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਵਟਣਾ ਲਾਈਏ ਨੀ ਕੁੜੀਏ,
ਵਟਣਾ ਲਾਈਏ ਨੀ,
ਮੇਹੰਦੀ ਤੇਲਾਂ ਨਾਲ,
ਸੁਹਾਗਣ ਬਣਾਈਏ ਨੀ,
ਵਟਣਾ ਲਾਈਏ ਨੀ ਕੁੜੀਏ।
s6.romanization:
Vatnaa laaeeae ni kurriye,
vatnaa laaeeae ni,
mehndi telaan naal,
suhaagan banaaeeae ni,
vatnaa laaeeae ni kurriye.
s6.english_meaning: Let us apply the vatna, girl, let us anoint you with the bridal paste of mehndi and oil, making you a suhagan, an auspicious bride. This is a traditional pre-wedding shagan song sung during the turmeric-and-blessing ritual as women gather to beautify and bless the bride.

s6.title: Soun Ja Ni Guddiye
s6.occasion: Lullaby — infant sleep
s6.why_dense: This teaches the tender diminutive language used for children and the soothing rhythm of Punjabi caregiving speech. A learner gains one of the most universally recognizable home songs in Punjabi culture.
s6.cultural_signal: home / grandparents singing / diaspora family memory
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਸੌਂ ਜਾ ਨੀ ਗੁੱਡੀਏ, ਸੌਂ ਜਾ
ਲੋਰੀ ਦੇਵਾਂ ਤੈਨੂੰ, ਸੌਂ ਜਾ
ਚੰਨ ਤੇਰੇ ਲਈ ਝੂਲਾ ਪਾਵੇ
ਤਾਰੇ ਤੇਰੇ ਲਈ ਰਾਖੀ ਗਾਵਣ
ਮਾਂ ਦੇ ਹੱਥਾਂ ਨੀਂਦਰ ਆਵੇ, ਸੌਂ ਜਾ
s6.romanization:
Soun jaa ni guddiye, soun jaa
Lori devaan tainoon, soun jaa
Chann tere laee jhoola paave
Taare tere laee raakhi gaavan
Maan de hatthaan neendar aave, soun jaa
s6.english_meaning: Sleep, little doll, sleep; I sing you a lullaby. The moon sets a cradle for you and the stars keep watch in song, while sleep comes through your mother’s hands. This is a Punjabi lori for soothing an infant to sleep.

s6.title: Neendre Aaja
s6.occasion: Lullaby — calling sleep for a child
s6.why_dense: This teaches how sleep is personified and invited in Punjabi lullabies, revealing a poetic domestic worldview. A learner gains affectionate vocabulary like laal that remains common in family speech.
s6.cultural_signal: home / mothers and grandmothers / diaspora bedtime songs
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਨੀਂਦੜੇ ਆਜਾ ਮੇਰੇ ਲਾਲ ਨੂੰ,
ਤੈਨੂੰ ਰੱਬ ਦੀਆਂ ਰੱਖਾਂ।
ਕਦੇ ਨਾ ਰੋਵੇ ਮੇਰਾ ਨਿੱਕਾ,
ਤੈਨੂੰ ਚੰਨਣੀਆਂ ਰਾਤਾਂ ਵੱਖਾਂ।
s6.romanization:
Neendre aaja mere laal noo,
tainoo Rabb diyaan rakkhaan.
Kade na rove mera nikka,
tainoo channaniyaan raataan vakkhaan.
s6.english_meaning: Sleep, come to my little beloved child; I place you in God's keeping. May my little one never weep—I would set aside moonlit nights for you. This is a Punjabi lori, sung softly to soothe a baby and invoke divine protection.

s6.title: Moon Lullaby
s6.occasion: Lullaby — infant comfort with moon imagery
s6.why_dense: This teaches the kin-like address to the moon, a classic feature of Punjabi child language and imagination. A learner gains a culturally sticky image that links nature, affection, and early vocabulary learning.
s6.cultural_signal: home / nursery songs / diaspora parents teaching Punjabi
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਚੰਨ ਮਾਮਾ ਆ ਜਾ ਵੇ,
ਸੋਨੇ ਦੀ ਕਟੋਰੀ ਲਿਆ ਜਾ ਵੇ,
ਬੱਚੇ ਨੂੰ ਦੁੱਧ ਪਿਲਾ ਜਾ ਵੇ,
ਬੱਚਾ ਸੁੱਤਾ ਰਹੇ ਜਾ ਵੇ।
s6.romanization:
Chann maamaa aa jaa ve,
sone di katori liaa jaa ve,
bacche noo dudh pilaa jaa ve,
bacchaa suttaa rahe jaa ve.
s6.english_meaning: Moon-uncle, come here; bring a golden bowl and feed milk to the child, so the little one stays asleep. This is a traditional lori, using the familiar image of the moon as a loving maternal relative who helps soothe and settle the infant.

s6.title: Father Away from Home Lori
s6.occasion: Lullaby — soothing a child in separation
s6.why_dense: This teaches the emotional reality of migration and absence, themes deeply resonant in Punjabi family history. A learner gains language for longing that connects domestic song to the diaspora experience itself.
s6.cultural_signal: home / migrant family memory / diaspora households
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਪਿਉ ਪਰਦੇਸ ਗਿਆ ਨੀ ਪੁੱਤ,
ਮਾਂ ਘਰ ਬੈਠੀ ਰੋਵੇ,
ਸੌਂ ਜਾ ਮੇਰੇ ਲਾਲ ਨੀ ਪੁੱਤ,
ਰੱਬ ਤੇਰਾ ਰਾਖਾ ਹੋਵੇ।
s6.romanization:
Piu pardes giya ni putt,
Maan ghar baithi rove,
Saun jaa mere laal ni putt,
Rabb tera raakhaa hove.
s6.english_meaning: Your father has gone away to foreign parts, child, and your mother sits at home weeping. Sleep now, my precious son; may God watch over and protect you. This is a separation lullaby, where the mother soothes the child while voicing the sorrow of the father’s absence.

s6.title: Bird Imagery Lori
s6.occasion: Lullaby — infant sleep with bird imagery
s6.why_dense: This teaches common bird imagery and the way Punjabi lullabies recruit the natural world into childcare. A learner gains simple, memorable vocabulary that is ideal for early language acquisition through song.
s6.cultural_signal: home / children's song circles / diaspora language classes
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਆ ਵੇ ਚਿੜੀਏ ਲੋਰੀ ਦੇ,
ਮੇਰੇ ਨਿੱਕੇ ਬਾਲ ਨੂੰ ਲੋਰੀ ਦੇ,
ਸੌਂ ਜਾ ਮੇਰੇ ਰਾਜੇ ਪੁੱਤ,
ਤੇਰਾ ਬਾਪੂ ਆਵੇ ਰੋਟੀ ਲੈ,
ਤੇਰੀ ਮਾਂ ਪਾਵੇ ਲੋਰੀ ਵੇ।
s6.romanization:
Aa ve chirriye lori de,
mere nikke baal nu lori de,
saun ja mere raaje putt,
tera baapoo aave roti lai,
teri maan paave lori ve.
s6.english_meaning: Come, little bird, sing a lullaby to my tiny child. Sleep now, my little prince; your father will return with bread, and your mother rocks you to sleep. A simple Punjabi lori, it uses bird imagery and the warmth of the household to soothe an infant.

s6.title: Sundar Mundariye Ho
s6.occasion: Lohri festival — bonfire night
s6.why_dense: This teaches the most widely recognized Lohri refrain and introduces Dulla Bhatti through communal singing. A learner gains a complete cultural anchor for January in the Punjabi calendar with one instantly recognizable line.
s6.cultural_signal: Lohri night / schools / diaspora community events / family bonfires
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਸੁੰਦਰ ਮੁੰਦਰੀਏ ਹੋ!
ਤੇਰਾ ਕੌਣ ਵਿਚਾਰਾ ਹੋ!
ਦੁੱਲਾ ਭੱਟੀ ਵਾਲਾ ਹੋ!
ਦੁੱਲੇ ਨੇ ਧੀ ਵਿਆਹੀ ਹੋ!
ਸੇਰ ਸ਼ੱਕਰ ਪਾਈ ਹੋ!
ਕੁੜੀ ਦਾ ਲਾਲ ਪਟਾਕਾ ਹੋ!
ਕੁੜੀ ਦਾ ਸਾਲੂ ਪਾਟਾ ਹੋ!
s6.romanization:
Sundar mundariye ho!
Teraa kaun vichaaraa ho!
Dullaa Bhatti waalaa ho!
Dulle ne dhee viaahee ho!
Ser shakkar paaee ho!
Kurree daa laal pataakaa ho!
Kurree daa saaloo paataa ho!
s6.english_meaning: “O beautiful girl, who is your protector? Dulla Bhatti is.” This core Lohri verse recalls the folk hero Dulla Bhatti, celebrated for arranging a poor girl’s marriage and giving what little he could, like a seer of sugar and simple wedding cloth. It is sung around the Lohri bonfire in a lively, blessing-filled folk register.

s6.title: Dulla Bhatti Lohri Verse
s6.occasion: Lohri festival — narrative verse around the bonfire
s6.why_dense: This teaches the heroic narrative embedded inside Lohri and shows how festival song preserves social memory. A learner gains context for why Lohri is not only festive but also tied to justice, protection, and folklore.
s6.cultural_signal: Lohri night / community singing / diaspora cultural stages
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਸੁੰਦਰ ਮੁੰਦਰੀਏ ਹੋ!
ਤੇਰਾ ਕੌਣ ਵਿਚਾਰਾ ਹੋ!
ਦੁੱਲਾ ਭੱਟੀ ਵਾਲਾ ਹੋ!
ਦੁੱਲੇ ਨੇ ਧੀ ਵਿਆਹੀ ਹੋ!
ਸੇਰ ਸ਼ੱਕਰ ਪਾਈ ਹੋ!
ਕੁੜੀ ਦਾ ਲਾਲ ਪਟਾਕਾ ਹੋ!
ਕੁੜੀ ਦਾ ਸਾਲੂ ਪਾਟਾ ਹੋ!
s6.romanization:
Sundar mundariye ho!
Teraa kaun vichaaraa ho!
Dullaa Bhatti waalaa ho!
Dulle ne dhee viaahee ho!
Ser shakkar paaee ho!
Kurree daa laal pataakaa ho!
Kurree daa saaloo paataa ho!
s6.english_meaning: This is the core Lohri bonfire verse praising Dulla Bhatti, the folk hero remembered for rescuing and marrying off a girl as if she were his own daughter. The lines evoke the wedding gift of a seer of sugar and the bride’s red cloth and shawl, keeping the festive, communal call-and-response style of Lohri.

s6.title: Lohri for the New Bride
s6.occasion: Lohri festival — first Lohri after marriage
s6.why_dense: This teaches how Lohri marks family transitions, especially the arrival of a new bride into the household. A learner gains insight into how festivals and kinship milestones overlap in Punjabi social life.
s6.cultural_signal: Lohri night / family gatherings / diaspora first-Lohri celebrations
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਸੁੰਦਰ ਮੁੰਦਰੀਏ ਹੋ!
ਤੇਰਾ ਕੌਣ ਵਿਚਾਰਾ ਹੋ!
ਦੁੱਲਾ ਭੱਟੀ ਵਾਲਾ ਹੋ!
ਦੁੱਲੇ ਨੇ ਧੀ ਵਿਆਹੀ ਹੋ!
ਸੇਰ ਸ਼ੱਕਰ ਪਾਈ ਹੋ!
ਕੁੜੀ ਦਾ ਲਾਲ ਪਟਾਕਾ ਹੋ!
ਕੁੜੀ ਦਾ ਸਾਲੂ ਪਾਟਾ ਹੋ!
ਸਾਲੂ ਕੌਣ ਸਮੇਟੇ ਹੋ!
ਚਾਚਾ ਗਾਲੀ ਦੇਸੇ ਹੋ!
ਚਾਚੇ ਚੂਰੀ ਕੁੱਟੀ ਹੋ!
ਜ਼ਮੀਦਾਰਾਂ ਲੁੱਟੀ ਹੋ!
ਜ਼ਮੀਦਾਰ ਸੁਧਾਏ ਹੋ!
ਜਿੰਨੇ ਪੁੱਲੇ ਲਾਏ ਹੋ!
ਇੱਕ ਪੁੱਲਾ ਘਿਸ ਗਿਆ,
ਜ਼ਮੀਦਾਰ ਵੋਟੀ ਲੈ ਕੇ ਨੱਸ ਗਿਆ ਹੋ!
s6.romanization:
Sundar mundariye ho!
Teraa kaun vichaaraa ho!
Dullaa Bhatti waalaa ho!
Dulle ne dhee viaahee ho!
Ser shakkar paaee ho!
Kurree daa laal pataakaa ho!
Kurree daa saaloo paataa ho!
Saaloo kaun samete ho!
Chaachaa gaalee dese ho!
Chaache chooree kuttee ho!
Zamidaaraan luttee ho!
Zamidaar sudhaae ho!
Jinne pulle laae ho!
Ikk pullaa ghis giyaa,
Zamidaar voti lai ke nass giyaa ho!
s6.english_meaning: This is the core Lohri refrain about Dulla Bhatti, the folk hero remembered for rescuing and marrying off girls with honor, giving them sugar and wedding cloth. Sung at Lohri—including a new bride’s first Lohri—it blesses marriage, fertility, and household joy in a playful, communal festival register.

s6.title: Kikkli Kaleer Di
s6.occasion: Girls' play song — spinning dance
s6.why_dense: This teaches a classic children's game song with strong rhythm, repetition, and easy imagery. A learner gains a piece of Punjabi childhood culture that is still instantly recognized in schools and diaspora homes.
s6.cultural_signal: school songs / children's play / diaspora cultural classes
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਕਿੱਕਲੀ ਕਲੀਰ ਦੀ,
ਪੱਗ ਮੇਰੇ ਵੀਰ ਦੀ,
ਦੁਪੱਟਾ ਮੇਰੀ ਭਾਬੀ ਦਾ,
ਫੁਲਕਾਰੀ ਮੇਰੀ ਮਾਂ ਦੀ,
ਧੁੱਸਾ ਮੇਰੇ ਬਾਪੂ ਦਾ।
s6.romanization:
Kikkli kaleer di,
Pagg mere veer di,
Dupatta meri bhaabi da,
Phulkaari meri maan di,
Dhussa mere baapoo da.
s6.english_meaning: Kikkli of the bright tassel-spin: the turban is my brother's, the scarf my sister-in-law's, the embroidered phulkari my mother's, and the shawl my father's. This is a classic girls' spinning-play song, joyfully naming family garments in a playful village register.

s6.title: Thal Hand-Clap Song
s6.occasion: Girls' hand-clapping game
s6.why_dense: This teaches rhythm-based oral play and the social world of girls' group singing in Punjabi culture. A learner gains exposure to compact idiomatic phrasing that survives through repetition rather than formal teaching.
s6.cultural_signal: schoolyards / girls' games / diaspora Punjabi classes
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਥਾਲੇ ਵਿਚ ਚੰਨਣ ਪਾਇਆ,
ਕੁੜੀਏ ਨੀ ਤੇਰਾ ਕੌਣ ਵਿਹਾਇਆ,
ਮਾਂ ਵਿਹਾਇਆ ਬਾਪ ਵਿਹਾਇਆ,
ਵਿਹਾਇਆ ਮੇਰਾ ਵੀਰ,
ਵੀਰ ਦੇ ਘਰ ਪੈ ਗਈ ਪੀੜ।
s6.romanization:
Thaale vich channan paayaa,
kurriye ni teraa kaun vihaayaa,
maan vihaayaa baap vihaayaa,
vihaayaa mera veer,
veer de ghar pai gayi peer.
s6.english_meaning: “Moonlight is set in the platter” opens this playful girls’ thal song, sung with hand-claps in a circle. The singer teasingly asks who married the girl off—mother, father, and brother all did—and ends with the folk image that after marriage she has gone into her brother’s in-laws’ world and met the ache of separation.

s6.title: Saawan Teeyan Swing Song
s6.occasion: Teeyan festival — monsoon swings and women's gathering
s6.why_dense: This teaches the monsoon month of Saawan and the imagery of swings, reunion, and women's festivity. A learner gains a seasonal map of Punjabi life that is especially important for understanding songs sung by mothers and grandmothers.
s6.cultural_signal: Teeyan events / women's festivals / diaspora summer cultural programs
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਸਾਵਣ ਆਇਆ ਨੀ ਪੀਂਘਾਂ ਪਾਓ,
ਨੀ ਸਖੀਓ ਰਲ ਮਿਲ ਗੀਤ ਗਾਓ,
ਕਿਕਲੀ ਕਲੀਰ ਦੀ, ਪੱਗ ਮੇਰੇ ਵੀਰ ਦੀ,
ਦੁਪੱਟਾ ਮੇਰੀ ਭਾਬੀ ਦਾ, ਫੁੱਲਕਾਰੀ ਮੇਰੀ ਮਾਂ ਦੀ,
ਸਾਵਣ ਆਇਆ ਨੀ ਪੀਂਘਾਂ ਪਾਓ।
s6.romanization:
Saawan aaiyaa nee peenghaan paao,
nee sakhio ral mil geet gaao,
Kikli kaleer dee, pagg mere veer dee,
dupattaa meri bhaabi daa, phulkaari meri maan dee,
Saawan aaiyaa nee peenghaan paao.
s6.english_meaning: Saawan has come, so put up the swings; friends, gather together and sing. The verse carries the playful Teeyan mood of monsoon women’s gatherings, naming beloved family adornments and relationships in a festive, teasing folk register.

s6.title: Peeh Peeh Ve Main Bhardi Paratan
s6.first_line_gurmukhi: ਪੀਹ ਪੀਹ ਵੇ ਮੈਂ ਭਰਦੀ ਪਰਾਤਾਂ
s6.occasion: Work song — grain grinding and household labor
s6.why_dense: This teaches agricultural-household vocabulary around grain, grinding, and food preparation that shaped rural Punjabi life. A learner gains a strong sense of labor rhythm and women's work embedded in everyday song.
s6.cultural_signal: folk performance / rural memory / diaspora heritage programs
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਪੀਹ ਪੀਹ ਵੇ ਮੈਂ ਭਰਦੀ ਪਰਾਤਾਂ
ਲੋਕੀਂ ਮੰਗਣ ਦੁੱਧ ਮਲਾਈਆਂ
ਮੈਂ ਤਾਂ ਮੰਗਾਂ ਸੱਜਣਾ ਤੇਰੀਆਂ ਗੱਲਾਂ
ਕੰਮ ਕਰਾਂ ਤੇ ਨਾਲ ਸੁਣਾਈਆਂ
s6.romanization:
Peeh peeh ve main bhardi paraataan
Lokeein mangan dudh malaaiyaan
Main taan mangaan sajjanaa teriyaan gallaan
Kamm karaan te naal sunaaiyaan
s6.english_meaning: Grinding and filling the kneading trays, the woman contrasts ordinary household desires like milk and cream with her own wish: to hear her beloved’s words while she works. This is a domestic labor song, where repetitive work at the grindstone is softened by longing, flirtation, and spoken companionship.

s6.title: Charria Mahina Chet
s6.occasion: Seasonal song — spring month and agricultural cycle
s6.why_dense: This teaches Punjabi month names and the emotional shift that comes with spring's arrival. A learner gains calendar vocabulary and a seasonal worldview that helps decode many other folk songs.
s6.cultural_signal: folk singing / seasonal programs / diaspora Punjabi learning contexts
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਚੜ੍ਹਿਆ ਮਹੀਨਾ ਚੇਤ,
ਵਣਾਂ ਫੁੱਲੀਆਂ ਕਣਕਾਂ,
ਜੱਟਾ ਆਈ ਵਿਸਾਖ,
ਖੇਤਾਂ ਪੱਕੀਆਂ ਕਣਕਾਂ।
s6.romanization:
Charhia maheena Chet,
wanaan phullian kankaan,
Jattaa aaee Visaakh,
khetaan pakkian kankaan.
s6.english_meaning: The month of Chet has risen, and in the wild growth the wheat has come into flower; then comes Vaisakh, when the farmer sees the wheat ripened in the fields. This is a seasonal folk verse marking the turn from spring bloom to harvest time in the Punjabi agricultural cycle.

s6.title: Vaisakhi Harvest Song
s6.occasion: Vaisakhi festival — harvest celebration
s6.why_dense: This teaches the harvest setting of Vaisakhi and links festival joy to the agrarian economy of Punjab. A learner gains a clear understanding of why Vaisakhi is both a calendar marker and a lived rural celebration.
s6.cultural_signal: Vaisakhi melas / schools / diaspora gurdwara and community events
s6.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਆਈ ਵਿਸਾਖੀ ਖੇਤਾਂ ਵਿੱਚ,
ਪੱਕੀ ਹੋਈ ਫਸਲ ਸੁਹਾਣੀ,
ਡੋਲ ਵੱਜਣ ਖੁਸ਼ੀਆਂ ਛਾਈਆਂ,
ਨੱਚੇ ਗਾਵੇ ਹਰ ਇਕ ਜਵਾਨੀ।
s6.romanization:
Aayi Visaakhi khetaan vich,
pakki hoi fasal suhaani,
dhol vajjan khushiaan chhaaiyaan,
nachche gaave har ik jawaani.
s6.english_meaning: Vaisakhi has come into the fields, and the ripened crop looks beautiful. Drums resound and joy spreads everywhere as the young people sing and dance. This is a harvest-celebration verse for Vaisakhi, marking abundance and communal festivity.

s7.title: Daughters Abroad
s7.why_dense: This teaches a classic Punjabi metaphor where a simple bird image opens into migration grief in the second line. A learner gets diaspora vocabulary, family emotion, and the signature tappa pivot in just two lines.
s7.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਕਾਲੇ ਖੰਭ ਨੇ ਕਾਵਾਂ ਦੇ,
ਧੀਆਂ ਪਰਦੇਸ ਗਈਆਂ, ਦੁੱਖ ਮਾਪਿਆਂ ਦੇ।
s7.romanization:
Kaale khambh ne kaawaan de,
Dheean pardes gaiyaan, dukh maapiyaan de.
s7.english_meaning: The crows have black wings. In the same dark register, daughters have gone abroad, and the sorrow settles on their parents.

s7.title: Stones and Needs
s7.why_dense: This teaches how Punjabi folk verse moves from physical roughness to emotional isolation with startling compression. A learner gains idiomatic social vocabulary and a sharp example of modern folk melancholy.
s7.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਤਿੱਖੜੇ ਮੂੰਹ ਰੋੜਾਂ ਦੇ,
ਲੋੜਾਂ ਨੇ ਕੱਢਿਆ ਦੇਸੋਂ, ਕੀ ਦੋਸ਼ ਪਰਦੇਸੀਆਂ ਦਾ।
s7.romanization:
TikkhaRhe moonh roRaan de,
LoRaan ne kaDDhiaa deson, kee dosh pardesiaan da.
s7.english_meaning: The stones have sharp, cutting faces. It was need that drove me out of my homeland—how can the exile be blamed? The image of harsh stones pivots into the pain of separation caused by necessity, not choice.

s7.title: Beloved in Foreign Lands
s7.why_dense: This teaches the core separation formula of Punjabi love poetry through the emotionally loaded word ਪਰਦੇਸ. A learner immediately meets the folk voice of waiting, absence, and intimate address.
s7.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਪਰਦੇਸ ਗਿਆ ਵੇ ਮਾਹੀ,
ਸੁੱਕ ਗਏ ਨੇ ਬਾਗ਼ ਬਹਾਰਾਂ ਦੇ, ਅੱਖੀਆਂ ਰੋ ਰੋ ਕਾਹੀ।
s7.romanization:
pardes giya ve maahi,
sukk gaye ne baagh bahaaraan de, akkhiyaan ro ro kaahee.
s7.english_meaning: My beloved has gone to foreign lands. As if the gardens of spring have withered, my eyes keep weeping without cease; the outer fading of nature mirrors the inner desolation of separation.

s7.title: Single Thread in the Garland
s7.first_line_gurmukhi: ਕੱਲੀ ਕੱਲੀ ਤਾਰ ਵੇ ਹਾਰ ਵਿੱਚ
s7.why_dense: This teaches how an object image can carry loneliness without naming it directly. A learner gains metaphor vocabulary and sees how tappay compress inner feeling into domestic imagery.
s7.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਕੱਲੀ ਕੱਲੀ ਤਾਰ ਵੇ ਹਾਰ ਵਿੱਚ,
ਤੇਰਾ ਨਾਂ ਲਿਖਿਆ ਦਿਲ ਦੇ ਪਿਆਰ ਵਿੱਚ।
s7.romanization:
Kallee kallee taar ve haar vich,
Teraa naan likhia dil de pyaar vich.
s7.english_meaning: Each single thread in the garland stands out on its own; in the turn, the singer says your name is inscribed in the heart’s love. The image of separate strands becomes a metaphor for singular, intimate longing.

s7.title: Cuckoo of Separation
s7.why_dense: This teaches a major Punjabi nature symbol, the koyal, whose sweet call often intensifies sorrow rather than joy. A learner gets bird imagery, seasonal atmosphere, and emotional reversal in miniature.
s7.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਕੂਕਦੀ ਕੋਇਲ ਬਾਗਾਂ ਵਿੱਚ,
ਯਾਰ ਬਿਨਾ ਦਿਲ ਲੱਗਦਾ ਨਾ.
s7.romanization:
Kookdi koil baagaan vich,
Yaar binaa dil laggdaa naa.
s7.english_meaning: The cuckoo calls out in the gardens. In that spring image comes the turn: without the beloved, the heart finds no rest.

s7.title: Rain Came, Not You
s7.why_dense: This teaches the monsoon as a classic Punjabi emotional season where nature arrives on time but the beloved does not. A learner gains weather vocabulary and a textbook example of seasonal longing.
s7.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਸਾਵਣ ਆਇਆ ਘਟਾ ਘਿਰ ਕੇ,
ਚਿੱਤ ਨਾ ਲੱਗੇ ਯਾਰ ਬਿਨਾ, ਅੱਜਾ ਵੇ ਮਾਹੀ ਫਿਰ ਕੇ।
s7.romanization:
Saavan aaiyaa ghataa ghir ke,
Chitt naa lagge yaar binaa, ajjaa ve maahi phir ke.
s7.english_meaning: The monsoon has arrived with clouds massing all around. But my heart finds no rest without my beloved—come back to me, my love; the season has returned, but you have not.

s7.title: Cold Mother-in-Law's Embrace
s7.why_dense: This teaches kinship vocabulary through a bodily image that can be read as literal coldness or emotional distance. A learner gains insight into household relationships and the layered irony common in folk verse.
s7.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਸੱਸ ਦੀ ਬੁੱਕਲ ਠੰਡੀ,
ਜਿਵੇਂ ਕੱਛ ਦੀ ਛਾਂ ਠੰਡੀ।
s7.romanization:
Sass di bukkal thandee,
Jivein kachh di chhaan thandee.
s7.english_meaning: A mother-in-law’s embrace feels cold, like the cool shade in a barren stretch. The tappa pivots from the domestic image to a nature comparison, suggesting emotional distance through a chill, sheltering image.

s7.title: At the Well
s7.why_dense: This teaches the well as a central Punjabi village image tied to labor, meeting, and emotional depth. A learner gets concrete rural vocabulary and a strong example of how place becomes feeling.
s7.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਖੂਹ ਉੱਤੇ ਡੋਲ ਪਿਆ,
ਪਾਣੀ ਭਰਨ ਵਾਲੀਏ ਨੀ, ਤੇਰਾ ਰੂਪ ਕਮਾਲ ਪਿਆ।
s7.romanization:
Khooh utte dol piya,
Paani bharan waaliye ni, teraa roop kamaal piya.
s7.english_meaning: A bucket lies at the well. The image turns from the wellside scene to the girl drawing water: her beauty is so striking that it outshines the simple act of filling water.

s7.title: Sowing and Waiting
s7.why_dense: This teaches agricultural language as emotional metaphor, a hallmark of Punjabi oral poetics. A learner gains farming vocabulary and sees how growth and waiting mirror human attachment.
s7.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਬੀਜ ਵਿਆਹੇ ਖੇਤਾਂ ਵਿੱਚ,
ਮੇਘਾਂ ਦੀ ਰਾਹ ਤੱਕੇ ਦਿਲ, ਸੱਜਣਾ ਤੇਰੀ ਆਹਟ ਵਿੱਚ।
s7.romanization:
Beej viaahe khetan vich,
Meghaan di raah takke dil, sajjna teri aahat vich.
s7.english_meaning: Seeds are sown in the fields, and the heart waits for the clouds. In the turn of the second line, that seasonal waiting becomes longing for the beloved’s approach, joining rain-hunger in the land with desire in the heart.

s7.title: Wheat Fields of Longing
s7.why_dense: This teaches one of Punjab's most recognizable landscape images and how beauty can sharpen absence. A learner gets iconic rural vocabulary and a memorable nature-to-emotion pivot.
s7.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਕਣਕ ਦੇ ਖੇਤ ਸੁਹਾਵਣੇ,
ਦਿਲ ਲੈ ਗਿਆ ਹਾਏ ਓਏ ਤੇਰਾ ਮੁਖੜਾ ਸੋਹਣੇ।
s7.romanization:
Kanak de khet suhaavne,
Dil lai giya haaye oye teraa mukhraa sohne.
s7.english_meaning: The wheat fields look beautiful and inviting; then the feeling turns inward. Your lovely face has stolen my heart, linking the beauty of the fields with the ache of love.

s7.title: Eyes Sent a Message
s7.why_dense: This teaches a common Punjabi idea that eyes speak before words do. A learner gains expressive body-language vocabulary and a compact example of indirect romantic communication.
s7.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਅੱਖਾਂ ਨੇ ਸੁਨੇਹਾ ਘੱਲਿਆ,
ਦਿਲ ਵਾਲੇ ਹੀ ਸਮਝਦੇ ਨੇ।
s7.romanization:
Akkhaan ne sunehaa ghallia,
Dil waale hi samajhde ne.
s7.english_meaning: The eyes have sent a message; only those with loving hearts can understand it. The image begins with a silent glance carrying news, then pivots to the idea that love alone can read what words never say.

s7.title: Bracelets Remember
s7.why_dense: This teaches ornament vocabulary that is central to Punjabi wedding and love imagery. A learner gains culturally rich nouns and sees how objects become carriers of memory.
s7.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਵਾਲੀਆਂ ਚੂੜੇ ਖਨਕਦੇ,
ਦਿਲ ਲੈ ਗਿਆ ਮੇਰਾ ਢੋਲਾ, ਨੈਣਾਂ ਵਿਚੋਂ ਲੰਘਦੇ।
s7.romanization:
Vaaliyaan chooRe khanakde,
Dil lai giya mera dhola, nainaan vichon langhde.
s7.english_meaning: The bangles and bridal bracelets chime brightly, setting the scene with their intimate music. Then the feeling turns: my beloved stole my heart just by passing through the path of my eyes, linking the outer sound of adornment with the inner shock of love.

s7.title: Boy Teases Girl
s7.why_dense: This teaches direct gendered vocabulary and the flirtatious voice found in many short Punjabi lyrics. A learner gets a simple dialogue frame and a light example of folk courtship.
s7.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਮੁੰਡੇ ਨੇ ਕਿਹਾ ਕੁੜੀ ਨੂੰ,
ਤੇਰੇ ਨੈਣਾਂ ਨੇ ਮਾਰ ਮੁਕਾਇਆ।
s7.romanization:
Munde ne kiha kurri noo,
Tere nainaan ne maar mukaayaa.
s7.english_meaning: The boy says to the girl: your eyes have finished me off. The tappa pivots from direct address to the emotional blow, where her beauty becomes the force that overwhelms him.

s7.title: Riverbank Waiting
s7.why_dense: This teaches the river as a threshold image for waiting, distance, and emotional flow. A learner gains place vocabulary and a classic feminine voice of patient longing.
s7.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਦਰਿਆ ਕੰਢੇ ਬੈਠੀ ਆਂ,
ਲਹਿਰਾਂ ਗਿਣਦੀ ਤੇਰਾ ਰਾਹ ਵੇਖਦੀ ਆਂ।
s7.romanization:
Dariyaa kandhe baithi aan,
Lahiraan gindi teraa raah vekhdi aan.
s7.english_meaning: I sit on the riverbank, counting the waves. As the river keeps moving, so does my longing: with every wave, I keep watching the path for your return.

s7.title: Two Birds, One Gone
s7.why_dense: This teaches paired-bird imagery, a widespread folk shorthand for companionship and loss. A learner gets an easy visual metaphor and a complete emotional arc in two lines.
s7.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਜੋੜੇ ਪੰਛੀ ਟਾਹਣੀ ਤੇ,
ਇੱਕ ਉੱਡ ਗਿਆ, ਸਾਡਾ ਦਿਲ ਰਹਿ ਗਿਆ ਵਿਛੋੜੇ ਤੇ।
s7.romanization:
Jorre panchhee taahni te,
Ikk udd giya, saada dil reh giya vichhorre te.
s7.english_meaning: A pair of birds sat on the branch; then one flew away. In that image, the singer turns to separation: one beloved is gone, and the heart is left behind in the ache of parting.

s7.title: Saas and Nuh
s7.why_dense: This teaches two essential kinship terms through a familiar domestic tension. A learner gains practical family vocabulary and insight into how folk verse encodes household power.
s7.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਸੱਸ ਨੂਹ ਦੀ ਬਣਦੀ ਨਹੀਂ,
ਚਰਖਾ ਕੱਤਦੀ ਦਾ ਚਿੱਤ ਪਰਦੇਸੀਂ।
s7.romanization:
Sass nooh di bandi nahin,
Charkha kattdi da chitt pardesiin.
s7.english_meaning: The mother-in-law and daughter-in-law do not get along; while she spins at the wheel, her heart turns toward the absent beloved. The pivot shifts from household tension to inner longing, contrasting domestic friction with love’s pull.

s7.title: Nanke and Dadke
s7.why_dense: This teaches the culturally important contrast between maternal and paternal homes in Punjabi life. A learner gains kinship-world vocabulary and a compact map of emotional belonging.
s7.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਨਾਨਕਿਆਂ ਦੀ ਛਾਂ ਵੱਖਰੀ,
ਦਾਦਕਿਆਂ ਦੇ ਨੇਹ ਨਿਆਰੇ।
s7.romanization:
Naankeyan di chhaan vakhri,
Daadkeyan de neh niyaare.
s7.english_meaning: The shade of the maternal home is of one special kind; the affection of the paternal kin is special in another way. The tappa pivots from the image of sheltering shade to the emotional truth that both sides of the family hold their own distinct love.

s7.title: A Sister's Worth
s7.why_dense: This teaches the emotional value placed on sibling bonds in Punjabi culture. A learner gains family vocabulary and a memorable ethical statement wrapped in folk simplicity.
s7.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਭੈਣ ਭੈਣ ਦੀ ਕੀਮਤ ਜਾਣੇ,
ਜਿਵੇਂ ਧਰਤੀ ਜਾਣੇ ਮੀਂਹ ਨੂੰ।
s7.romanization:
Bhain bhain di keemat jaane,
Jiven dhartee jaane meenh noo.
s7.english_meaning: A sister truly knows the worth of a sister, just as the earth knows the value of rain. The tappa pivots from kinship to nature, comparing sisterly bond to the deep, life-giving longing between dry land and rainfall.

s7.title: Baari Barsi Handkerchiefs
s7.why_dense: This teaches the most recognizable boli opening formula and the rhythmic setup-punchline style of performance verse. A learner gets repetition, wedding energy, and a highly reusable cultural pattern.
s7.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਬਾਰੀ ਬਰਸੀ ਖੱਟਣ ਗਿਆ ਸੀ,
ਖੱਟ ਕੇ ਲਿਆਂਦਾ ਰੁਮਾਲ,
ਜੇ ਮੇਰਾ ਰੱਬ ਮੈਨੂੰ ਪੁੱਤ ਦੇਵੇ,
ਸੋਹਰਾ ਪਾਵਾਂ ਮਹੀਨੇ ਸਾਲ।
s7.romanization:
Baari barsi khattan giya si,
khatt ke liyaanda rumaal,
je mera Rabb mainu putt deve,
sohra paawaan maheene saal.
