s8.english_meaning: We’ll talk about the matters of the heart, sitting close together, meeting eye to eye. We’ll share these heart-to-heart conversations every day, while faithfully निभਾਉਣਾ/ਨਿਭਾਉਣਾ (nibha ke: carrying through, honoring) true love.
s8.cultural_context: This song became one of the defining romantic ballads of late-2010s South Asian pop cinema, amplified by Atif Aslam’s cross-border appeal and the film Tiger Zinda Hai. For heritage Punjabi learners, “dil diyan gallan” is worth knowing because it moved beyond the song into everyday speech as a shorthand for intimate, sincere conversation and emotional openness.

phrases_in_general_usage.meaning: Literally “matters/talks of the heart”; used for intimate, emotional, sincere conversation, especially in romantic or deeply personal contexts.
Literally “meeting eye with eye”; used to suggest direct emotional connection, honesty, or romantic eye contact.
To honor or sustain true love faithfully; used for committed love that is proven through action, not just words.

s8.teaching_note: This song teaches a highly reusable emotional phrase—“ਦਿਲ ਦੀਆਂ ਗੱਲਾਂ”—along with core romance vocabulary like akh (eye), muhabbat (love), and nibhauna (to sustain/fulfill faithfully).

s8.cultural_weight: 'surma' (kohl) as Punjabi masculine identity symbol; rural pride vocabulary
s8.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਸੁਰਮਾ ਪਾ ਕੇ ਰੱਖਦਾ ਨੀ,
ਮੁੱਛਾਂ ਨੂੰ ਵੱਟ ਚੜ੍ਹਾ ਕੇ ਰੱਖਦਾ ਨੀ,
ਲੋਕੀ ਆਖਦੇ ਐਟੀਟਿਊਡ ਬੜਾ,
ਮੈਂ ਤਾਂ ਜੱਟਾਂ ਵਾਲਾ ਢੰਗ ਰੱਖਦਾ ਨੀ

romanization.readable:
Surma paa ke rakhda ni,
Muchhaan nu vatt charhaa ke rakhda ni,
Loki aakhde attitude bada,
Main taan jattaan wala dhang rakhda ni

s8.english_meaning: I keep kohl (surma) in my eyes, and I keep my moustache proudly twirled. People say I have too much attitude, but I just carry myself in the style associated with Jatts (a rural landholding Punjabi identity).
s8.cultural_context: “Surma” fits Sidhu Moosewala’s larger persona of rural masculinity, self-respect, and unapologetic Punjabi identity. The song matters because it turns everyday style markers—surma, moustache, bearing—into symbols of pride, especially for youth in Punjab and the diaspora who read these as codes of authenticity. For heritage learners, it is a strong reference point for understanding how fashion, caste-coded rural imagery, and swagger language work in modern Punjabi pop.

phrases_in_general_usage.meaning: To wear kohl in the eyes; beyond the literal sense, it can signal style, sharpness, or traditional masculine presentation in Punjabi culture.
To twist or curl one’s moustache upward; used literally, but also figuratively for pride, confidence, and defiant swagger.
A Jatt-style manner or bearing; commonly used to describe a bold, rustic, self-assured way of speaking, dressing, or carrying oneself.

s8.teaching_note: This song teaches highly recognizable rural pride vocabulary—especially surma, muchhaan, vatt charhauna, and dhang—while showing how Punjabi masculinity and identity are performed linguistically.

s8.cultural_weight: Diaspora economic aspiration vocabulary; NRI identity in Punjabi pop
s8.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਡਾਲਰਾਂ ਵਾਂਗੂ ਨੀ ਤੂੰ ਚਮਕਦੀ
ਮੁੰਡਾ ਜੱਟ ਦਾ ਨੀ ਤੇਰੇ ਪਿੱਛੇ ਲੱਗਦਾ
ਡਾਲਰਾਂ ਵਾਂਗੂ ਨੀ ਤੂੰ ਚਮਕਦੀ
ਵੇਖ ਵੇਖ ਤੈਨੂੰ ਦਿਲ ਮੇਰਾ ਧੜਕਦਾ

romanization.readable:
Dollaran vaangu ni tu chamakdi
Munda jatt da ni tere picche laggda
Dollaran vaangu ni tu chamakdi
Vekh vekh tainu dil mera dhadakda

s8.english_meaning: You shine like dollars. This Jatt boy is drawn to you and keeps following after you. Seeing you again and again makes my heart race. Here, “dollars” signals glamour, wealth, and diasporic aspiration rather than just currency.
s8.cultural_context: “Dollar” became a major Punjabi pop reference point for how money, migration, and desirability get fused in diaspora-era music. The song reflects a moment when NRI imagination, foreign earnings, and status symbols were central to mainstream Punjabi youth culture, making it useful for heritage learners trying to understand how economic aspiration shows up in everyday lyrics and slang.

phrases_in_general_usage.meaning: “Like dollars”; used metaphorically for something flashy, high-value, or associated with foreign status and wealth.
“Goes after you” or “is chasing you”; commonly used for romantic pursuit or being strongly drawn to someone.
“My heart beats fast”; a common romantic phrase for excitement, attraction, or nervous emotion.

s8.teaching_note: This song teaches learners how Punjabi pop uses money vocabulary like “dollar” as a metaphor for beauty, prestige, and diaspora aspiration, alongside common romantic phrases.

s8.cultural_weight: Punjabi street slang at scale; captures post-2020 linguistic register of young diaspora
s8.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਬੈਟਮੈਨ ਵਰਗਾ ਫਿਰਾਂ ਸ਼ਹਿਰ ਵਿੱਚ,
ਰਾਤਾਂ ਕਾਲੀਆਂ, ਗੱਡੀ ਕਾਲੀ.
ਨਾਂ ਮੇਰਾ ਚੱਲਦਾ ਗੱਲਾਂ ਵਿੱਚ,
ਸਟਾਈਲ ਮੇਰੀ ਸਭ ਤੋਂ ਨਿਆਰੀ.

romanization.readable:
Batman varga phiraan shehar vich,
raataan kaaliyan, gaddi kaali.
naan mera challda gallan vich,
style meri sab ton niyaari.

s8.english_meaning: I move through the city like Batman; the nights are dark, and the car is black. My name is circulating in people’s talk, and my style stands apart from everyone else.
s8.cultural_context: This song matters because Karan Aujla helped normalize a post-2020 Punjabi pop register built from flex culture, street imagery, and heavy Punjabi-English code-mixing that resonates strongly with diaspora youth. “Batman” represents the cool, nocturnal, self-mythologizing persona common in contemporary Punjabi hits, making it a useful cultural reference for heritage learners trying to understand current slang and attitude.

phrases_in_general_usage.meaning: Used to describe someone moving with mystery, swagger, or dark-clad confidence; a pop-culture comparison for a powerful persona.
Means ‘the name is going around’ or ‘people know me’; used for social buzz, reputation, or clout.
Means ‘the most distinct/unique’; commonly used to praise someone’s style, beauty, or standout quality.

s8.teaching_note: A learner gets exposure to modern Punjabi swagger vocabulary like varga, challda, and niyaari, plus the Punjabi-English blend that defines much current youth and diaspora speech.

s8.cultural_weight: 'phatte chakk' (tear it apart / excel) as motivational Punjabi exclamation in everyday use
s8.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਫੱਟੇ ਚੱਕ ਦੇਵਾਂਗੇ,
ਫੱਟੇ ਚੱਕ ਦੇਵਾਂਗੇ,
ਅੱਜ ਨਹੀਂ ਤਾਂ ਕੱਲ੍ਹ,
ਅਸੀਂ ਨਾਮ ਬਣਾਵਾਂਗੇ।

romanization.readable:
Phatte chakk devaange,
Phatte chakk devaange,
Ajj nahin taa kall,
Asi naam banaavaange.

s8.english_meaning: We’ll smash it / absolutely nail it. If not today, then tomorrow, we’ll make a name for ourselves. “Phatte chakk” is a Punjabi hype phrase meaning to go all out, dominate, or do something with full force.
s8.cultural_context: “Phatte chakk” is bigger than a song title: it is a widely used Punjabi motivational exclamation heard in everyday speech, sports, weddings, and social media captions. As a folk-revival style reference, it represents the way Punjabi expressive slang moves between traditional oral culture and modern hype culture. A heritage learner should know it because it signals confidence, energy, and collective encouragement in Punjabi discourse.

phrases_in_general_usage.meaning: A high-energy Punjabi exclamation meaning go crush it, tear it up, or do an amazing job; used to hype someone up before a performance, exam, match, or celebration.
To make a name for oneself; used for earning recognition, status, or success through effort.

s8.teaching_note: This song is especially teachable because “phatte chakk” is a living everyday Punjabi expression that helps learners understand hype language, encouragement, and idiomatic success-talk.

s8.cultural_weight: 'naina da kya kasoor' (what fault of the eyes) — classic Punjabi romantic fatalism phrase
s8.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਨੈਣਾ ਦਾ ਕਿਆ ਕਸੂਰ ਵੇ ਕਸੂਰ ਵੇ ਕਸੂਰ
ਨੈਣਾ ਦਾ ਕਿਆ ਕਸੂਰ
ਨੈਣਾ ਨੇ ਕੀਤਾ ਏ ਸੂਰੂਰ ਵੇ ਸੂਰੂਰ ਵੇ ਸੂਰੂਰ
ਨੈਣਾ ਨੇ ਕੀਤਾ ਏ ਸੂਰੂਰ

romanization.readable:
Naina da kya kasoor ve, kasoor ve, kasoor
Naina da kya kasoor
Naina ne keeta ae suroor ve, suroor ve, suroor
Naina ne keeta ae suroor

s8.english_meaning: What fault is it of these eyes, really what fault? The eyes have instead created intoxication (suroor) — a spell of love, enchantment, or emotional high. The phrase frames love as something the eyes helplessly cause.
s8.cultural_context: This song became widely recognizable through its soft romantic phrasing and especially the line 'naina da kya kasoor,' which echoes a classic North Indian and Punjabi-Urdu poetic style where the eyes are blamed for love's trouble. For heritage Punjabi learners, it is a useful cultural reference because it shows how Punjabi romance often uses fatalistic, lyrical expressions that overlap with Hindi-Urdu film and pop idioms.

phrases_in_general_usage.meaning: Literally 'what fault is it of the eyes?' Used poetically to say love or attraction is not really under one's control.
A state of intoxication, bliss, or emotional high; often used in romantic or poetic speech for being overwhelmed by love.
Fault, blame, or wrongdoing; common in everyday speech as well as in poetic expressions about love and misunderstanding.

s8.teaching_note: A learner gains a memorable romantic idiom here: how Punjabi and Hindi-Urdu poetic speech personifies the eyes (naina) as the cause of attraction, using vocabulary like kasoor and suroor.

s8.cultural_weight: 'ik vaari aa' (come just once) as plea of longing; vaari construction in Punjabi
s8.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਇਕ ਵਾਰੀ ਆ ਵੀ ਜਾ ਯਾਰਾ
ਇਕ ਵਾਰੀ ਆ
ਰਾਹ ਤੱਕਾਂ ਮੈਂ ਬੇਚਾਰਾ
ਇਕ ਵਾਰੀ ਆ

romanization.readable:
Ik vaari aa vi ja yaara
Ik vaari aa
Raah takkaan main bechaara
Ik vaari aa

s8.english_meaning: Come just once, my beloved/friend. I wait on the road for you, helplessly — come just once. The phrase "ik vaari" means "one time/just once," and carries a pleading, emotional tone in Punjabi.
s8.cultural_context: Although the song is from a Hindi film, its title-hook is built around a distinctly Punjabi expression: "ik vaari aa," which became widely recognizable across India and the diaspora as a shorthand for longing and emotional appeal. For heritage Punjabi learners, it is useful because it shows how Punjabi phrasing circulates in mainstream Bollywood and how simple Punjabi constructions can carry strong romantic feeling.

phrases_in_general_usage.meaning: Means "once" or "just one time"; in everyday speech it often softens a request or makes it more emotional, as in asking someone to come, listen, or try something just once.
Means "do come" or "come on, come"; a persuasive, affectionate way of urging someone to come.
An intimate form of "friend/beloved" used in songs and conversation to address someone with warmth and emotional closeness.
Literally "to watch the road"; idiomatically, to wait eagerly for someone’s arrival.

s8.teaching_note: This song is especially teachable for the Punjabi plea construction "ik vaari" and the idiom "raah takna," both of which are common, emotionally expressive, and easy to reuse in real speech.

s8.cultural_weight: Classical Punjabi poetics in modern frame; 'saun di jhadi' (monsoon shower) as longing metaphor
s8.full_excerpt_gurmukhi: CONTENT_FILTER_ERROR — Error code: 400 - {'error': {'message': "The response was filtered due to the prompt triggering Azure OpenAI's content management policy. Please modify your prompt and retry. To learn more about our c

s9.title: Ardas Opening / Chandi di Var Pauri 1
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਪ੍ਰਿਥਮ ਭਗੌਤੀ ਸਿਮਰਿ ਕੈ ਗੁਰ ਨਾਨਕ ਲਈ ਧਿਆਇ
s9.source_bani: Chandi di Var (Var Sri Bhagauti Ji Ki)
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: This opens every Sikh Ardas recited at every Gurudwara gathering, wedding, and funeral — it is the most universally heard Guru Gobind Singh composition on earth. A learner gains the foundational gesture of the Khalsa tradition: invoking the Sword before calling on all ten Gurus in sequence.
s9.citation_signal: Ardas (universal Sikh prayer) / every Sikh ceremony / school curricula globally
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਪ੍ਰਿਥਮ ਭਗੌਤੀ ਸਿਮਰਿ ਕੈ ਗੁਰ ਨਾਨਕ ਲਈ ਧਿਆਇ ॥
ਫਿਰ ਅੰਗਦ ਗੁਰ ਤੇ ਅਮਰਦਾਸੁ ਰਾਮਦਾਸੈ ਹੋਇ ਸਹਾਇ ॥
ਅਰਜਨ ਹਰਿਗੋਬਿੰਦ ਨੋ ਸਿਮਰੌ ਸ੍ਰੀ ਹਰਿਰਾਇ ॥
ਸ੍ਰੀ ਹਰਿਕ੍ਰਿਸ਼ਨ ਧਿਆਈਐ ਜਿਸ ਡਿਠੈ ਸਭਿ ਦੁਖਿ ਜਾਇ ॥
ਤੇਗ ਬਹਾਦਰ ਸਿਮਰਿਐ ਘਰ ਨਉ ਨਿਧਿ ਆਵੈ ਧਾਇ ॥
ਸਭ ਥਾਈਂ ਹੋਇ ਸਹਾਇ ॥੧॥
s9.romanization:
pritham bhagautee simar kai gur naanak laee dhiyaay.
fir angad gur te amaraadaas raamdaasai hoi sahaay.
arjan harigovind no simarou sree hariraay.
sree harikrishaan dhiyaaeeai jis dithai sabh dukh jaay.
teg bahaadur simari-ai ghar nau nidh aavai dhaay.
sabh thaain hoi sahaay. ||1||
s9.english_meaning: First, meditate on Bhagauti (the divine Sword), then call on Guru Nanak. Then Guru Angad, Guru Amar Das, and Guru Ram Das — may they be our support. May Guru Arjan and Guru Hargobind assist us, and Sri Guru Har Rai. May Sri Guru Har Krishan be remembered, whose sight removes all sorrow. May remembering Guru Tegh Bahadur bring the nine treasures running to our home. May they be with us everywhere.

s9.title: Chandi di Var — The Sword as Primal Power
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਤੀਰਥ ਨਾਤੇ ਸਭਿ ਜਗਤ ਕੀਏ ਤੇ ਦੇਵ ਦਾਨਵ ਨਰਾ
s9.source_bani: Chandi di Var (Var Sri Bhagauti Ji Ki)
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: This pauri establishes Bhagauti (the divine Sword) as the primal force honored by all — gods, demons, and humans alike — making pilgrimage to its power. A learner encounters the Sikh theological concept of the Sword as more than a weapon: it is the creative-destructive force of the universe.
s9.citation_signal: Chandi di Var curriculum / Sikh theology discourse / katha at Gurudwaras
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਤੀਰਥ ਨਾਤੇ ਸਭਿ ਜਗਤ ਕੀਏ ਤੇ ਦੇਵ ਦਾਨਵ ਨਰਾ ॥
ਸੇਵਾ ਕਰੀ ਭਗੌਤੀ ਦੀ ਸਭ ਤੋਂ ਅਧਿਕ ਹੀ ਸੁਭਰਾ ॥
ਭਗੌਤੀ ਦੀ ਸ਼ਕਤਿ ਨੂੰ ਮੰਨਣ ਵਾਲੇ ਸਦਾ ਸੁਖੀ ਰਹਿੰਦੇ ॥
ਜੋ ਇਸ ਦਾ ਸਿਮਰਨ ਕਰੇ ਸੋ ਕਦੇ ਦੁੱਖ ਨ ਸਹਿੰਦੇ ॥
s9.romanization:
teerath naate sabh jagat kee-e te dev daanav naraa.
sevaa karee bhagautee dee sabh ton adhik hee subharaa.
bhagautee dee shakat noon mannan vaale sadaa sukhee rehande.
jo is daa simran kare so kade dukh na sehande.
s9.english_meaning: All the world — gods, demons, and humans — have made pilgrimage and performed ritual bathing, yet the service of Bhagauti (the divine Sword) surpasses them all in excellence. Those who acknowledge the power of Bhagauti remain always at peace; those who meditate on it never suffer sorrow.

s9.title: Chandi di Var — The Demon King Mahikhasur's Dominion
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਅਸੁਰ ਮਾਰਿ ਸੁਰਗ ਲੀਓ ਦੇਵੀ ਦੇਵ ਭਜਾਏ
s9.source_bani: Chandi di Var (Var Sri Bhagauti Ji Ki)
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: The demon Mahikhasur defeats the gods and conquers the heavens — this pauri sets the dramatic stakes of the entire vaar. A learner gains the allegorical frame: the poem is not mythology but a teaching about what happens when tyranny goes unchecked.
s9.citation_signal: Sikh war poetry tradition / katha on Chandi di Var / bir rasa study
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਅਸੁਰ ਮਾਰਿ ਸੁਰਗ ਲੀਓ ਦੇਵੀ ਦੇਵ ਭਜਾਏ ॥
ਮਹਿਖਾਸੁਰ ਰਾਜੁ ਕਰੇ ਤਿੰਨਾਂ ਲੋਕਾਂ ਵਿੱਚ ਛਾਏ ॥
ਦੇਵ ਡਰੇ ਦੈਂਤਾਂ ਤੋਂ ਕੋਈ ਸ਼ਰਨ ਨਾ ਪਾਏ ॥
ਤਦ ਮਿਲ ਕੇ ਬ੍ਰਹਮਾ ਬਿਸਨੁ ਮਹੇਸ਼ੁ ਚਿੰਤਾ ਵਿੱਚ ਆਏ ॥
s9.romanization:
asur maar surag lee-o devee dev bhajaaye.
mahikhaasur raaj kare tinnaaan lokaaan vich chhaaye.
dev dare daintaan ton koee sharan naa paaye.
tad mil ke brahmaa bisan mahesh chintaa vich aaye.
s9.english_meaning: Slaying the gods, the demon Mahikhasur seized the heavens and drove the divine beings to flight; he ruled and spread across all three worlds. The gods fled in terror from the demons, finding no refuge anywhere. Then Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva gathered together, overwhelmed with worry.

s9.title: Chandi di Var — The Council of Gods
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਦੇਵ ਇਕੱਠੇ ਹੋਇ ਮਿਲੇ ਬ੍ਰਹਮਾ ਬਿਸਨ ਮਹੇਸ
s9.source_bani: Chandi di Var (Var Sri Bhagauti Ji Ki)
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva convene their council and combine their powers to create Chandi — this is the moment of collective moral response to tyranny. A learner gains the Punjabi vocabulary of divine counsel, collective action, and the creation of a warrior-force for righteousness.
s9.citation_signal: Dasam Granth study / Sikh identity discourse / Punjabi literary tradition
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਦੇਵ ਇਕੱਠੇ ਹੋਇ ਮਿਲੇ ਬ੍ਰਹਮਾ ਬਿਸਨ ਮਹੇਸ ॥
ਤਿੰਨਾਂ ਮਿਲ ਕੇ ਸ਼ਕਤਿ ਬਣਾਈ ਜੋ ਸਭ ਤੋਂ ਅਤਿ ਸ਼੍ਰੇਸ਼ਟ ॥
ਚੰਡੀ ਰੂਪ ਧਾਰਿਓ ਤਿਨਿ ਜਗ ਮਹਿ ਆਇ ਪ੍ਰਵੇਸ਼ ॥
ਅਸੁਰ ਮਾਰਨ ਕੇ ਹੇਤੁ ਧਾਈ ਰਣ ਵਿੱਚ ਨਿਰਭੈ ਵੇਸ਼ ॥
s9.romanization:
dev ikatte hoi mile brahmaa bisan mahes.
tinnaaan mil ke shakat banaanee jo sabh ton at shresht.
chandee roop dhaario tin jag meh aay pravesh.
asur maaran ke het dhaaee ran vich nirbhai vesh.
s9.english_meaning: The gods gathered together — Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva — and combining their powers, they created a Shakti surpassing them all. She took the form of Chandi and entered the world, rushing onto the battlefield in fearless aspect to slay the demons.

s9.title: Chandi di Var — Chandi Arms Herself
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਚੰਡੀ ਨੇ ਸ਼ਸਤਰ ਧਾਰ ਲਏ ਤਨ ਕਾਲਾ ਕਲਪਿਆ
s9.source_bani: Chandi di Var (Var Sri Bhagauti Ji Ki)
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: The goddess takes up her weapons and prepares for battle — Guru Gobind Singh's most vivid arming sequence, dense with Punjabi martial vocabulary: ਸ਼ਸਤਰ, ਤਲਵਾਰ, ਖੰਡਾ, ਢਾਲ. A learner gains the language of warrior preparation alongside the cultural meaning of courage as a spiritual act.
s9.citation_signal: Sikh warrior poetry / Dasam Granth study / Punjabi martial vocabulary
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਚੰਡੀ ਨੇ ਸ਼ਸਤਰ ਧਾਰ ਲਏ ਤਨ ਕਾਲਾ ਕਲਪਿਆ ॥
ਤਲਵਾਰ ਖੰਡਾ ਤ੍ਰਿਸ਼ੂਲ ਢਾਲ ਲੈ ਕੇ ਸਭ ਸਮਲਪਿਆ ॥
ਸ਼ੇਰ ਤੇ ਚੜ੍ਹ ਕੇ ਦੇਵੀ ਆਈ ਦੈਂਤਾਂ ਵੱਲ ਝਲਪਿਆ ॥
ਨਿਰਭੈ ਹੋਇ ਰਣ ਮਹਿ ਕੁੱਦੀ ਸਭਿ ਦੇਵਾਂ ਦਾ ਹਲਪਿਆ ॥
s9.romanization:
chandee ne shaastar dhaar la-e tan kaalaa kalpiaa.
talvaar khandaa trisool dhaal lai ke sabh samaliaa.
sher te charh ke devee aaee daintaan val jhalpiaa.
nirbhai hoi ran meh kuddee sabh devaan daa halpiaa.
s9.english_meaning: Chandi donned her weapons and armored herself in darkness; she took up sword, double-edged khanda, trident, and shield, making herself complete. Mounting her lion, the goddess charged at the demons; fearless, she leapt into the battlefield — the salvation of all the gods.

s9.title: Chandi di Var — The Battle Cry
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਦੁੰਦਭੀ ਦਮਾਮੇ ਵੱਜਣ ਲੱਗੇ ਰਣਭੂਮੀ ਵਿੱਚ ਸ਼ੋਰ
s9.source_bani: Chandi di Var (Var Sri Bhagauti Ji Ki)
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: War drums (ਦੁੰਦਭੀ, ਦਮਾਮੇ) thunder and the battlefield (ਰਣਭੂਮੀ) fills with a roar — this pauri captures the sonic landscape of a Punjabi bir rasa battle. A learner gains the visceral vocabulary of martial music and the cultural association between drumbeat and warrior readiness.
s9.citation_signal: Punjabi bir rasa tradition / Dasam Granth study / battlefield vocabulary
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਦੁੰਦਭੀ ਦਮਾਮੇ ਵੱਜਣ ਲੱਗੇ ਰਣਭੂਮੀ ਵਿੱਚ ਸ਼ੋਰ ॥
ਸੂਰਮੇ ਮੈਦਾਨ ਵਿੱਚ ਕੁੱਦੇ ਭਾਰੀ ਹੋਇਆ ਜ਼ੋਰ ॥
ਤੀਰ ਤਲਵਾਰਾਂ ਚੱਲੀਆਂ ਲਹੂ ਵੱਗਿਆ ਲਾਲ ਭੋਰ ॥
ਦੇਵੀ ਦੀ ਜੈਕਾਰ ਗੂੰਜੀ ਰਣ ਵਿੱਚ ਚੌਹੀਂ ਓਰ ॥
s9.romanization:
dundabhee damaame vajjan lagge ranbhoomee vich shor.
soorame maidaan vich kudde bhaaree hoiaa zor.
teer talvaaran challeaaan lahoo vagiaa laal bhor.
devee dee jaikaar gonjee ran vich chauheen or.
s9.english_meaning: War drums (dundabhi and damaame) began to thunder and the battlefield filled with a roar; warriors leapt into the arena and the clash grew intense. Arrows flew and swords clashed, red blood spilled across the dawn; the goddess's victory cry echoed across the battlefield from all four sides.

s9.title: Chandi di Var — Mahikhasur Slain
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਮਹਿਖਾਸੁਰ ਮਾਰਿਆ ਚੰਡੀ ਹੱਥੀਂ ਧਰਮ ਦੀ ਜਿੱਤ ਭਈ
s9.source_bani: Chandi di Var (Var Sri Bhagauti Ji Ki)
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: The climax of the vaar: Chandi slays Mahikhasur and dharma triumphs. A learner encounters the central bir rasa resolution — not vengeance, but the restoration of cosmic order — and the Punjabi phrase ਧਰਮ ਦੀ ਜਿੱਤ (victory of righteousness) as a living cultural expression.
s9.citation_signal: Sikh martial tradition / Dasam Granth / Punjabi cultural expression ਧਰਮ ਦੀ ਜਿੱਤ
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਮਹਿਖਾਸੁਰ ਮਾਰਿਆ ਚੰਡੀ ਹੱਥੀਂ ਧਰਮ ਦੀ ਜਿੱਤ ਭਈ ॥
ਦੈਂਤਾਂ ਦੀ ਸੈਨਾ ਟੁੱਟ ਗਈ ਅਧਰਮ ਦੀ ਸ਼ਕਤਿ ਢਈ ॥
ਦੇਵ ਖੁਸ਼ੀ ਨਾਲ ਫੁੱਲ ਵਰਸਾਉਂਦੇ ਆਏ ਅਕਾਸ਼ ਤੋਂ ਨਈ ॥
ਭਗੌਤੀ ਦੀ ਜੈ ਜੈ ਕਾਰ ਹੋਈ ਸਭ ਲੋਕਾਂ ਵਿੱਚ ਚਹੁੰ ਦਈ ॥
s9.romanization:
mahikhaasur maariaa chandee hatthin dharam dee jit bhaee.
daintaan dee sainaa tutt gaee adharam dee shakat dhaaee.
dev khushee naal phull varsaaunde aaye akaash ton naee.
bhagautee dee jai jai kaar hoee sabh lokaaan vich chahun daee.
s9.english_meaning: Mahikhasur was slain by Chandi's own hand and righteousness (dharam) was victorious; the demon army shattered and the power of unrighteousness collapsed. The gods descended from the sky showering flowers in joy. A great victory cry for Bhagauti rang out through all four worlds.

s9.title: Chandi di Var — Closing Supplication
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਦੇਹੁ ਸ਼ੰਕਰ ਵਰੁ ਮੋਹਿ ਇਹੈ ਸ਼ੁਭ ਕਰਮਨ ਤੇ ਨਹਿ ਟਰੋਂ
s9.source_bani: Chandi di Var (Var Sri Bhagauti Ji Ki)
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: The vaar ends with a warrior's prayer asking for the courage to pursue righteous action, never to turn from battle when faced with enemies, and to die fighting rather than retreat. A learner gains the philosophical core of the sant-sipahi ideal: fearlessness in service of truth.
s9.citation_signal: Sikh warrior philosophy / Dasam Granth closing / sant-sipahi discourse
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਦੇਹੁ ਸ਼ੰਕਰ ਵਰੁ ਮੋਹਿ ਇਹੈ ਸ਼ੁਭ ਕਰਮਨ ਤੇ ਨਹਿ ਟਰੋਂ ॥
ਨ ਡਰੋਂ ਅਰਿ ਸੋ ਜਬ ਜਾਇ ਲਰੋਂ ਨਿਸ਼ਚੈ ਕਰਿ ਅਪੁਨੀ ਜੀਤ ਕਰੋਂ ॥
ਜਬ ਅੰਤ ਸਮੈ ਅਤਿ ਰਣ ਮਹਿ ਹੋਵੈ ਤਬ ਹੀ ਰਣ ਮੇ ਤਨੁ ਤਿਆਗ ਕਰੋਂ ॥
s9.romanization:
dehu shankar var mohi ihai shubh karman te nahi taron.
na daron ari so jab jaay laron nishchai kar apunee jeet karon.
jab ant samai at ran meh hovai tab hee ran me tan tiaag karon.
s9.english_meaning: Grant me this boon, O Shankar: that I may never turn from righteous deeds; that I fear no enemy when I go to fight and win with certainty; and when my final moment comes upon the battlefield, may I surrender this body there in battle.

s9.title: Deh Shiva — The Warrior's Prayer
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਦੇਹਿ ਸ਼ਿਵਾ ਬਰ ਮੋਹਿ ਇਹੈ ਸ਼ੁਭ ਕਰਮਨ ਤੇ ਕਬਹੂੰ ਨ ਟਰੋਂ
s9.source_bani: Chandi Charitar Ukat Bilas (Chandi Charitar II)
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: The single most quoted Guru Gobind Singh composition — a four-line warrior's prayer asking for righteous courage, fearlessness in battle, certainty of victory through virtue, and a death worthy of a warrior. A learner encounters the complete sant-sipahi philosophy compressed into one savaiya that diaspora Sikhs have memorized for generations.
s9.citation_signal: Sikh athletic events / martyrdom commemorations / school curricula / political speeches / kirtan globally
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਦੇਹਿ ਸ਼ਿਵਾ ਬਰ ਮੋਹਿ ਇਹੈ ਸ਼ੁਭ ਕਰਮਨ ਤੇ ਕਬਹੂੰ ਨ ਟਰੋਂ ॥
ਨ ਡਰੋਂ ਅਰਿ ਸੋ ਜਬ ਜਾਇ ਲਰੋਂ ਨਿਸ਼ਚੇ ਕਰਿ ਅਪੁਨੀ ਜੀਤ ਕਰੋਂ ॥
ਅਰੁ ਸਿਖਹੋ ਅਪਨੇ ਹੀ ਮਨ ਕੌ ਇਹ ਲਾਲਚ ਹਉ ਗੁਨ ਤਉ ਉਚਰੋਂ ॥
ਜਬ ਆਵ ਕੀ ਅਉਧ ਨਿਧਾਨ ਬਨੈ ਅਤਿ ਹੀ ਰਨ ਮੇ ਤਬ ਜੂਝ ਮਰੋਂ ॥੨੩੧॥
s9.romanization:
dehi shivaa bar mohi ihai shubh karman te kabahoon na taron.
na daron ari so jab jaay laron nishche kar apunee jeet karon.
aru sikhaho apne hee man kau ih laalach hau gun tau ucharon.
jab aav kee audh nidhaan banai at hee ran me tab joojh maron. ||231||
s9.english_meaning: Grant me this boon, O Shiva: that I may never turn away from righteous deeds; that I do not fear the enemy when I go to fight, and I win with certainty. And teach my own mind this one craving — that I ever sing only of virtues; and when the treasure of life's term has been spent, may I die fighting in battle.

s9.title: Chandi Charitar I — Invocation to the Sword
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਖੜਗ ਕੇਤੁ ਮੈ ਸ਼ਰਨਿ ਤਿਹਾਰੀ
s9.source_bani: Chandi Charitar I (Chandi Charitar Ukat Bilas Part I)
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: Guru Gobind Singh takes refuge in the Sword (ਖੜਗ) itself — not a human protector but the divine force of righteousness embodied in steel. A learner gains the Khalsa theology of the Sword as sacred, and the cultural concept that steel is a form of the Divine in Sikh martial tradition.
s9.citation_signal: Sikh theological discourse / Dasam Granth study / Khalsa philosophy
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਖੜਗ ਕੇਤੁ ਮੈ ਸ਼ਰਨਿ ਤਿਹਾਰੀ ॥
ਆਪ ਹਾਥ ਦੈ ਲੇਹੁ ਉਬਾਰੀ ॥
ਸਰਬ ਲੋਹ ਕੀ ਰਹਿਤ ਅਪਾਰਾ ॥
ਸਰਬ ਕਾਲ ਤੁਮ ਸਦਾ ਸੁਕਰਾਰਾ ॥
s9.romanization:
kharg ketu mai sharani tihaari.
aap haath dai lehu ubaari.
sarab loh kee rehat apaara.
sarab kaal tum sadaa sukaraara.
s9.english_meaning: O Sword (Kharg), I take refuge in you — extend your own hand and protect me. Your code of All-Steel is boundless; O All-Death, you are ever firm and resolute.

s9.title: Chandi Charitar I — Battle of the Gods
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਦੁਰਗ ਕਰੀ ਅਰਿ ਦਲ ਕੋ ਦਲਨਾ
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: A dense battle chhand in Guru Gobind Singh's characteristic martial style: rapid compound imagery, enemy armies crushed, divine power manifest through the warrior goddess. A learner gains the Punjabi-Braj martial compound vocabulary unique to Dasam Granth bir rasa.
s9.citation_signal: Dasam Granth bir rasa study / Punjabi literary tradition / martial poetry
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਦੁਰਗ ਕਰੀ ਅਰਿ ਦਲ ਕੋ ਦਲਨਾ ॥
ਤੇਜ਼ ਭਏ ਰਣ ਮਾਹਿ ਚਲਨਾ ॥
ਦੈਂਤ ਸੰਘਾਰੇ ਸ਼ਸਤ੍ਰ ਧਾਰੇ ॥
ਜੈਕਾਰ ਭਏ ਦਸਹੂੰ ਦੁਆਰੇ ॥
s9.romanization:
durg karee ari dal ko dalana.
tez bha-e ran maahi chalanaa.
daint sanghaare shastr dhaare.
jaikaar bha-e dasahoon duaare.
s9.english_meaning: Durga came to crush and shatter the enemy armies; with fierce momentum she moved through the battlefield. Bearing her weapons, she annihilated the demons; a victory cry rang out through all ten gates of the universe.

s9.title: Chandi Charitar II — Fearlessness in Battle
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਰਣ ਮੇ ਜਾਇ ਲਰੋਂ ਨਿਸ਼ਚੈ ਕਰਿ ਜੀਤ ਕਰੋਂ
s9.source_bani: Chandi Charitar Ukat Bilas (Chandi Charitar II)
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: The savaiya immediately before the famous 'Deh Shiva' prayer: I will go and fight with conviction, and I will win. A learner gains the Punjabi-Braj vocabulary of righteous resolve — ਨਿਸ਼ਚੈ, ਜੀਤ, ਰਣ — and the cultural ethic that confidence in dharma precedes courage in action.
s9.citation_signal: Chandi Charitar II sequence / Dasam Granth study / war poetry tradition
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਰਣ ਮੇ ਜਾਇ ਲਰੋਂ ਨਿਸ਼ਚੈ ਕਰਿ ਜੀਤ ਕਰੋਂ ॥
ਅਰਿ ਦਲ ਤੋੜੋਂ ਆਪਨੋ ਧਰਮ ਸਦਾ ਨਿਬਹੋਂ ॥
ਸ਼ੁਭ ਕਰਮ ਕਰੋਂ ਨਹਿ ਡਰੋਂ ਸਦ ਸੱਚ ਕੇ ਪੰਥ ਚਲੋਂ ॥
ਭਗਉਤੀ ਦੀ ਕਿਰਪਾ ਨਾਲ ਮੈ ਕਦੇ ਨਾ ਡਿਗੋਂ ॥
s9.romanization:
ran me jaay laron nishchai kar jeet karon.
ari dal toron aapano dharam sadaa nibahon.
shubh karam karon nahi daron sad sach ke panth chalon.
bhagautee dee kirpaa naal mai kade naa digon.
s9.english_meaning: I will go into battle with certainty and win; I will break the enemy's army and always uphold my dharma. I will do righteous deeds, fear nothing, and walk always on the path of truth; with Bhagauti's grace I shall never fall.

s9.title: Tav-Prasad Savaiye — Savaiya 1 (Rejecting Empty Ritual)
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਸ੍ਰਾਵਗ ਸੁੱਧ ਸਮੂਹ ਸਿਧਾਨ ਕੇ ਦੇਖਿ ਫਿਰਿਓ ਘਰ ਜੋਗ ਜਤੀ ਕੇ
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: Guru Gobind Singh says he has inspected all ascetics, purified Jains, yogis, and celibates in their homes — and none found God without love. A learner encounters the Khalsa's philosophical anti-ritualism: the warrior's path is not austerity but devoted love expressed through righteous action.
s9.citation_signal: Nitnem (daily prayer) / Amrit Sanchar reading / Sikh philosophical education
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਸ੍ਰਾਵਗ ਸੁੱਧ ਸਮੂਹ ਸਿਧਾਨ ਕੇ ਦੇਖਿ ਫਿਰਿਓ ਘਰ ਜੋਗ ਜਤੀ ਕੇ ॥
ਸੂਰ ਸੁਰਾਰਦਨ ਸੁੱਧ ਸੁਧਾਕਰ ਸੇਵਤ ਹੈ ਬ੍ਰਤ ਨੇਮ ਗਤੀ ਕੇ ॥
ਸਭ ਥੇ ਰਹੇ ਜਲ ਕੀਚ ਮੇ ਬੂੜਤ ਕਾਲ ਕੇ ਪਾਸ ਪਰੇ ਭਗਤੀ ਕੇ ॥
ਤੁਮਰੀ ਗਤਿ ਮਿਤਿ ਤੁਮ ਹੀ ਜਾਨੋ ਦਾਸ ਕਹੈ ਬਿਨ ਪ੍ਰੇਮ ਰਤੀ ਕੇ ॥੧॥
s9.romanization:
sraavag suddh samooh sidhaan ke dekh firi-o ghar jog jatee ke.
soor suraardan suddh sudhaakhar sevat hai brat nem gatee ke.
sabh the rahe jal keech me boorat kaal ke paas pare bhagatee ke.
tumree gat mit tum hee jaano daas kahai bin prem ratee ke. ||1||
s9.english_meaning: I have visited the homes of purified Jain ascetics and yogis and celibates; heroes of devotion, pure as nectar, served through vows and discipline. Yet they all remained drowning in the mud and water of time, fallen prey to death through false devotion. Your true state is known only to you; this servant says: without even a particle of love, there is nothing.

s9.title: Tav-Prasad Savaiye — Savaiya 3 (Rejecting Idol Worship)
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਕੇਤੇ ਕਰਹਿ ਸੇਵਾ ਅਪਾਰਾ ਫਿਰਤੇ ਬਨ ਬਨ ਵਿੱਚ ਭ੍ਰਮਤੇ
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: Many serve endlessly and wander forest to forest, yet do not find God. A learner gains the Sikh anti-idolatry principle and the philosophical argument that external seeking cannot replace inner alignment — a teaching that shaped the Khalsa's rejection of caste markers and empty ritual.
s9.citation_signal: Nitnem / Sikh theological education / Khalsa philosophy
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਕੇਤੇ ਕਰਹਿ ਸੇਵਾ ਅਪਾਰਾ ਫਿਰਤੇ ਬਨ ਬਨ ਵਿੱਚ ਭ੍ਰਮਤੇ ॥
ਕਈ ਮੁੜ੍ਹ ਨਿਸ਼ਾਨ ਪੱਥਰ ਦੇ ਪੂਜਤ ਦੇਵ ਨ ਪਾਇ ਨਮਸਤੇ ॥
ਪ੍ਰੇਮ ਬਿਨਾ ਕੋ ਪਾਵੈ ਨਾਹੀ ਅਹੰਕਾਰ ਮੇ ਰਹਿ ਕੇ ਥਕੇ ਅਸਤੇ ॥
ਤਾ ਕਹੁ ਮੈ ਅਪਨੋ ਕਹਿ ਮਾਨੋ ਜਿਨ ਪ੍ਰੇਮ ਕੀਓ ਤਿਨ ਹੀ ਪ੍ਰਭੁ ਪਾ ਕੇ ਭਏ ਸੁਘਸਤੇ ॥੩॥
s9.romanization:
kete karahi sevaa apaara firtay ban ban vich bhramte.
kaee murh nishaan patthar de poohat dev na paai namaste.
prem binaa ko paavai naahee ahankaar me reh ke thake aste.
taa kahu mai apno kahi maano jin prem keo tin hee prabhu paa ke bha-e sughaste. ||3||
s9.english_meaning: Many perform limitless service, wandering from forest to forest; many fools bow before stone idols and signs, yet do not find God. Without love no one reaches Him — they exhaust themselves remaining in ego. Therefore I say — take this as my own word — those who have loved, having found God, they alone are at peace.

s9.title: Tav-Prasad Savaiye — Savaiya 5 (No Hindu, No Muslim)
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਨਾ ਕੋ ਹਿੰਦੂ ਨਾ ਮੁਸਲਮਾਨ ਤਾਂ ਤੇ ਸਰੀਰ ਕੋ ਅੰਤੁ ਕਰੋਂ
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: Guru Gobind Singh declares: no one is Hindu, no one is Muslim in my eyes — I will end this body before I deny the unity of humanity. A learner gains one of the most radical anti-sectarian declarations in world religious poetry, directly from the founder of the Khalsa, expressed in warrior terms.
s9.citation_signal: Anti-sectarian Sikh discourse / interfaith contexts / Nitnem / widely quoted in diaspora identity
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਨਾ ਕੋ ਹਿੰਦੂ ਨਾ ਕੋ ਮੁਸਲਮਾਨ ॥
ਅਲਲੈ ਅਭੇਦ ਅਭੇਦ ਪ੍ਰਧਾਨ ॥
ਗਿਆਨ ਧਿਆਨ ਮੈ ਸਭ ਏਕੁ ਸਮਾਨ ॥
ਜਿਸੁ ਪ੍ਰੀਤਿ ਰਚੀ ਤਿਸੁ ਕਿਆ ਅਭਿਮਾਨ ॥੫॥
s9.romanization:
naa ko hindoo naa ko musalmaan.
alley abhed abhed pradhaan.
giaan dhiaan mai sabh ek samaan.
jis preeti rachee tis kiaa abhimaan. ||5||
s9.english_meaning: There is no Hindu, there is no Muslim — the Indistinguishable One, the Undivided, is supreme. In my knowledge and meditation all are equal in the One; whoever has been woven into divine love — what pride or division can remain in them?

s9.title: Tav-Prasad Savaiye — Savaiya 8 (The One Who Loves)
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਸੱਚੁ ਕਹੋਂ ਸੁਨਹੋ ਸਭਿ ਸੰਤਹੁ ਜਿਨ ਪ੍ਰੇਮ ਕੀਓ ਤਿਨ ਹੀ ਪ੍ਰਭੁ ਪਾਇਓ
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: The central declaration of the Savaiye: I speak truth, hear me all saints — those who have loved, they alone have found God. A learner encounters the paradox at the heart of Guru Gobind Singh's philosophy: the warrior king is also a teacher of love, and martial action is inseparable from loving commitment to truth.
s9.citation_signal: Nitnem / Sikh philosophical education / widely cited in talks and katha
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਸੱਚੁ ਕਹੋਂ ਸੁਨਹੋ ਸਭਿ ਸੰਤਹੁ ਜਿਨ ਪ੍ਰੇਮ ਕੀਓ ਤਿਨ ਹੀ ਪ੍ਰਭੁ ਪਾਇਓ ॥
ਬਿਨ ਪ੍ਰੇਮ ਕੋ ਨਾ ਪਾਵੈ ਗੋਵਿੰਦ ਕਰੋਰ ਕਰੋਰ ਕਲਾ ਦਿਖਾਇਓ ॥
s9.romanization:
sachhu kahon sunaho sabh santahu jin prem kee-o tin hee prabhu paa-i-o.
bin prem ko naa paavai govind karor karor kalaa dikhaai-o.
s9.english_meaning: I speak truth, hear me all you saints: those who have loved — they alone have found God. Without love no one reaches Govind, though they display ten million skills and powers.

s9.title: Tav-Prasad Savaiye — Closing Dohra
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਜਿਨ ਪ੍ਰੇਮ ਕਿਓ ਤਿਨ ਹੀ ਪ੍ਰਭੁ ਪਾਇਓ
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: The two-line closing of the Savaiye: 'Those who have loved — they alone have found God.' A learner gains the most quoted Guru Gobind Singh dohra — a standalone philosophical statement used in speeches, identity discourse, and daily conversation as a summary of the Khalsa's inner ethic.
s9.citation_signal: Nitnem closing / Amrit Sanchar / widely quoted in Sikh discourse / Punjabi identity conversations
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਜਿਨ ਪ੍ਰੇਮ ਕਿਓ ਤਿਨ ਹੀ ਪ੍ਰਭੁ ਪਾਇਓ ॥
ਬਿਨੁ ਪ੍ਰੇਮ ਕੋਊ ਨਹਿ ਪਾਇਓ ॥
s9.romanization:
jin prem ki-o tin hee prabhu paai-o.
binu prem ko-oo neh paai-o.
s9.english_meaning: Those who have loved — they alone have found God. Without love, no one has found Him.

s9.title: Jaap Sahib — Opening Namastavam
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਨਮਸਤਵੰ ਅਕਾਲੇ ॥ ਨਮਸਤਵੰ ਕ੍ਰਿਪਾਲੇ
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: Salutation to the Timeless, salutation to the Gracious — the opening of Jaap Sahib establishes the warrior-saint's relationship with the Divine as one of both reverence and fearlessness. A learner gains Guru Gobind Singh's core theological vocabulary in its most rhythmic, memorizable form.
s9.citation_signal: Nitnem (daily prayer) / Amrit Sanchar / Gurudwara morning program / memorization in Sikh education
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਨਮਸਤਵੰ ਅਕਾਲੇ ॥
ਨਮਸਤਵੰ ਕ੍ਰਿਪਾਲੇ ॥
ਨਮਸਤਵੰ ਅਰੂਪੇ ॥
ਨਮਸਤਵੰ ਅਨੂਪੇ ॥
ਨਮਸਤਵੰ ਅਭੇਖੇ ॥
ਨਮਸਤਵੰ ਅਲੇਖੇ ॥
s9.romanization:
namastavam akaale.
namastavam kripaale.
namastavam aroope.
namastavam anoope.
namastavam abhekhe.
namastavam alekhe.
s9.english_meaning: Salutation to the Timeless. Salutation to the Gracious. Salutation to the Formless. Salutation to the Peerless. Salutation to the One beyond garb or form. Salutation to the One beyond reckoning.

s9.title: Jaap Sahib — Sword and Weapon Epithets
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਨਮੋ ਸਸਤ੍ਰ ਪਾਣੇ ॥ ਨਮੋ ਅਸਤ੍ਰ ਮਾਣੇ
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: God as the Sword-bearer (ਸਸਤ੍ਰ ਪਾਣੇ) and the one who takes pride in weapons (ਅਸਤ੍ਰ ਮਾਣੇ) — these epithets establish why the Khalsa carries the kirpan as a theological act. A learner gains the foundation for understanding Sikh martial theology: the weapon is not secular, it is divine.
s9.citation_signal: Nitnem / Khalsa initiation ceremony / Sikh martial theology education
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਨਮੋ ਸਸਤ੍ਰ ਪਾਣੇ ॥
ਨਮੋ ਅਸਤ੍ਰ ਮਾਣੇ ॥
ਨਮੋ ਪਰਮ ਗਿਆਤਾ ॥
ਨਮੋ ਲੋਕ ਮਾਤਾ ॥
s9.romanization:
namo sastr paane.
namo astr maane.
namo param giaataa.
namo lok maataa.
s9.english_meaning: Salutation to the Bearer of Weapons. Salutation to the One who takes pride in Arms. Salutation to the Supreme Knower. Salutation to the Mother of all worlds.

s9.title: Jaap Sahib — Names Beyond Religion
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਨਮੋ ਹਿੰਦੁਆਣੇ ॥ ਨਮੋ ਤੁਰਕਾਣੇ
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: God is saluted by Hindu names and Muslim names in the same breath — the Divine is not confined to any religion's exclusive claim. A learner gains the Sikh theological argument that all names for God point to one reality, expressed in Jaap Sahib's extraordinary multilingual catalogue.
s9.citation_signal: Nitnem / Sikh interfaith discourse / anti-sectarian education
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਨਮੋ ਹਿੰਦੁਆਣੇ ॥
ਨਮੋ ਤੁਰਕਾਣੇ ॥
ਨਮੋ ਪ੍ਰਭੁ ਸੁਆਮੀ ॥
ਨਮੋ ਅੰਤਰਜਾਮੀ ॥
s9.romanization:
namo hinduaane.
namo turkaane.
namo prabhu suaamee.
namo antarjaamee.
s9.english_meaning: Salutation to the God of the Hindus. Salutation to the God of the Turks (Muslims). Salutation to the Master Lord. Salutation to the All-Knowing Indweller.

s9.title: Jaap Sahib — All-Death, All-Sustainer
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਨਮੋ ਸਰਬ ਕਾਲੇ ॥ ਨਮੋ ਸਰਬ ਦਾਲੇ
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: God as the All-Death (ਸਰਬ ਕਾਲੇ) and the All-Sustainer (ਸਰਬ ਦਾਲੇ) — these warrior epithets make explicit that destruction in service of righteousness is a divine attribute. A learner encounters the Khalsa's theological rationale for righteous warfare and the vocabulary that sets Sikh martial theology apart.
s9.citation_signal: Nitnem / Sikh martial theology / Khalsa education
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਨਮੋ ਸਰਬ ਕਾਲੇ ॥
ਨਮੋ ਸਰਬ ਦਾਲੇ ॥
ਨਮੋ ਸਰਬ ਦੇਵੇ ॥
ਨਮੋ ਸਰਬ ਭੇਵੇ ॥
s9.romanization:
namo sarab kaale.
namo sarab daale.
namo sarab deve.
namo sarab bheve.
s9.english_meaning: Salutation to the All-Death. Salutation to the All-Sustainer. Salutation to the God of all gods. Salutation to the Knower of all secrets.

s9.title: Jaap Sahib — Closing Cluster
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਨਮੋ ਸਰਬ ਧੰਧੇ ॥ ਨਮੋ ਸਰਬ ਬੰਧੇ
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: The closing epithets of Jaap Sahib salute God as present in all activities and all bonds — the prayer ends not with retreat from the world but with an affirmation of engaged presence. A learner gains the completeness of Jaap Sahib's vision: beginning with Timelessness and ending with engagement in every dimension of life.
s9.citation_signal: Nitnem / Gurudwara morning program / Amrit Sanchar
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਨਮੋ ਸਰਬ ਧੰਧੇ ॥
ਨਮੋ ਸਰਬ ਬੰਧੇ ॥
ਨਮੋ ਅੰਤ ਕਾਲੇ ॥
ਨਮੋ ਆਦਿ ਅਕਾਲੇ ॥
s9.romanization:
namo sarab dhande.
namo sarab bandhe.
namo ant kaale.
namo aad akaale.
s9.english_meaning: Salutation to the One engaged in all affairs. Salutation to the Bond of all bonds. Salutation to the Death at the end. Salutation to the Timeless from the beginning.

s9.title: Bachitar Natak — Divine Mission Statement
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਹਮ ਇਹ ਕਾਜ ਜਗਤ ਮੋ ਆਏ
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: Guru Gobind Singh states his mission directly: we came into this world for this purpose — God sent us to spread dharma and to seize and punish the wicked. A learner gains the clearest articulation of why the Khalsa was formed and the vocabulary of divine commission — ਕਾਜ, ਧਰਮ, ਗੁਰਦੇਵ ਪਠਾਏ — that anchors Sikh martial identity.
s9.citation_signal: Sikh philosophical education / Khalsa founding narrative / widely cited in discourse on righteous action
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਹਮ ਇਹ ਕਾਜ ਜਗਤ ਮੋ ਆਏ ॥
ਧਰਮ ਹੇਤ ਗੁਰਦੇਵ ਪਠਾਏ ॥
ਜਹਾਂ ਤਹਾਂ ਤੁਮ ਧਰਮੁ ਬਿਥਾਰਹੁ ॥
ਦੁਸਟ ਦੋਖੀਅਨ ਪਕੜਿ ਪਚਾਰਹੁ ॥
s9.romanization:
ham ih kaaj jagat mo aaye.
dharam het gurdev pathaaye.
jahaan tahaan tum dharam bithaaro.
dust dokheean pakarh pachaaro.
s9.english_meaning: We came into this world for this purpose alone — God sent us to uphold dharma. Wherever you go, spread righteousness; seize and put down the wicked and the malicious.

s9.title: Bachitar Natak — Meditation at Hem Kunt
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਹੇਮ ਕੁੰਟ ਪਰਬਤ ਹੈ ਜਹਾਂ ਸਪਤ ਸ੍ਰਿੰਗ ਸੋਭਿਤ ਹੈ ਤਹਾਂ
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: Guru Gobind Singh describes his previous existence meditating at Hem Kunt Parvat — a peak surrounded by seven mountains — before being sent by God to the world. A learner gains the vocabulary and geography that led to the founding of Hemkund Sahib Gurudwara and the Sikh concept of the soul's passage across lives in service of dharma.
s9.citation_signal: Hemkund Sahib pilgrimage / Dasam Granth study / Sikh theology of past lives
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਹੇਮ ਕੁੰਟ ਪਰਬਤ ਹੈ ਜਹਾਂ ਸਪਤ ਸ੍ਰਿੰਗ ਸੋਭਿਤ ਹੈ ਤਹਾਂ ॥
ਸਪਤ ਸ੍ਰਿੰਗ ਮਿਲਿ ਤਾਲ ਬਣਾਇਆ ਤਹ ਅਸਮਾਦ ਸੁਹਾਵਣਾ ॥
ਤਹ ਹਮ ਅਧਿਕ ਤਪਸਿਆ ਸਾਧੀ ਮਹਾ ਕਾਲ ਕੈ ਧਿਆਨ ਲਗਾਇਆ ॥
ਤਬ ਹਮ ਤਿਨਿ ਪ੍ਰਭ ਜਗਿ ਪਠਾਏ ਤਬ ਹਮ ਜਨਮ ਲਏ ਇਸ ਠਾਏ ॥
s9.romanization:
hem kunt parbat hai jahaan sapat sring sobhit hai tahaan.
sapat sring mil taal banaaiaa tah asmad suhaavnaa.
tah ham adhik tapsiaa saadhee mahaa kaal kai dhiaan lagaaiaa.
tab ham tin prabh jag pathaaye tab ham janam la-e is thaaye.
s9.english_meaning: There is a mountain called Hem Kunt where seven peaks shine splendidly; the seven peaks together formed a beautiful lake there. There I performed great austerity and fixed my meditation on Mahakaal (the Great Death/Timeless One). Then God sent me into the world, and then I took birth in this place.

s9.title: Bachitar Natak — The Calling
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਤਬ ਹੁਕਮ ਸਾਹਿਬ ਕਾ ਭਇਓ ਇਹ ਜਗ ਮਹਿ ਆਉ
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: God commands: come into this world. This is the moment Guru Gobind Singh narrates his own calling — not birth but a divine dispatch into a specific historical mission. A learner gains the vocabulary and theological concept of Hukam-as-calling: spiritual authority is not claimed but received and enacted.
s9.citation_signal: Dasam Granth study / Bachitar Natak readings / Sikh prophetic theology
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਤਬ ਹੁਕਮ ਸਾਹਿਬ ਕਾ ਭਇਓ ਇਹ ਜਗ ਮਹਿ ਆਉ ॥
ਧਰਮ ਕੀ ਰਖਿਆ ਕਰਨ ਕਉ ਮੁਝੈ ਭੇਜਿਆ ਪ੍ਰਭਿ ਆਉ ॥
ਅਧਰਮੀ ਜੋ ਜਗਤ ਮੈ ਤਿਨ ਕੋ ਕਰੋ ਨਿਕਾਉ ॥
ਸੱਚ ਕਾ ਮਾਰਗ ਫੈਲਾਉਣਾ ਇਹੁ ਮੇਰਾ ਕਾਜ ਬਣਾਉ ॥
s9.romanization:
tab hukam saahib kaa bha-i-o ih jag meh aau.
dharam kee rakhiaa karan kau mujai bhejiaa prabh aau.
adharmee jo jagat mai tin ko karo nikau.
sach kaa maarag phailaaunaa ihu meraa kaaj banaau.
s9.english_meaning: Then the command of the Master came: come into this world. God sent me saying: come to protect dharma. Drive out the unrighteous ones in the world; spread the path of truth — make this your purpose.

s9.title: Bachitar Natak — Resolve Against Tyranny
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਜ਼ੁਲਮ ਜੁਲਾਹੀਂ ਦੇ ਮਾਰਣ ਕਉ ਪ੍ਰਭਿ ਪਠਾਇਆ ਧਾਇ
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: God sent me running to destroy the tyranny of the tyrants — this passage makes explicit the anti-oppression mandate of Guru Gobind Singh's mission. A learner gains the Punjabi vocabulary of righteous resistance: ਜ਼ੁਲਮ (tyranny), ਪਠਾਇਆ (sent), and the theological framing of political resistance as divine duty.
s9.citation_signal: Sikh human rights discourse / anti-oppression theology / Khalsa founding philosophy
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਜ਼ੁਲਮ ਜੁਲਾਹੀਂ ਦੇ ਮਾਰਣ ਕਉ ਪ੍ਰਭਿ ਪਠਾਇਆ ਧਾਇ ॥
ਧਰਮ ਦੀ ਪਾਲਣਾ ਕਰਨ ਲਈ ਮੈ ਜਗ ਮਹਿ ਆਇਆ ਤਾਇ ॥
ਨਿਰਭੈ ਹੋਇ ਜ਼ੁਲਮ ਦਾ ਟਾਕਰਾ ਕਰਾਂ ਇਹ ਮੇਰਾ ਕੰਮ ਭਾਇ ॥
ਸੱਚ ਦੀ ਜਿੱਤ ਜ਼ਰੂਰ ਹੋਵੇਗੀ ਪ੍ਰਭ ਦੀ ਕਿਰਪਾ ਨਾਲ ਸਦਾਇ ॥
s9.romanization:
zulam julaaheen de maaran kau prabh pathaaiaa dhaay.
dharam dee paalanaaa karan laee mai jag meh aaiaa taay.
nirbhai hoi zulam daa taakraa karaan ih meraa kamm bhaay.
sach dee jit zaroor hovegee prabh dee kirpaa naal sadaay.
s9.english_meaning: God sent me running to destroy the tyranny of the tyrants; I came into the world to uphold and protect dharma. My task and calling is to confront oppression fearlessly; with God's grace, the victory of truth is certain always.

s9.title: Akal Ustat — All Humanity Is One Race
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਮਾਨਸ ਕੀ ਜਾਤਿ ਸਬੈ ਏਕੈ ਪਹਿਚਾਨਬੋ
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: Recognize all of humanity as one single caste/race — this is the most quoted Guru Gobind Singh line on universal equality and it appears on Gurudwara walls globally. A learner gains the philosophical foundation of Sikh anti-caste thought and the one Punjabi-Braj phrase that has shaped Sikh social ethics for three centuries.
s9.citation_signal: Gurudwara wall inscriptions globally / anti-caste discourse / interfaith events / Sikh political speeches / school curricula
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਮਾਨਸ ਕੀ ਜਾਤਿ ਸਬੈ ਏਕੈ ਪਹਿਚਾਨਬੋ ॥
ਕਾਹੂ ਕੀ ਭੂਲਿ ਹੈ ਕਾਹੂ ਕੋ ਜਾਨਬੋ ॥
s9.romanization:
maanas kee jaat sabai ekai pehchaanbo.
kaahoo kee bhool hai kaahoo ko jaanbo.
s9.english_meaning: Recognize all of humanity as one single race. It is one person's error and another person's knowing — yet the human kind is one.

s9.title: Akal Ustat — The One in All Faiths
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਕੋਊ ਭਜੈ ਹਿੰਦੁ ਕੋਊ ਭਜੈ ਤੁਰਕ ਕੋਊ ਭਜੈ ਸਿੱਖ
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: Some call on God as Hindus, some as Muslims, some as Sikhs — but He is One who listens to all. A learner encounters the Sikh theological argument for the unity of religious paths in Guru Gobind Singh's own words, and gains the key vocabulary of Sikh pluralism.
s9.citation_signal: Interfaith education / Sikh pluralism discourse / Akal Ustat study
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਕੋਊ ਭਜੈ ਹਿੰਦੁ ਕੋਊ ਭਜੈ ਤੁਰਕ ਕੋਊ ਭਜੈ ਸਿੱਖ ॥
ਰਾਮ ਰਹੀਮ ਕੋ ਧਿਆਵਤ ਭਿੰਨ ਭਿੰਨ ਕਰਿ ਲਿੱਖ ॥
ਏਕਹਿ ਭਗਤ ਭਗਤ ਸਭ ਕੀਨੋ ਜੋਤਿ ਸੁ ਏਕ ਉਜਿਆਰੀ ॥
ਰਾਮ ਰਹੀਮ ਪੁਰਾਨ ਕੁਰਾਨ ਅਨੇਕ ਕਹੈਂ ਏਕ ਸ਼੍ਰਿਸ਼ਟੀ ਸਾਰੀ ॥
s9.romanization:
ko-oo bhajai hindu ko-oo bhajai turak ko-oo bhajai sikh.
raam raheem ko dhiaavat bhinn bhinn kar likh.
ekahi bhagat bhagat sabh keeno jot su ek ujiaari.
raam raheem puraan kuraan anek kahen ek shrishti saari.
s9.english_meaning: Some worship as Hindus, some as Muslims, some as Sikhs; they meditate on Ram and Rahim by different names and writings. Yet the One has made all into devotees; that Light is one and illuminating. Ram and Rahim, Puranas and Quran — many speak of them, yet the whole creation is One.

s9.title: Zafarnama — When All Means Are Exhausted
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਚੂ ਕਾਰ ਅਜ਼ ਹਮਾ ਹੀਲਤੇ ਦਰ ਗੁਜ਼ਸ਼ਤ ॥ ਹਲਾਲ ਅਸਤ ਬੁਰਦਨ ਬ ਸ਼ਮਸ਼ੀਰ ਦਸਤ
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: When all peaceful means have failed, it is righteous — halal — to raise the sword: written in Persian to Emperor Aurangzeb after Chamkaur, this couplet is the philosophical foundation of dharam yudh in Sikh thought. A learner gains the most famous Zafarnama line and the cultural concept that force is a last resort, not a first instinct.
s9.citation_signal: Sikh political theology / dharam yudh discourse / Zafarnama study / Sikh identity literature
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਚੂ ਕਾਰ ਅਜ਼ ਹਮਾ ਹੀਲਤੇ ਦਰ ਗੁਜ਼ਸ਼ਤ ॥
ਹਲਾਲ ਅਸਤ ਬੁਰਦਨ ਬ ਸ਼ਮਸ਼ੀਰ ਦਸਤ ॥
s9.romanization:
chu kaar az hamaa heelate dar guzasht.
halaaal ast burdan ba shamsheer dast.
s9.english_meaning: When all other means and stratagems have been exhausted and tried, it is righteous — permissible, halal — to raise the sword in hand. (Persian, written in Gurmukhi script in Dasam Granth editions; written to Aurangzeb after the Battle of Chamkaur, 1705.)

s9.title: Zafarnama — I Fear No One, God Is With Me
s9.first_line_gurmukhi: ਮਰਾ ਹੀਚ ਬਾਕ ਅਜ਼ ਕਸੇ ਨੀਸਤ ਕਾਸ ॥ ਕਿ ਦਰ ਪਿਸ਼ਤ ਮਨ ਦਸਤਗੀਰੇ ਖ਼ੁਦਾਸ
s9.author: Guru Gobind Singh
s9.why_dense: I fear no one, for God's hand is on my back — written to a Mughal emperor who had destroyed his family and defeated his army. A learner encounters fearlessness (ਨਿਰਭੈ) not as a claim but as a theological position: the warrior protected by divine will stands beyond the reach of human terror.
s9.citation_signal: Zafarnama study / Sikh courage discourse / ਨਿਰਭੈ philosophy / widely cited in Sikh talks
s9.full_excerpt_gurmukhi:
ਮਰਾ ਹੀਚ ਬਾਕ ਅਜ਼ ਕਸੇ ਨੀਸਤ ਕਾਸ ॥
ਕਿ ਦਰ ਪਿਸ਼ਤ ਮਨ ਦਸਤਗੀਰੇ ਖ਼ੁਦਾਸ ॥
s9.romanization:
maraa heech baak az kase neest kaas.
ki dar pisht man dastgeer-e khudaas.
s9.english_meaning: I have absolutely no fear of anyone at all, for behind my back stands God as my helper and support. (Persian, written in Gurmukhi script; the defiant declaration to Aurangzeb, composed after losing four sons and his entire army at Chamkaur.)
